name: Holiday
description:
I need a quiet adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I’m frightened and will need a secure garden to learn about being outside.

I am too scared to go out for walks right now and will need a careful introduction.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home.

Holiday originally came to us from a breeder. She was adopted and found a lovely home, but sadly it wasn't the right match for her and the resident dog wouldn't accept her, so she came back into our care. Holiday has now moved to a foster home where she is settling well and learning all about home life.

Holiday is a very scared girl who is overwhelmed and clearly hasn't had much socialisation before. She finds human interaction quite worrying at the moment and is very hand-shy, so she will need a patient and understanding home that can help her come out of her shell at her own pace. Holiday's initial instinct is to run away from you and she isn't keen on being picked up or handled. She finds this so frightening that if she feels pushed beyond her comfort zone, she may react by nipping out of fear. She did nip her previous adopter once when she became overwhelmed during handling.

Holiday will still be looking for a calm and quiet, adult-only home with no visiting children. She will need adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will allow her to settle slowly, with no pressure or expectations. She relies heavily on other dogs for comfort and reassurance and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing.

Since returning to us, Holiday has begun to make some really encouraging progress. At the rescue we have been working with her on very gentle touch, always entirely on her terms and she has been doing well with this. She has recently moved to a foster home where she is settling in and is now happy to approach her fosterers to take a treat and will sniff their hands. She is very curious when the other dogs in the home are getting gentle fusses and the next step will be getting her used to a gentle fuss in her foster home.

Minus her brief adoption, Holiday has never lived in a home before, but she seems to be enjoying life in her foster home, she likes to spend her evenings in the living room sitting on a rug or by the sofa and isn't phased by the TV. She has so far been clean in the house and loves to snuggle up with the other dogs in the kitchen overnight. She has been left home with other dogs for a few hours and coped very well.

Holiday doesn't yet walk on a lead and will find this quite overwhelming, but she has visited a secure field with her fosterers and absolutely loves spending time there exploring and watching the other dogs play. She has even done a little sprint around it!

Holiday is a very special girl who will need someone willing to go slowly and earn her trust, but for the right home, the rewards will be incredibly meaningful. There is always someone out there for every dog, and Holiday is patiently waiting for hers.

Holiday has been given the Gift of Life by Ellie Worron

Holiday has settled well into foster care. She is worried about being handled at the moment so we haven’t tried to stroke her or pick her up, we wanted to give her the chance to settle and feel safe first.

She will take treats from our hands, walk through doorways easily, even with us there, and she seeks us out if she doesn’t know where we are. This all indicates that it won’t be long until we can begin gentles strokes etc. When we are fussing our dogs she gets closer to watch and has touched my hand or sniffed my legs. Holiday is not as jumpy as I thought she would be, which is great, and we can walk quite close to her without her moving away.

When we are all settled in the evening, she comes into the sitting room to see where we all are and what we are doing. She isn’t phased by the TV and will often sit on a rug next to my chair.

We have a very small field which most new fosters are a bit scared of as they are not used to big spaces, Holiday loves spending time out there exploring and watching the other dogs play, we’ve even seen her do the odd little sprint!

She has been clean in the house and loves snuggling with the dogs at night in the kitchen, she is left overnight and we can go out for a couple of hours and she is fine as long as the dogs are with her too.

She has the potential to be the best dog and I don’t think it will be long before she is seeking attention and ready to try her harness again. She has very gentle eyes and you can see the sadness ebbing away a little more each day.

This week Holiday has been making lovely progress with lead work. We have been helping her get used to having a lead clipped onto her harness and trailing behind her, and she is becoming much more comfortable with this. Clipping the lead onto her harness is now far less of a concern for her, which is a big step forward, and when she is out in the yard she barely seems aware of it anymore.

She is also doing wonderfully with different people putting her harness on, which is a very significant achievement for Holiday given how sensitive she has been around handling. She is taking these steps in her stride and continues to make really encouraging progress.

Recently, I’ve been spending some dedicated time working with Holiday, and she’s making such lovely progress. She’s beginning to trust me more each day and has even allowed me to gently put a harness on her. Everything is done slowly and at her pace, with plenty of treats and reassurance, but this is a huge milestone for this sensitive girl.

We’ve also been working on gentle touch, always on her terms. When she chooses to come and sit beside me in her bed, we take things one small step at a time, building her confidence with each positive experience (and a few tasty rewards along the way!).

Holiday is starting to show more of her personality now. She’s often one of the first up for a treat and has even begun wagging her tail. It might sound like a small thing, but for Holiday, it’s truly something special.

She’s a wonderful dog who just needs a patient, understanding home where she can continue to grow in confidence and feel safe. Watching her come out of her shell is incredibly rewarding, and she’s so ready for someone to give her the love she deserves.

I am writing this update as someone who has not had much interaction with Holiday although I have worked with her on a few occasions. It is obvious from her previous update that there has been progress in her coming out of her shell and I was surprised to discover that Holiday would accept treats from my hand although when trying to smooth her coat, Holiday swiftly moved out of reach.

She is getting there but slowy and anyone wanting to adopt her will need to go slowly and earn that important trust. Holiday's regular carer can stroke Holiday when she is settled in a bed but only in one place. If a position is changed, Holiday will become scared and move.

It is apparent after speaking with Holiday's regular carer that for Holiday to make that important next step in her progress, it would be better for her to be in a home situation. Holiday is fantastic with other dogs and would definitely need to live with at least one other dog to give her that hope of progress and comfort. There is always someone out there for every dog and vice versa Holiday is waiting.

Holiday originally came to us from a breeder a few months ago. She was adopted and found a lovely home, but sadly it wasn't the right match for her and the resident dog wouldn't accept her, so she is now back in our care.

Holiday is a very scared girl who is very overwhelmed to find herself here and clearly hasn't had much socialisation before. She finds human interaction quite difficult at the moment and is very hand-shy, so she will need a patient and understanding home that can help her come out of her shell at her own pace. Holiday will run away from you when you enter her kennel and isn't keen on being picked up or handled. She finds this so scary, that if she feels like she is being pushed out of her comfort zone she can choose nip or bite, purely through fear. She did bite her previous adopter once when she was being handled as she became so worried.

For this reason, Holiday will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where there are no visiting children. She will need adopters who hav experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will let her settle in slowly at her own pace with no pressure or expectations.

Holiday finds comfort and reassurance from the company of her canine friends and so she will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing. This dog will need to allow her to snuggle up to them and will need to be happy for her to follow them around. A kind resident dog will be an important source of reassurance and companionship for her as she adjusts to her new life.

Although she spent some time in foster and was adopted for a short period, she had never lived in a home before this, so Holiday will still need to learn all about home life and new skills like house training. She will also need to learn to walk on a lead and harness and will need to practice wearing these around the home and garden, once her confidence with handling has grown a little more. She will then need to practice walking around the garden until she is confident enough to venture further.

Since arriving back with us, Holiday has started to make some more progress and will now take a treat from our hands as long as she has a doggy friend next to her for support. With time, love and patience she will start to leave her past behind her and will truly start to blossom. Holiday's journey won't be a short one, but it will be so worth it when you see her tail wag for the first time or when she first willingly accepts some fuss and attention. In the right home, she will thrive, so please don't keep her waiting too long.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Toby
description:
I need an experienced, adult only home with no visiting children.

I would prefer to be an only dog and no cats please!

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I can walk well on a lead but can be reactive to bikes.

I was born with some deformed toes, which have been amputated.

Toby is a handsome Cocker Spaniel who has been through a lot in his young life and is now looking for a special someone to help him write his next chapter. Originally coming to us from a breeder at just three months old, Toby was a very scared little puppy who found the world overwhelming. Although he found a loving home, he was sadly returned to our care after his adopters felt unable to manage some of his behaviours.

Toby is still a sensitive soul and will need a calm, adult-only home with no visiting children. His new family should have experience with worried or complex dogs and be confident in helping him feel safe. He will need a home without cats as he has met them at the rescue, and was too interested. While he is generally affectionate and loving, Toby has shown some resource guarding tendencies, such as grumbling when people pass by his bed or the sofa when he is resting there. He did once nip his adopter. This was an isolated incident, but it’s important that any new adopters are comfortable managing and working with these behaviours using gentle, positive methods. That said, since being back with us Toby has come out of his shell beautifully. He greets staff with a wagging tail, and absolutely loves attention, affection, and food, especially food! Those who work with him absolutely adore him and think he is the most special and wonderful boy!

Toby will be looking for a home where he can be the only dog. He did live with another dog previously and while they did get along, he did sometimes guard his adopter’s attention, so for this reason he is best suited to being homed as an only dog.

Out and about, Toby enjoys his walks and is lovely on and off the lead. He has good recall and mixes well with other dogs, although he can be reactive to bicycles, so should only be let off lead in safe, secure areas away from the public.

Toby was born with some deformed toes that were later amputated. He has always had a slightly unusual gait due to this, but our vets have noted he does also have a luxating patella and is on pain relief for this at the moment, but this may improve with gentle exercise. He also has a naturally short tail, which just adds to his unique charm.

Toby is a sweet, loving boy who truly wants to connect. He has already won the hearts of everyone here, and we’re all rooting for him to find a calm, understanding home where he can feel safe, build trust, and thrive.

If you have the time, patience, and experience to offer Toby the gentle guidance he needs, he will reward you with loyalty, love, and plenty of cuddles.

Tobywill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

This week, Toby’s kennel mum celebrated his 1 year anniversary by breaking him out of the rescue for a couple of hours of fun!!Toby travelled good as hold in the car, and happily climbed into the back of the car & had his seatbelt harness fitted with no issues at all.He travelled to Pupstars Paddock with his kennel mum & another staff member who was new to him. He was absolutely brilliant with that staff member who could only sing his praises!!Toby absolutely LOVED the secure field, his tail did not stop wagging, he followed his kennel mum around everywhere and was (very gently) taking treats of both staff members.Once Toby bonds with you, he is the most loyal boy in the world. His circle of trust is only getting bigger, with more and more staff members falling in love with him as time goes on. He may have a stricter criteria than most, but it just means he is just an incredibly special boy in need of a special home. He is such a darling and we wish more people would be able to see it!

Toby’s one year anniversary with us is slowly approaching, and it honestly breaks my heart every day knowing he’s still not curled up on a sofa in a home of his own.

Toby is literally such an amazing dog when he knows you, all he wants to do is spend time with you, whether that be sitting with you, or going out for a gentle stroll!

Recently, I’ve been working on Toby’s lead reactivity, and he’s been doing incredibly well. He’s making such good progress, and I’m so proud of how far he’s come!

I really hope this is the last update I have to write about toby, hopefully his next updates will be coming from his new pawrents, showing us him blossoming in a home and how truly special he really is!

I’ve had the absolute joy of working with Toby for a couple of months now, and he truly is one of my more sensitive, special boys!

Once Toby gets to know you, he turns into the happiest, most affectionate little soul you could ever meet! He absolutely loves zooming around our big play yard, or heading out for a calm, gentle stroll along the lanes taking everything in at his own pace, Toby can be reactive towards passing cars or other dogs, it is something we are currently working on with him!

With the weather getting nicer, I decided it was the perfect day for a little adventure down to the river! To get there, we walk through a huge open field, so I popped Toby on a long line to give him some extra freedom to explore.I don’t think I’ve ever seen a dog so happy he spent most of his time rolling around on his back in the grass with the biggest grin on his face!

When we reached the river, Toby was a little unsure at first. He sat at the edge, watching the water. But after a few minutes of building up his courage, he dipped his paws in and that was it, he was happily paddling back and forth, enjoying every second. It was such a special moment to watch.

I truly adore Toby with my whole heart, he’s one of those dogs who just stays with you, always on your mind.

He can still feel a bit unsure when meeting new people and might bark or back away at first, but give him a little time and patience, and he’ll become your most loyal, loving best friend.

I honestly can’t believe Toby is still waiting for his forever home. He is such a gentle, beautiful soul who deserves the absolute best life has to offer!

Toby is a sweet older gentleman who truly enjoys the quieter side of life. He loves nothing more than snuggling up for a nap and being close to his people.

Toby still has a playful spark and enjoys his daily walks as well as pottering around the yard. He would thrive in a calm, peaceful home and is best suited to a household with no children or cats, where he can relax and enjoy his golden years in comfort.

I love Toby. He is so loved by many of us here at the rescue. He's a confident boy once he knows you, but until he feels comfortable around you he can bark and sometimes be a bit standoffish.

Toby is a big cuddle bunny, he absolutely loves a fuss, and will happily accept butt scratches any time of the day.

He enjoys going out for walks but absolutely loves being off lead and running around the big play yard, when his butt wiggles with happiness and excitement it really doesn't stop, it's super adorable. He likes chasing a tennis ball but he won't bring it back, instead he rolls around with it usually in his mouth and will then lay with it and play with it, he's a comical boy.

Again, once he's comfortable with you he's very easy to handle, I can get a coat or a jumper on him with no problems, I can pick him up and I can brush him with no problems, he had a groom recently and he was no issue at all.

It's a shame he keeps getting overlooked, yes he might have some typical cocker issues such as guarding, but it hasn't been a huge issue since he's been here, I can go in and out of his kennel, pick things up etc and he's been fine. I've also been yarding him with other dogs to try and socialise him more, and although he can be selective of who he likes, once he's found a friend then he just wants to play. He is a beautiful boy inside and out, and in my opinion anyone would be lucky to have him as part of their family.

Also he is kennel clean so house training shouldn't take any time at all.

Please, don't let Toby spend another year in kennels, he truly deserves to find his forever home, as they all do. It's getting colder, it's been snowing, a nice warm, cosy house with a loving family is what he wants, I know his forever home is out there somewhere so please come and find him.

Toby is still doing incredibly well with us here and hasn’t shown any signs of guarding, while this has stopped with us here we still are unsure how he will react in a home environment.

It is an absolute pleasure working with Toby and he always brings a smile to your face throughout the day. Toby is a gentle, loving and happy boy and is desperate to have somebody he can give all his love to.

Toby has definitely come out of his shell since he's been here, he still barks when you first see him but it's a happy bark and his tail doesn't stop, before he would stay for scratches for a little bit, but know he doesn't want to leave you and wants all the attention. He is a very loving dog.

Toby originally came to us from a breeder when he was around 3 months old. He was a terrified puppy who was worried about everything and would even squeak in fear when being handled. He was quickly adopted and found a loving home, but he has sadly been returned to our care as he was displaying some behaviours that his adopters felt unable to cope with.

Toby was homed with another dog and while they got along well, he did show some signs of guarding his adopter from the other dog, for this reason we now think he would be most suited to being homed as an only dog.

Toby can also sometimes grumble if people walk past the sofa or his bed and he is on them, so he will be looking for an adult only home with no visiting children, with adopters who have experience with dogs with similar issues and will be able to work on this with him. He did nip his adopter once around 18 months ago, but that was the only time that resulted in more than a grumble.

Toby is still quite a scared boy in some aspects and certain things will make him extremely nervous, such as the hoover or sometimes even minor things like the sounds of a drawer closing, but with confident owners with experience of scared dogs we do believe he will progress further. He would be best suited to a calm and quiet home, where he can settle in at his own pace and follow a routine.

He enjoys going for walks and is very good both on and off lead. He has good recall and gets along well with other dogs outside of the home. He can be reactive to bikes, so his adopters would need to be mindful of this and keep him on lead in any areas where he may encounter bikes and only let him off lead in secure areas.

As he has lived in a home before, we believe he is house trained, but he may need some reminders.

Toby is a sweet boy at heart and in the right home and with the right adopters we think he will thrive. Since his arrival with us he has been on his best behaviour and already adores all of the staff that work with him and they adore him too. He absolutely loves his food and has a true zest for life which makes us smile each day, so we are so hopeful that his perfect home is out there and will find him soon.

Toby was born with some deformed toes, which have since been amputated but don't seem to bother him, though he does walk with a slightly abnormal gait. He also has a short tail.

Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Arctic
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a multi dog household and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I'm new to lead walking and need some practice!

I have lived in a home, but may still need to learn house training.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home!

Please meet Arctic! She is a very special girl looking for a very special home with patient, experienced adopters who will never give up on her and who will love her for exactly who she is.

Arctic first came to us from a breeder in 2025. When she arrived, she was an extremely frightened and shut-down girl who found people and handling very overwhelming. Since then, life has been a bit of a rollercoaster for this special girl. She was adopted twice but has sadly found herself back in our care both times. Her first adoption sadly wasn’t the right match due to her level of fear and as she could nip through fear when pushed out of her comfort zone. Her second return came after she accidentally escaped through the front door. In the panic of the situation, she bit her adopter when they tried to catch her, and it was decided that returning her to us was the best option.

Despite everything she has been through, Arctic has made good progress since she first arrived. She is still a very anxious girl and remains particularly worried about being touched or handled, but she will now allow handling when approach gently and calmly. She will need experienced adopters who understand that trust cannot be rushed and who will be happy to let her settle entirely at her own pace, without expecting too much from her too soon.

Arctic will need a very special, adult-only home with adopters who have experience of scared ex-breeding dogs. If she feels pushed beyond her comfort zone, she can choose to make the wrong decisions purely out of fear, so her new family will need to be patient, understanding and committed to helping her continue building her confidence over the long term. She may never be a lap dog, but she is a lovely girl who will show her affection in her own quiet ways, and watching her grow in confidence will be incredibly rewarding.

Arctic had never experienced life in a home before her adoption. Although she spent around six months in her most recent home and had begun learning about home life, including toilet training, she will likely find moving again quite daunting and may need a refresher while she settles into her new routine.

She is starting to learn all about walking on a lead and harness, but this is still very new to her and so she will be looking for a home with a secure garden that she can potter around in and enjoy the fresh air.

One thing Arctic has always loved is the company of other dogs. They are her greatest source of comfort and reassurance, and we feel she would thrive in a home with multiple dogs, as she relies on canine company so much. She can have a bit of a strong personality, so will need resident dogs who won’t mind her being a bit bossy from time to time.

Arctic is looking for a home that will love her for exactly who she is, rather than who they hope she might become. With experienced adopters, patient guidance and confident canine companions by her side, she will continue to make progress and enjoy the happy, secure future she so deserves.

When Arctic originally came to us, she had some fur loss, but this has grown back wonderfully. She also had an enlarged mammary gland, but our vets are not concerned about this at present, but it may require monitoring by her adopters and their vet. She is currently receiving treatment for some small patches of chronic dermatitis on her lower lip.

Arctic has been given the Gift of Life by Laura Woods

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

name: Nuzzle
description:
I would like a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I would like a home with a dog my size or larger. I'm fine with cats!

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I haven't tried walks just yet and will find this a bit overwhelming at the moment.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have a Grade 1-2 heart murmur and have had 2 teeth extracted.

Nuzzle is a truly gentle, special little girl who has already come so far on her journey. She originally came to us from a breeder and, after a short adoption, sadly found her way back to the rescue as it was not the right fit for her. While in the home, she did make progress, but she was fearful of the man in the household and reacted out of fear on two occasions, showing that she needs very understanding, experienced adopters. She is now in a foster home where one of her fosterers is male and has settled in beautifully, which shows great progress for her.

When Nuzzle first arrived with us, she was an extremely nervous and flighty soul, spending most of her time hiding at the back of her kennel, too scared to join in or even accept touch. The world felt overwhelming, being in the yard, meeting people, or simply being handled was terrifying for her, and she lived in a constant state of panic and flight.

Since then, the transformation in Nuzzle has been truly heart‑warming. With patience, kindness, routine, and consistency, she has slowly begun to blossom. She now approaches her carers for gentle fusses and, when she feels safe, will even ask for more. She enjoys being groomed and is learning to walk on a harness, making steady, positive progress every day.

She shared her kennel well with other dogs of a similar size and is now in a foster home where she is getting along well with the other dogs in the home. Nuzzle thrives when she has another kind, confident dog by her side and will need a resident dog, her size or larger, in her new home for emotional support and confidence-building. She can bond quite closely with her doggy friends and can get quite protective of them, so may prefer to be homed with just one other resident dog who can be her bestie. She is in foster with cats and is fine with them so could potentially live with a dog savvy cat.

Nuzzle is not a dog for busy or noisy environments and will need a calm, quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand ex-breeding dogs and are prepared to be patient, gentle, and consistent, allowing her to progress at her own pace.

Despite her fears, Nuzzle is a sweetheart who adores comfort, routine, and affection. She enjoys cosy beds, soft fuss, tasty treats, and the safety of familiar people and dogs. She is house trained and really enjoys the comfort of home life.

From a medical point of view, Nuzzle has a Grade 1–2 heart murmur which does not currently require treatment but should be monitored by a vet. She has had two teeth removed when she was spayed, and she also has a fatty lump on her side which currently requires no action.

Nuzzle has already achieved so much and continues to surprise everyone with her courage and gentle nature. She has incredible potential and, in the right home with the right support, she will continue to grow in confidence and trust. She is a quiet, affectionate companion who simply wants a safe, loving place where she can feel secure and cherished.

With the right family who will celebrate every small step and understand her past, Nuzzle will flourish beautifully and become a deeply rewarding companion.

Today was my first grooming day with my foster brother. As my foster brother was due to be groomed my new foster parents thought they would treat me to one too and I was so brave and good. Although I wasn't very keen on the dryer and I'm still not very keen on being picked up, I did so well. Now everyone says I look so beautiful and fluffy!

Little Nuzzle has been with us for week now and it brings me to one question, and that question is why has this little darling not got a forever home already?She really is a little star from day one she has been nothing but a pleasure to foster. Her toilet training is 100% (only one accident on the first day she came), she gets on well with all the other dogs here and also gets on well with our cats. She's had 4 other dogs visit and was fine with them also.She sleeps well throughout the night, eats well, loves treats and will take them out of your hand. She walks on a lead with encouragement in quiet areas. She will allow you to fuss her but can be a little hand shy when putting on her harness and lead so does need vocal encouragement.Nuzzle is looking for preferably an adult only home with at least one other fur baby to help her continue with living her best life. Potential adopters must also be aware of her grooming needs.

Nuzzle is desperately missing her home comforts and I think she would love nothing more than to be back in a warm, cosy house. The weather at the rescue has been SO bad over the past couple of months and even though her kennel is set up like a palace because she’s so good, it’s still not the same as a home. She ADORES being snuggled up in bed and it’s definitely her happy place. I can only imagine how grateful she’ll be once she’s back on a sofa. We can dream!!!

This sweet girl completely has my heart and is breaking it at the same time. I cannot emphasise enough how easy she is to look after at the rescue. She quite literally doesn’t put a paw out of place in any aspect. She keeps her kennel and herself sparkling clean, she runs to and from the yard and back to her kennel perfectly every time, she shares everything including food and toys with her friends, and she’s easy to bath and groom. From the perspective of a kennel mum, she’s pretty perfect.

Her best friend got adopted a couple of weeks ago and she has been a little quieter since, but she now has a new kennel friend that she cuddles with a lot and has taken a shine to. I would so love for her to find her forever brother or sister soon because it would make her so happy to have a constant soulmate in her life.

Nuzzle has been back at the rescue for just over a month now and I have to say it’s been an absolute pleasure to look after her. She was on my section the first time she was here over a year ago and it’s been so lovely to see the difference in her. She was a very, very frightened girl back then and extremely panicky all the time. She was basically living in flight mode and it was so hard to have to watch. When she arrived back last month I was SO surprised when she approached me to ask for a little fuss on the first day. That is definitely not the same dog I knew!!

For the first couple of weeks I left her to decompress and settle back into my routine, and thankfully she did this pretty quickly. Since then, we’ve been doing lots of harness practice and she’s doing SO well. She’s been so much of a good influence that she’s even helped her best friend Denise learn how to walk too - so now they go out together on little strolls up the road and back. She’s still not perfect and won’t be up for going for big hikes anytime soon, but she’s getting better every day.

Her favourite thing in the world is being snuggled up in the back of her kennel under a heat lamp. She has a huge donut bed which she shares with Denise and you’ll find her tucked in for the majority of the day. They often enjoy a dental stick each while in the donut bed too, and it’s so sweet to watch!!

Nuzzle has SO much potential and I am so certain that in the right home with adopters who know what they’re doing with ex-breeders, she will thrive. She’s come such a long way and I know she has more to give, so please give her a chance soon.

Nuzzle is a truly gentle, special little girl who has already come so far on her journey. She originally came to us from a breeder and was adopted for a short period of time but has unfortunately found her way back to us as it was not the right fit for her. While in the home, Nuzzle did progress in some ways but was fearful of the man in the home and acted out of fear on two separate occasions without warning.

When she first arrived with us from a breeder, she was an extremely nervous and flighty soul, spending most of her time in the back of her kennel, too scared to join in or even accept touch. The world felt overwhelming, and every new experience, being in the yard, meeting people, or even being touched, was terrifying.

But since, returning, oh how far she has come! With patience, kindness, and the comfort, Nuzzle has slowly begun to blossom. She now approaches her kennel mummy for gentle fusses and, when she’s ready, will even ask for more. She’s learning to walk on a harness, enjoys being groomed, and moves confidently to and from the yard as part of her daily routine. She shares her kennel with a few other dogs of a similar size, and she gets along beautifully with them, sharing food, curling up together in bed, and finding reassurance in their company.

Nuzzle thrives when she has another kind, confident dog by her side, and in the right home, she will continue to grow in confidence and trust. She still relies on her canine friends for comfort, so she will need a resident dog of a similar size or larger. She also needs a calm, quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand ex-breeding dogs and are prepared to be patient, gentle, and consistent as she navigates life at her own pace.

Despite her challenges, Nuzzle is a sweetheart who adores comfort and love. She has shown that she can be fully housetrained in the home, but may need a few reminders in her new environment. She enjoys cosy beds, soft fuss, and tasty treats, and has a bright, promising future if given the right support. She does have a Grade 1–2 heart murmur, which does not currently need treatment but should be monitored by a vet, and she had two teeth removed when she was spayed. She also has a fatty lump on her side which requires no action currently.

Nuzzle has already achieved so much and continues to surprise us with her courage and gentle nature. She is a quiet, affectionate companion who just wants a safe place to feel loved and secure. With the right family who will celebrate every little step she takes, Nuzzle will flourish beautifully.

name: Oletta
description:
I can live with older dog savvy teens, 13+ who will allow me time to settle in.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I would like a large, and secure garden that I can settle into.

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy one or two walks a day!

Please be patient with me, I may need to learn house training.

I have arrived overweight and will be spayed before adoption.

06-07-26 Oletta is a very fun lady who has come into our care from a breeder. She is an affectionate little girl with a lovely gentle nature. Oletta adores a tummy rub and is always on the lookout for a bit of fuss and attention. She is great with other dogs and enjoys having a run around with her friends before coming over for cuddles. She also loves carrying a toy around and is the cutest little lady!

Oletta gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws. She can live with teenagers, aged 13+ who allow her time to settle in, and give her plenty of belly rubs!

As Oletta has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training and the other basics of home life. Her new family will need to give her time to settle into her new routine and learn at her own pace.

Oletta can already walk on a lead, so once she has settled into her new home, she would enjoy one or two walks around the local area with her resident dog.

Oletta has arrived slightly overweight, so she will need a good diet and gentle exercise to help her reach and maintain a healthy weight. This will need to be continued in her new home.

Oletta is a happy, easy-going girl who takes life in her stride. She has so much love to give and will make a wonderful companion for a family looking for a fun, affectionate dog to share their lives with.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Millie
description:
I can live with older children, aged 16+, who understand I need time to settle in.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I will need an extra secure garden, at least 6ft+ where I will be safe!

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

I'm fully vaccinated and will be spayed before adoption.

06-07-26 Millie is a lovely lady who has come into our care from a breeder. She has been described as a big softy, and it's easy to see why. Millie can be a little apprehensive in the kennel environment, but once she feels comfortable, she really enjoys attention and a gentle fuss. Although she has come from a breeder, we have been told that she has never had any litters. However, she will still need adopters with experience to allow her time to settle in at her own pace.

Millie can be nervous when meeting strangers and will sometimes lie down and become submissive while she works out that she is safe. Taking things at her pace and letting her build trust will help her settle.

We have been told that Millie gets along well with all the dogs she meets, with a particular fondness for Poodle breeds! She is currently kennelled with dogs of a similar size and enjoys their company. Millie will need another kind, confident dog in her new home to help her settle and show her the ropes. We think she would be happiest in a home with just one other dog, as she can become overwhelmed in larger groups and tends to retreat into herself. She will need a secure garden of at least 6ft to help keep her safe as she settles in.

As Millie has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Her new family will need to be patient and give her time to settle into her new routine. She can live with older dog-savvy teens, aged 16+ who will allow her time to settle in at her own pace.

Millie is a gentle, affectionate girl who just needs a quiet home where she can feel safe. With a kind canine friend by her side and patient adopters, we hope she'll grow in confidence and enjoy everything home life has to offer.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Elana
description:
I can live with calm, older teens, aged 16+ who allow me time to settle in.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I'd like a secure garden of at least 6ft to exercise in.

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

I have lived in a home but I need to learn to walk on a lead.

I have been spayed and I’m ready for my new home!

06-07-26 Elana is a nervous lady who has come into our care from a breeder. Although she came from a breeder, she has never been used for breeding and has never had any litters. We have been told she was born at the breeder's with a naturally small tail, which is why she was never bred from.

Elana is a lovely girl who just needs people to be patient with her. She can be very wary when meeting new people, so it's important to let her take things at her own pace. Once she feels comfortable, she is happy to accept a gentle fuss and begins to relax.

She absolutely loves other dogs and really enjoys playing with them. Elana has spent her life living in a rural setting, mostly off lead, and has had very little experience of people outside of those she already knew. Because of this, strangers can be quite worrying for her.

Although she was not used to breed, she will need adopters who have experience with ex-breeders and understand the background she has come from. Elana will need another kind and confident dog in the home, and this dog will be a much-needed support system as she settles into family life and continues to build her confidence. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens, aged 16+ that will provide her with a calm home.

Elana has lived in a home before, so she will be familiar with the sights and sounds of a home environment. However, she may still need some help with house training and lead walking as she adjusts to her new home and routine. She will need an extra secure garden, of at least 6ft to help keep her safe.

Elana is a sweet girl who enjoys the company of other dogs and is looking for a patient family who will let her settle in at her own pace. With time, understanding, and a confident canine friend by her side, we hope she will continue to grow in confidence and enjoy all that her new life has to offer.

I can live with older dog-savvy children who understand I need time to settle in.

17-06-26 Pheonix is a sweet boy who has come to us from a breeder as part of an unsold litter. He is a very energetic boy who will need an active household that can keep up with him.

As he is just a puppy, he has never lived in a home before and will need to learn all about house training, lead walking, and the basics of home life once he has finished his vaccinations. He can be a little flighty around us humans and seems to have had very little experience with people so far, so he will need at least one resident dog in the home that he can look to for reassurance as he learns about trusting people and navigating the world as an adult dog. He can live with dog-savvy older children who are sensible and understand that he is still learning.

His adopters will need to be committed to taking him to socialisation and training classes to help him become a well-rounded adult dog and build his confidence. With time, patience, and guidance, we are sure he will come on leaps and bounds.

Pheonix is a lovely boy with lots of energy and potential, and he is sure to light up any loving home. Pheonix has arrived a little underweight and will need a healthy and heart diet (plenty of treats!) to ensure he is helped back to a healthy weight.

Pheonix has been given the Gift of Life by Carolina Olsen

description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I will need to be the only dog in my new home.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I am learning to walk on a lead and harness, but it's still very new for me.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I am spayed and ready to find my forever home.

Xenon has come to us from a breeder and is now looking for a loving forever home where she can finally begin the life she deserves. When she first arrived, she found everything incredibly overwhelming and was very anxious around people, especially when inside her kennel. Since then, Xenon has come such a long way and it has been wonderful to watch her confidence grow.

It is heartbreaking that Xenon is still waiting for her forever home while all of her siblings have been adopted, because she really is such a special girl. Once she knows and trusts you, Xenon is incredibly affectionate, loyal and loving. She enjoys being close to her favourite people and has formed lovely bonds with the team who care for her every day.

Xenon can still be nervous when meeting new people and will often shy away at first, but it doesn't take her long to realise they mean her no harm. With a little patience and kindness, her true personality quickly shines through. Every day she has been building her confidence by exploring new areas of the rescue, and the progress she has made has been incredible. What once felt far too frightening for her is now something she takes in her stride, showing just how much she is learning to trust the world around her.

Although Xenon is a worried girl, we feel she would be happiest as the only dog in her new home. She has been assessed with a variety of dogs and is happy to walk alongside them or share open spaces, but she does not enjoy dogs entering her personal space and can become uncomfortable if they get too close. We believe that, with continued positive experiences, she may become more confident around unfamiliar dogs in the future, but for now she will need to be the only pet in the home and cannot live with cats.

Xenon will be looking for a calm and quiet home where her adopters are around most of the time. She would benefit from experienced adopters who understand nervous rescue dogs and are happy to let her settle at her own pace. She could share her home with older, dog-savvy teenagers aged 16 and over who can respect her space and understand that she may need a little time to warm up.

As Xenon has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be patient and understanding while she continues learning about home life, including house training and all the everyday routines that come with being part of a family. She has been making lovely progress with her lead and harness training and is growing in confidence with every new experience.

Xenon really is one of the easiest and most loving girls you could hope to meet. All she needs is someone willing to look beyond those first few nervous moments and give her the chance she deserves. In return, they will gain a loyal, affectionate companion who will reward their patience a thousand times over.

Xenon has been given the Gift of Life by Lyn Guthrie

It breaks my heart that Xenon is still waiting for her forever home while all of her siblings have been adopted. Xenon is a super affectionate, loyal, and confident girl with the people she knows. She can be nervous around new people and will often shy away at first, but it really doesn't take her long to realise they're not so scary. Every day, I take her around the front of the rescue to help build her confidence. The first time she was so frightened she actually popped herself out of fear, but now she'll happily walk around there and is so much more settled. Watching her confidence grow has been amazing.

Xenon has been assessed with multiple dogs and is happy walking alongside them or sharing open spaces, but she doesn't like dogs in her personal space. Because of this, she needs to be the only dog in the home. That said, I believe that with time, patience, and continued positive experiences, Xenon could one day socialise more comfortably with other dogs. She's only ever really known life with her siblings, so meeting unfamiliar dogs is understandably overwhelming for her, and she's still learning how to navigate those interactions and unfortunately she can't live with cats. She really is one of the easiest, most loving girls you'll ever meet. All she needs is someone willing to look past those first few nervous moments and give her the chance she deserves

Xenon has come to us from a breeder and is now looking for a loving forever home where she can begin her new life. She is overwhelmed to find herself here and clearly hasn’t had much human interaction before, so she can be quite anxious about being handled at the moment. She is especially nervous when inside her kennel and will run away from you, however, once she is out of her kennel she begins to come out of her shell and will even snuggle up next to you in the play yard. She is already trying so hard to be brave and with time, patience and plenty of TLC, we think she will flourish in a home.

Although Xenon is a worried girl, we feel she would be best suited to being homed as an only dog as she isn't keen on sharing her space with other dogs. She will be looking for a calm and quiet home where her adopters are around most of the time. She would need adopters who have experience with scared rescue dogs and who will let her settle in at her own pace and we may consider homing her with older, dog savvy teenagers 16+.

As Xenon has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be patient and understanding while she learns all about home life and new skills such as house training. She is just starting to learn about walking on a lead and harness and will need continued support and practice as her confidence grows.

With time, love and kindness she will begin to leave her past behind her and realise just how wonderful her future can be.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. name: Swanny
description:
I could live with dog savvy teenagers who respect my space.

I can be an only or live with a larger male. I cannot live with cats.

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I do my best to keep my kennel clean, but may need help in a home!

Swanny is a truly lovely little lady who has come to us from a breeder and has been quietly stealing hearts ever since. With her unmistakable Beagle nose, Swanny loves to explore her surroundings and see what’s going on around her. While she can still have a slightly shy side, her confidence has blossomed beautifully during her time with us, and her sweet, gentle nature really shines through. She ha sbecome people orientated and rather vocal which is lovely to see.

Over the past few weeks, Swanny has made wonderful progress. She is now walking beautifully on the lead and thoroughly enjoys heading out for walks or having a run around our big yard, where she shows her playful and inquisitive side. She also does her best to keep her kennel clean during the day, which suggests that house training in a home environment should be very straightforward with a little routine and patience.

Swanny gets on well with larger male dogs, particularly those who are calm, respectful, and able to give her the space she needs. She has shown us that she can feel overwhelmed if other dogs are too boisterous or in her face, so thoughtful introductions would be important. Thanks to her growing confidence, we now feel that Swanny could also live as an only dog in the right, understanding home where she can continue to thrive at her own pace or she can live with a larger, calm male dog. She cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Swanny has never lived in a home before, she will need kind and patient adopters who can help her learn the routines of family life. She could share her home with dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate and understand that she will need a gentle, quiet introduction to the home and her own space to settle in. With gentle guidance, consistency, and love, she is sure to settle quickly and become a devoted and affectionate companion. An active family who enjoy getting out and about would be ideal, as Swanny loves having both her mind and body kept busy.

Swanny has cataracts in both eyes, which do not appear to bother her at all. She has had an umbilical hernia repaired at the time of her spay, along with two teeth extractions. She also has one fractured pre-molar, which our vets have advised leaving for now, though it may need monitoring in the future. Swanny also has a travel document in place.

Swanny is an absolute delight to care for here at the rescue, and we truly believe that whoever opens their heart and home to her will be gaining a very special little dog who has so much love to give.

How Swanny has not been adopted yet is a mystery to me .

Perhaps it's her demeanor when meeting new people? Swanny can be a little shy at first but soon makes a connection and there's nothing wrong with being cautious.

Swanny walks beautifully on a lead and loves to sniff and smell the grass.

As Swanny does have enough confidence, she can live as the only dog in the house or equally she would be happy living with a larger male dog.

I would say, to see the true Swanny, you need to meet her, walk her and give her a little time to show affection. It really is rewarding when it happens.

Beagles are really pretty dogs and Swanny is no exception, so if you would like a beautiful beagle in your life, Swanny is ready to be the one.

Swanny is the sweetest angel, and it truly is a privilege to care for her five days a week. She has the most gentle and loving nature.

Swanny can be a little nervous when meeting new people, which is completely understandable, but with a bit of patience and kindness, she soon begins to show her true self.

Once you’ve built that special bond with Swanny, she becomes the most loyal companion you could ever ask for. She loves to  be close to you and will happily follow you around like you’re shadow!

Like a true beagle, Swanny absolutely loves her walks. The moment she’s outside, her nose is straight to the ground, exploring every scent along her way. Watching her take in the world through her nose is such a lovely reminder of how much joy the simple things can bring.

Swanny really is the most perfect dog in her own unique way. With the right family who can give her patience, reassurance, and lots of love, she will truly flourish.

She has so much to offer and would make the most wonderful addition to a caring and understanding home.

Swanny is such a sweet girl who is blossoming in confidence every single day!

Swanny has made so much progress over the last few weeks, I now believe she can be an only dog in the right circumstances.

Swanny does get on with larger male dogs absolutely fine, but prefers dogs who aren’t in the her face and give her the space she needs.

During her time here so far swanny has had a few disagreements with the male dogs she has shared kennels with when they get to much in her space.

Swanny is now walking beautifully on lead, and enjoys spending time going out for walks, or running freely down our big yard!

Swanny also tries to keep her kennel clean during the days, so house training should be a breeze!

Swanny is honestly a delight to look after here at the rescue, whoever welcomes her into their family are going to be very lucky people!

Swanny is a fabulous little lady who has come to us from a breeder. She’s an inquisitive girl with the unmistakable nose of a Beagle, always eager to explore and see what’s going on around her. Swanny can be confident when she chooses, but she still has a shy side, and with the gentle encouragement of her kennel friends, she’s starting to show her sweet, sociable nature.

She will need a confident, larger male resident dog in her new home to help her settle, guide her, and teach her about life in a home. Once she’s comfortable, Swanny would absolutely love an active family who can take her on plenty of adventures and keep her mind and body engaged.

Swanny is an absolute gem and, as she has never lived in a home before, will need patient adopters to help her learn house training, walking on a lead, and all the routines of home life. With kindness, consistency, and love, we believe she will settle quickly and become a devoted, loving companion.

Swanny has come to us with cataracts in both eyes, that does not seem to bother her. She has had an umbilical hernia repaired at the time of her spay as well as 2 teeth extracted. She also has one fractured pre-molar which our vets have advised to leave, but this may need to be looked at in the future.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Tinkerbell
description:
I need an adult-only home with ex-breeder experience that can show me love.

I need a resident dog to help me feel safe but I haven’t met cats yet so I'm not sure

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

Please be patient with me, I'm nervous and learning everything from scratch.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home.

Tinkerbell has come to us from a breeder and has never lived in a home before, so she is understandably unsure and very sensitive to touch. She can go into panic mode if handled too quickly and may try to nip out of fear as she is simply terrified and trying to protect herself. The initial handling is what scares her most, so patience and slow, careful introductions are essential.

Since being with us, Tinkerbell has begun to settle into a routine and is starting to show very encouraging signs of progress. While she is still too worried to accept touch and may move away if approached, she is becoming more comfortable being around people she knows. She will now approach to sniff fingers, gently take treats, and has even started to offer little kisses, which is a lovely sign that she is beginning to build trust. She is also happy to relax nearby, often choosing to lie close by, which shows she is starting to feel a little safer in our presence.

Tinkerbell has made particularly good progress with being picked up. This used to be extremely stressful for her, but she is now becoming more tolerant of this with calm and gentle handling, which is a huge step forward for her.

She will still need a very experienced, adult-only home with people who have handled very scared or sensitive dogs before. Tinkerbell will also need a kind, confident resident dog to guide her and help her feel more secure. Her adopters will need to understand that she may never be a typical dog who seeks constant cuddles or fuss, but with patience, love, and gentle guidance, she can form a very special bond.

As she has never experienced home life, Tinkerbell will need continued support with house training and learning to walk on a lead. Starting with calm, gentle garden time will help her confidence as she gradually explores her new environment.

Tinkerbell was recently groomed and found the experience very stressful, so it will be important for her adopters to gently and patiently help her build positive associations through gradual desensitisation. She will need regular grooming to keep her comfortable, and her new family should be prepared to work with an experienced, understanding groomer or may need to look at having her groomed under sedation until she becomes more comfortable with this. She has been making regular trips to our grooming room to have a brush and the fur around her eyes trimmed and is getting better with this each time.

Tinkerbell is a very special girl who needs an equally special home willing to invest time, TLC, and understanding. With consistency, patience, and love, she has the potential to form a unique bond and thrive in a safe, nurturing environment.

This week at the rescue we’ve had a big heatwave which resulted in a lot of our dogs needing to be shaved, including my beautiful Tinkerbell as she started to struggle having quite a thick coat. She has the most beautiful fur and she keeps herself so clean and dry, but it had to come off for her comfort. She was taken into the vets to be sedated again, but only because the weather has been so warm.. it would have been too hot to put her through the stress of shaving her all off with no sedation. I have been taking her into the grooming room regularly to brush her and trim the hair around her eyes, and she does definitely get better and better each time, so this is a good sign!! The next time she’s due a shave, I will try again without taking her into the vets to see how she does.

Even though Tinks is still a nervous girl, especially around people she doesn’t know, she is still progressing slowly and steadily. She’s still very reliant on other dogs and tends to copy them a lot of the time, so this has been good for her learning to trust me. She’s watched so many other dogs come up to me for a fuss, take treats from me, and just be comfortable in general being around me, and she is following suit.. just at her own pace. And that’s okay!

She is SUCH a sweet girl who has clearly been through a lot, and it’s not her fault she has such a strong guard up. I absolutely adore working with her and I understand and accept her for who she is. Seeing her get excited over toys and treats, and greeting me with a waggy tail in the mornings really makes me smile, and it gives me hope for her future. She deserves adopters who will love and cherish her the way she always should have been, regardless of if the feelings are mutual.. but I do think she is capable of loving and trusting.. she just needs to find herself with the right people.

Over the last few weeks, our lovely Tinks has reached a huge milestone. After seeing how well she has been progressing with her handling, we felt it was time to take the next step — harness training.

For a dog who finds handling so worrying, we knew there was a strong chance she might not accept wearing a harness. But unless we try, we’ll never know.

Tinks’ other kennel mummy and I took her into the grooming room, where we carefully introduced and fitted her harness. As expected, she was a little unsure and understandably a bit overwhelmed, but she made us both incredibly proud with how she coped. The next step was returning her to her kennel to see how she would manage wearing it throughout the day. Many of our more worried dogs will freeze or refuse to move in this situation, but not our Tinks. She carried on with her day as normal, moving around comfortably with the harness on.

To some, this might not seem like a huge achievement, but for Tinkerbell, it is a massive step in the right direction. Over the coming weeks, we will continue to build on this by practicing daily, with the hope that we can eventually begin introducing a lead in the yard.

With a dog like Tinkerbell, progress takes time, patience, and understanding. Thankfully, she has all of that in abundance, and we will never give up on her.

Tinkerbell has settled into my routine at the rescue beautifully now, so I’m finally starting to see glimpses of the real her. Her guard is definitely still up, but it has dropped slightly and I can tell she’s a lot more comfortable being around me than she used to be. She still won’t allow me to touch her and will bolt away if I go towards her, but she’s approaching me to sniff my fingers, sometimes give me kisses, and taking treats from me every single day now. She also feels content enough to lay near me when I’m sat in the yard eating my lunch, often with her back towards me which I guess is a sign that she does trust me!

One thing she has improved massively with is being picked up. I mentioned in my last update I still had to use a thick blanket and she would still sometimes try to bite (just not as often), but that’s even better again now. This week I was able to pick her up with no blanket, which is HUGE for Tinks!!

Because of how much better she had gotten, I thought I would attempt a full groom - going into it with absolutely no expectations. She allowed me to shave her back, but that’s as far as we could get. She started to get more and more stressed and I really didn’t want to push her and ruin the work we’ve done, so I took her into the vets so she could be sedated to complete the groom. So, she’s now shaved (minus her INCREDIBLE eyelashes), and I think she looks absolutely beautiful. Now that we’ve started fresh with her coat, I’m going to start taking her into the grooming room just once a week or so, and gently brush her etc to get her more used to it all.

Tinkerbell has made slow and steady progress since she’s been on my section and I’m so proud of how brave she’s trying to be. I can tell she wants to come out of her shell a bit more and join in with the rest of the dogs, but she’s not there just quite yet. Something big did happen last week though!! During dinner time I was hand feeding one of her friends, and Tinks came over and started licking the food off my hand for the first time!!!! I was absolutely over the moon and so happy that she felt comfortable enough to do that. Since then she’s done it nearly every day and has also started to take treats from me too if they’re yummy enough. I know this probably won’t sound like a lot to most people, but for a dog like Tinberbell it’s huge. For me, taking food from my hand is usually the first step in earning trust with my scared dogs, so I think we’re heading in the right direction.

We have also made a small amount of progress with Tinkerbell’s fear of being picked up. She still doesn’t like it - at all - but she’s not panicking as much any more and definitely doesn’t try to bite me as much as she did before. I still have to use a thick blanket and be very slow and calm with her, but I think she is starting to slowly realise that I’m not going to hurt her. I trimmed her fringe again a few days ago as her hair seems to grow quite quickly, so I took this opportunity to stroke her head gently for a little while once I was done. She actually started to close her eyes after a minute or so and I think she may have been enjoying it, or at least I hope she was.I adore this little lady and I’m going to do everything I can to show her what love and kindness is. I just hope a special someone will come along and feel the same way about her soon.

Tinkerbell has recently been moved to my section, as she struggles a great deal with being touched and handled by us humans. At present, she goes into complete panic mode whenever I go to pick her up, and she will try to bite to protect herself. This doesn’t mean she’s a nasty dog at all; she’s literally just so scared she will do anything she can to stop it happening. Once I have her comfortably in my arms, she will actually settle really well and will allow me to do things such as give her a gentle fuss and trim the hair on her face. I think the initial picking up is what scares her the most so this is what we’ll work on slowly together.

Tinkerbell is going to need a very experienced home with people who have had terrified dogs before. She’s going to need a lot of work and TLC put into her, and also a lot of understanding. Her adopters will need to accept the fact that she may never be a normal dog, and she may never be a dog who wants fusses or cuddles, but love her anyway. She’s a very special girl, and she needs a home equally as special to help her and give her what she needs.

I’m going to work as closely as I can with her over the next couple of weeks, and hopefully I can earn her trust and prove to her that she doesn’t need to be afraid of me. I’ll keep you all updated!

Tinkerbell has come to us from a breeder and can be a little unsure at first. She is touch-sensitive and may need time to feel comfortable, but once she calms down, she begins to show her gentle, sweet side. Tinkerbell will need an adult-only home that can handle her sassy personality and provide the patience she needs to settle in at her own pace.

She will also need a kind and confident resident dog to guide her and take her under their wing as she adjusts. Tinkerbell is looking for someone to teach her about trust, companionship, and the joys of home life.

As she has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be gentle and understanding while she learns about house training and walking on a lead. Starting with a few gentle garden sniffs will be perfect for her confidence as she begins to explore her new world.

Tinkerbell is a little gem who, with time and love, will grow into a devoted and affectionate companion.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

name: Lassie
description:
I need an adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I cannot live with cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I can walk well on a lead and would love an active home.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home.

Lassie has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived she was a very nervous girl as she had not had much experience with people, so everything felt very new and overwhelming to her. She could be quite skittish when approached and was worried about meeting new people and being handled. Since then her confidence has started to really grow. Once she gets to know you, she is the most loyal, loving and affectionate girl and it's so wonderful to see.

Lassie will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would benefit from adopters who have breed experience and experience with worried dogs.

She will be looking for a home where there was at least one other kind and playful resident dog to be her friend and guide. She gets on brilliantly with all dogs here out and about and socialises well with a variety of breeds and sizes. However, we have found that she is most comfortable sharing her home with other Collies and will therefore need a home with at least one resident Collie. She cannot be homed with cats or small furries. She doesn't like to share her food with other dogs, so her adopters would need to ensure she was fed separately and would need to separate when feeding any long last treats or chews too.

Lassie now walks well on a lead and would love to find an active home where she can go on lots of walks and adventures with her new family and where her clever collie brain can be kept both mentally and physically stimulated. She is only used to walking in quiet areas, so would need gentle introduction to any busier places. Lassie has never lived in a home before so her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she settles into the home and learns all about home life and new skills like house training.

With time, patience, and lots of love, Lassie will blossom. Once she has settled and gained confidence, she will become a cracking addition to her new family and will enjoy all the adventures life has to offer.

Lassie will need more than one meet and greet with her prospective adopters to allow her time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Beautiful beautiful Lassie! What a collie she is, she is so bright and has really come on so much with her basic training. Now knowing "sit" and "lie down". When we're out walking and a car is approaching, all I have to do is ask her to the verge and she sits and waits beautifully until we're able to start walking again. She really doesn't seem to be fazed by much traffic, but is very fixated with sheep if she comes across any. She passes horses lovely and will happily walk past other dogs no issue, but isn't keen on them approaching her. She's a big fan of food which helps with her training.She just needs people that understand that the girl she is when they first meet her is very worried but once she trusts you, her true personality really shines through.

Lassie is such a pretty girl who would do anything for you when she trusts you but is still wary of people initially. She did take treats off of a new person yesterday which was amazing to see. She needs to be in a collie experienced home with someone who will allow her the time to trust them at her own pace. She does love having a playfriend and she seems to be happiest when amongst other collies and collie crosses. She doesn't like sharing her food but is happy to share toys, beds and treats.

Lassie absolutely loves going out for her walks and gets very excited when she knows it’s time to head out. She really enjoys having a good sniff along the way and is always very interested in watching what’s going on in the fields as she passes by. Once she gets to know you, she is incredibly loving and affectionate, although she can take a little bit of time to warm up to new people. Lassie would still need a home with a resident dog, and she would truly thrive in a collie-experienced home where her needs and personality can be well understood.

Lassie is such a beautiful girl and once she knows you, she is the most affectionate, loyal and loving companion. She now walks well on a lead and absolutely loves her walks.

She will need a home with at least one other kind and confident resident dog who is a similar size to her and who loves to have a good play sometimes.

She doesn't like to share her food bowl, so he will need to be fed separately and this is something that her adopters will need to manage. In the right home she'll be someone's absolute best friend.

Lassie is a worried little lady who has come to us from a breeder. She has not had much experience with people, so everything feels very new and overwhelming to her. She can be quite skittish when approached, so she will need a calm, experienced home where she can be given the time and patience she needs to learn to trust.

At the moment, Lassie is scared of being touched, but she has already started to build a bond with her kennel mum and now allows gentle strokes from her. This shows that with consistency and kindness, shecanlearn to trust, and once she feels safe, she will begin to show her sweet side.

Lassie does walk on a lead, though she finds new experiences worrying, so she will need gentle introductions to the big wide world. She will also need to learn about house training and all the basics of living in a home, as these are things she has never known before.

Because Lassie is such a sensitive soul, she will need an adult-only home with no visiting children, and adopters who are happy to take things slowly at her pace. She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home who can help her feel secure and show her the ropes. She gets on brilliantly with all dogs here and socialises well with a variety of breeds and sizes. She cannot be homed with cats or small furries.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.
