name: Royal
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I can be an only dog or live with a female around my size. No cats please!

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I can walk on a lead and harness and love to explore quiet areas.

I am clean in my kennel but may need a few reminders as I settle!

I'm fully vaccinated and have been neutered.

Royal is a very special Chow Chow who has come to us from a breeder in search of his forever home. When he first arrived, he was extremely frightened of the world around him and was very wary of being touched or handled. Thanks to gentle care and patience, Royal has made wonderful progress and is beginning to show his sweet, trusting side.

Each day, Royal grows in confidence. He now greets his kennel mum with a happy tail wag and has recently taken a huge step forward and is now walking beautifully on lead! He also loves food, toys, and spending time outside, which all help him to relax and enjoy life a little more each day.

He has recently had a bath and full groom, and was an absolute star. His kennel mummy has made it a point to ensure he gets comfortable with brushing his fur, as he is a chow and he has been such a gentlemen during the process!

He has made such wonderful progress that we would now consider homing him as an only dog in the right circumstances. If he was to be homed as an only dog his adopters would need to be around most of the time and have experience with nervous rescue dogs and Chow Chow experience would also be beneficial. He could also be homed with a kind confident female resident dog around his size who could be his friend and help his confidence grow further. He cannot be homed with cats.

He has never lived in a home before, so his adopters will need to be kind and understanding as he settles in and learns all about home life as well as new skills like house training.

Although Royal has blossomed, he is still a sensitive soul and it can take time for him to warm up to new people. For this reason, he will need a calm, quiet, adult-only home with adopters who have experience with nervous dogs. His new family will find it incredibly rewarding to watch him grow into the playful, loving companion we know he can be.

Royal is a very special boy who, with time and understanding, has the potential to become a big, playful, and loving companion. Watching him learn to trust and enjoy life will be an incredibly rewarding experience for his forever family.

Royal is not a dog I’ve had the chance to work with until recently. I’ve heard a lot about him, as he’s one of our longer stayers. I had heard many great things from other members of staff, including the incredible progress he’s made since joining us!When I did get the chance to work with him, my first impression was “oh my days, you are beautiful!” On first sight, he is one of the most beautiful dogs I’ve ever seen and certainly the most stunning chow I’ve ever seen! How can you look at this handsome boy and not fall in love?!When I actually began working with him, I realised how shy and timid he actually was. Initially, he wouldn’t come to the front of the kennel to see me, he’d hide away in the back. It was evident to me that this boy did not trust me. As I continued to work with him, I was very aware of my actions, to ensure I wasn’t causing any anxiety. I began with treats (who doesn’t love a cheeky bribe?). It slowly worked. Initially, he wanted nothing to do with me. I didn’t want to invade his safe spaces (safe spaces are so important for a more scared or anxious dog. Could you imagine a big scary stranger coming into your home and not leaving? I wouldn’t feel too easy if that occurred to me). So, I’d place the treat and leave the kennel. He’d come out, have a little nosey and take the treat. With time, he began slowly coming out of the back as I entered his kennel. He then began taking treats from my hands and now, whenever I walk onto the section, he comes straight to the gate and waits for me. Honestly, such an incredible feeling making a bond like this with such an incredible boy! There is honestly no feeling like it. They say a dog is a humans best friend but, what no one tells you it works both ways. To build that bond, teach him that I’m not scary, I’m here to help him feel safe, comfortable, happy and to get such a response… it’s honestly the best feeling in the world. Royal needs a home, he needs people to show him this on a day to day basis. We do what we can but, we can’t teach him the safety and comfort of a home, of a family that’s there for him 24/7.Once I got to know this guy a little more, I was taken away. We go out on walks. I feel like this was another bonding experience for us. He loves a walk but, loud noises and interactions with loud people/dogs do freak him out, thus we try to go for quieter walks. He also loves frolicking in the yard. From my experience, he seems to just love being outside!When I worked with him, he didn’t have a friend in his kennel, however, since working with him I have seen him enjoying in the yard with other dogs and is now kennelled with a boxer. He seems very happy. As previously mentioned on his profile, he is to be homed with females around his size or alone. I have not worked with him enough to comment further on his compatibility and amiability with other dogs so, please read the rest of his profile and updates to get a fuller understanding of this incredible boy!At this point, I’ve worked with him enough for him to trust me. He’s happy to see me, he’s fine for me to touch him and fuss him. The Royal I see is a happy dude. He is a scared dog, I’m very aware of that but, the Royal I know now is far different to the Royal I first met. You just have to gain his trust and he’ll let you see him. When you first meet him, he will be shy. He’ll hide and he will not trust you. DO NOT let this deter you. He is one of the sweetest dogs I’ve ever met. From when I first met him to now, I feel like he’s a completely different dog. I was respectful of his boundaries, slow and calm, I did what I could to make him feel comfortable and safe, he then began to trust me and show me his true self (he hides it beneath all that fur). Honestly, I love him. I will take time out of my day to go see him, give him a little treat and some love, just because he is honestly that special. I cannot believe he’s been here over a year. He is one of our most special dogs. I don’t see how anyone can get to know this dog and not love him.Don’t overlook this fluffy dude. Yes, he’s shy and nervous but, the longer you spend with him, care for him and gain his trust, the more you’ll fall in love with him. I can say with confidence; if you put the effort in with Royal, you will fall in love with him.

I absolutely love this boy with my whole heart.

I've been working hard on helping him get used to being brushed because, as a Chow, it's something his future adopters will need to keep up with regularly to keep his coat healthy and prevent mats from developing, especially behind his ears.

Royal is now an absolute angel when it comes to grooming. I brush him every day, and with it being shedding season, no matter how long I brush for, there always seems to be more fur coming out! These days, he simply finds a comfy spot, settles down, and lets me get on with it. If he's had enough, or if I come across a mat that's uncomfortable for him, he communicates so well to let me know he's done for the day.

I also recently decided it was time to give him a bath. I wasn't sure how he'd cope, as it involved picking him up and putting him in the bath, but he was an absolute angel from start to finish. He allowed me to pick him up without any fuss and stood beautifully while I bathed and shampooed him.

I'm so incredibly proud of him. Afterwards, I introduced him to the grooming blower to help remove some of his loose coat. He wasn't a huge fan of it, but he tolerated it for a short while, and I'll continue using it regularly so he can build his confidence with it.

I honestly think all this grooming time has strengthened our bond even more, because lately he seems to be seeking out so much more affection and attention from me. He's such a special boy, and it has been wonderful watching his confidence grow.

Royal has now been with us for over a year, and we’re still waiting for the right person to see just how special he is. He’s had two meet and greets during this time, but sadly neither worked out as he was too nervous meeting new families.

Royal is a sensitive boy who simply needs time, patience, and understanding. He can be unsure at first, but once he begins to trust you, his cheeky, affectionate personality really shines through and it’s such a joy to see.

With a consistent routine, Royal truly thrives. He now enjoys running freely around our large yard without a harness or lead, and when it’s time to go back, he comes straight over. He’s also been out on two longer walks away from the rescue with one of our regular volunteers and has done amazingly well.

All Royal is looking for is someone willing to give him the time and patience he deserves. In return, he’ll become a loyal and loving companion.

I’ve come on leaps and bounds over the past month. I’m still a little shy and can get nervous when I meet new people, but I’m working on it!

I feel most comfortable when I spend quality time with people. It doesn’t take long for me to start feeling at ease (though I still sometimes like my own space). The more time you spend with me, the more comfortable I become—and treats help too! I’ll return your love in equal measure, as long as I’m given the right time, support, and respect.

I love going out for walks, though I can get nervous around loud noises or sudden movements. They might make me jump or feel uneasy, but a bit of love and reassurance always helps!

I feel more comfortable every day. I benefit not only from interaction with my humans, but also from the company of my kennel friends that I live with.

Another month has gone by, and my cheeky boy Royal is still waiting in kennels. I honestly don’t know why this handsome lad isn’t getting much interest. Not only is he completely unique-looking, but he also has the funniest, quirkiest personality. If you want a dog who’ll make you laugh every single day here he is!

Royal is 99% kennel clean and always keeps his bedding tidy… well, until I make his bed perfectly for him. Then he likes to “show me how it’s supposed to be done” by pulling it all across the floor. He walks beautifully on the lead now; he can still get a little spooked from time to time, but he’s made massive progress since the day he arrived.

He loves his toys he just doesn’t love sharing them! He’s also a bit selective with kennel mates, so his ideal home would include a calm, placid large female dog who won’t make too much fuss over him.

Royal truly is an amazing boy, inside and out, and I hope he doesn’t have to wait much longer for the forever home he deserves.

Royal never fails to make us smile with his crazy, funny, and quirky personality. He’s got such a cheeky side to him just waiting for the right people to help it shine.

On the lead, Royal is already walking beautifully, though he can still get a little nervous at times. His new family will need to be patient and willing to help him build confidence. Loud noises or sudden movements can overwhelm him, so he’ll do best with calm, quiet walks to start.

This week Royal had his very first trip down to the big yard! He loved it, though he found his confidence by sticking close to his doggy friend. With more practice, we’re sure he’ll begin to explore on his own.

Royal absolutely adores other dogs. Once he’s settled in, he loves to play, so he’ll need a kind and confident resident dog to show him the ropes, give him reassurance, and of course, enjoy plenty of playtime together.

He’s such a handsome boy with so much love to give we can’t believe he hasn’t found his forever home yet!

This past month has been a huge step forward for our boy Royal! Once a little unsure of the world, Royal is now walking beautifully on lead, it’s such a massive step for him considering how nervous he once was!

Royal currently shares his kennel with both a male and a female dog and he’s proving to be a respectful and easy companion. He enjoys their company, often watching what they do and following their lead. Royal will definitely need another resident dog around his size in his home, so he can continue to have the doggy companionship to help him continue to grow in confidence.

Despite his progress, Royal is still a sensitive soul, and it takes a little time for him to warm up to you. Royals forever family will need to give him time and patience to come out of his shell, so for this reason I believe Royal would be best suited to a home with no children.

Please don’t leave Royal waiting much longer for his forever home, he really deserves a cosy sofa to curl up on!

I’m so proud of Royal, this boy has come on tremendously since arriving at Many Tears. From arriving a terrified dog and very unsure of us humans, he has now slowly but surely begun to trust us and realise we are not so bad, especially when we arrive with cheesy treats.

Royal loves food, toys and being outside in our yards. He has just began harness training, ready to begin lead walking. At the moment he is not so keen on lead walking but he has taken a few tentative steps and with the help of some squeezy cheese and possibly a confident doggie friend to follow I think it won’t be long before he can begin to enjoy exploring the world.

Royals adopters will need to understand he is still very wary and unsure of his environment and he is going to need plenty of time, love and patience to trust his humans, but in the right home and given time I think this boy is going to be a big, playful and lovable boy. Royal will need a home where there is already a resident dog.

Royal is a very scared boy who has come to us from a breeder. He is especially scared when it comes to being touched by us humans but when you get him in the back of the kennel he will allow you to gently stroke him but he is uncomfortable with this. At the moment Royal could possibly make the wrong decision if handled incorrectly but this is purely through fear and he is already starting to make improvements. He will actually get excited to see his kennel mum in the mornings and will wag his tail but he’s not sure how to interact with her yet.

Royal has never lived in a home so this is going to be completely overwhelming at first and he will definitely need at least one other dog in the home to help his confidence. This will help him with house training and lead walking but these are things that are going to take him time to learn as the garden may be as scary as the home at first.

Royal will need a very calm and quiet, adult only home and adopters who have lots of experience with scared dogs. We think he’ll come out of his shell a lot more over time but Royal needs a lot of TLC, time and patience.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Tulip
description:
I'd like a quiet home, but I can live with older, dog-savvy teens!

I need either a male resident dog or a larger dog in my new home.

I'd like a secure garden to potter around in.

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

I am spayed and ready to find my forever home.

Tulip is a worried girl who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a sensitive little soul who initially found people quite overwhelming and would run away when approached. Since being in our care, Tulip has come on in leaps and bounds. She now happily allows her kennel mummies to pick her up and has grown much more confident with being gently handled. She is also making fantastic progress with her harness training and now calmly lets her harness be put on. While she still takes great comfort from the company of her kennel friends, it has been wonderful to watch her confidence continue to blossom.

Tulip is still a worried girl who can be unsure in new situations, so she will need patient adopters who are willing to let her progress at her own pace without placing any pressure on her. Although she has made incredible progress with us, she will need time to build trust in her new home, and her adopters should be prepared for her confidence to take a little step back while she settles in. She will be best suited to adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens, aged 16+, who are sensible and will allow her the time she needs to settle at her own pace. A calm, predictable environment will give her the best opportunity to continue building her confidence and feel safe.

Tulip relies on other dogs for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws. She would be best suited to living with either a male dog or a female dog that is larger than her, as she can sometimes struggle with smaller female dogs.

As Tulip has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. However, she has already shown us how capable she is of learning, and her recent progress with handling and harness training gives us every confidence that, with patience and positive guidance, she will continue to flourish.

Tulip may still find the world a little scary at times, but she is a lovely little girl with a gentle nature who is growing in confidence every day. We know that, with time, patience, and the support of a canine companion, Tulip will continue to blossom and discover just how wonderful life can be.

Tulip has come on in leaps and bounds with her handling. She now happily allows her kennel mummies to pick her up and has grown much more confident with being gently handled. She's also making great progress with her harness training and now calmly lets her harness be put on. Tulip is gaining confidence every day, and it's wonderful to see her blossoming.

Tulip is a worried girl who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start.

She is a worried little soul who currently finds people quite overwhelming and will run away when approached. Tulip much prefers the company of her kennel friends and takes great comfort from being around them. She is an active and spritely girl with a heart of gold, but at the moment she lacks confidence around humans and is still learning that people can be kind.

Tulip is difficult to handle right now because she is very scared and unsure of our intentions. This is completely understandable given her limited experience with human interaction, and she will need patient adopters who are willing to let her progress at her own pace without placing any pressure on her. She will be best suited to adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens, aged 16+ that are sensible and will allow her time to settle in at her own pace. A calm, predictable environment will give her the best opportunity to build her confidence and feel safe.

Tulip relies on other dogs for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws. She would be best suited to living with either a male dog or a female dog that was larger than her as she can sometimes not get along with smaller female dogs.

As Tulip has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Her adopters will need to be understanding and committed to helping her navigate these new experiences gently and positively.

Tulip may be finding the world a little scary right now, but she is a lovely little girl with a gentle nature. We hope that, with time, patience, and the support of a canine companion, she will continue to gain confidence and discover that life has so many wonderful things to offer.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Flash
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home with ex-breeding experience to understand me

I would like to be the only animal in my new home.

I would like a good sized, secure garden to explore.

I can walk on a lead but find this very overwhelming at the moment.

I have entropion in both eyes, which have been repaired by the vets.

Flash is a truly striking Standard Poodle with a big personality, a sensitive soul, and an oversized puppy spirit. He first came to us after his previous owners could no longer give him the time and exercise he needed. Since then, we’ve watched him grow from a worried boy into a much more confident and affectionate companion, and he continues to make us proud every single day.

Flash is still very much a character, goofy, playful, and full of energy but beneath his bouncy exterior lies a gentle heart that just wants to love and be loved. He can be sensitive in new situations and still finds the world a little overwhelming at times, but with people he trusts, his sweet and loyal nature shines through. Once Flash bonds with you, he is yours for life.

Recently, he’s been showing us more of his loving side. On rainy days when we huddle together out of the downpour, he rolls over for belly rubs, brings his toys for attention, and happily drifts off to sleep during a cuddle. It’s in these quiet moments that his true personality comes out, affectionate, trusting, and eager to please.

Flash’s training is going from strength to strength. He’s mastered “touch” (booping his nose to your hand) and is now learning “place.” He still gets a little mouthy when he’s overexcited, but he’s come a long way since his arrival and continues to improve with patience and consistency. He’ll need his future family to have ex-breeding experience and to continue this gentle guidance in the home so he can keep building on all the progress he’s made.

Being an active breed, Flash loves his walks and enjoys exploring the world at his own pace. He’s happiest in calm, quiet areas where he can relax and take everything in without feeling rushed. He will need to be the only pet in the home, as he prefers human companionship over other animals, and he’s not a fan of pigeons or seagulls either!

Flash has had successful surgery for entropion in both eyes, and though he still needs a little care for mild dermatitis around them. He is currently being treated for sore and sensitive ears.

What Flash truly needs now is a calm, understanding, adult-only home with ex-breeding experience and with someone who’s around most of the time, someone who can offer him patience, structure, and all the love he deserves. With time and trust, Flash will repay his person a hundred times over with loyalty, affection, and that special spark that makes him so unforgettable.

Please don’t overlook this beautiful boy. Flash has come so far and is ready to start the next chapter of his life he’s just waiting for that one person to believe in him and bring him home.

Flash will need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Flash has officially been waiting for a home for a whole year. After a first few months of (a lot of) patience and training, it's then blossomed into the most incredible remaining months where we’ve really seen Flash’s true heart and soul. This dog is phenomenal in more ways than one, and it’s so sad he’s now become part of the MT furniture, and that the rescue has now become his home.

So for Flash’s 1 year anniversary, I took him off site to the nearby country park for a lovely stroll, followed by a visit to McD’s for some chicken nuggets.

Flash was suuuuuuch a good boy on his walk. He took in all the new smells, and took the new environment in his stride. He was even approached by 3 off lead small dogs, and didn’t react at all. He trotted along the park, with the world at his feet and it was the most amazing thing to see.

He is an absolute darling in the car, so we made a swift visit to McD’s for a box of 20 nuggets to eat together in our on site flat.

It was so nice to see Flash in a home like environment, he jumped straight on the sofa, and wandered around inspecting every room. We sat on the floor together eating our nuggets, and he eventually rested on the raised bed and had a little snooze.

It was heartbreaking putting him back in his kennel, when he seemed so at peace and settled in the flat. I just wish there was someone out there who could see him for everything he is and more!!!

Our beautiful, bonkers Flash has learnt a new trick and we thought we would share.

Flash has also been enjoying the sunshine with lots of walks and plays in the yards this week.

Flash continues to work hard on growing his calmness around new people and he is doing ok. He has now been 10 months here at the rescue and has an increasing fan base amongst the staff but he of course longs for a home and people of his own.

When I first started working with him, I was apprehensive. He was a much larger dog than I was used to working with, and he would mouth you til his hearts content. Not because he is a nasty dog, or means any harm whatsoever, but simply because he has come from a home (just out of puppyhood) and he has become extremely anxious and unsettled being in a kennel environment. On top of that, he has been taught 0 manners which definitely didn’t help with the matter!!! I struggled to bond with him, I struggled to understand why he wouldn’t listen when being told, and why all he wanted to do was treat my arm like a toy…

However I found myself thinking, what has Flash been through in his life that was ever HIS fault? He didn’t ask to spend so much of his life in kennels. He never asked to be put in situations where his anxiety gets the better of him, it was never his decision to not be trained to behave normally. So I decided to buck up my ideas and realise he’s not as scary as he seems, and he’s probably just as anxious being around me as I was him. And from that moment on, our relationship has blossomed…

This boy is truly a superstar in every sense of the word. He is SUCH a goofball, and can have you in fits of laughter within seconds of being with him. His personality is infectious, and as soon as he is comfortable with you and realises you are no threat to him, he shines like the star he is. It’s gutting that Flash doesn’t allow every staff member to see all sides of him. The side that shows his softness, his sweetness, his kindness. But that just makes me so much more grateful that he feels comfortable enough around me to do so.

You can see from his pictures, Flash is muzzle trained. Not because he is an aggressive dog, not because he is a violent dog, it’s simply due to his anxiousness. On one hand, Flash is a super intelligent Poodle but on the other hand, he doesn’t really show it. His anxiety gets the better of him, and he puts himself in uncomfortable positions with new people & then panics whilst he’s in it. It is SUPER important that whoever adopts Flash’s main priority is keeping him safe, and keeping him from making decisions that he feels uncertain about. He wants to make friends with everyone, but also decides halfway through some interactions that he’s not to keen on the thought too!

Among (of course) all of the other precious angels I look after the rescue, Flash is one of the reasons I get excited to go to work. You shouldn’t really have favourites, but Flash does take the cake. I cherish every single moment I get with him. Our lovely strolls up the country lanes, our snuggles in the back of his kennel after a long day, our zoomies down our big play yard. Every single moment & memory I look forward to recreating every single day. Nothing tops being around this dog, nothing else matters when you’re with Flash.

If I was in a position to take him home & work with him through his quirks, I absolutely would, in heartbeat in fact. There is just something so special about him, and I truly envy whoever is fortunate to adopt this incredible young man. And for as long as Flash remains in kennels, I will advocate for him, make him feel safe, comfortable and most of all loved.

Flash has completely changed my mind about Poodles. I am absolutely in love with him. He might not be the sharpest boy in the bunch, but he more than makes up for it with his goofy charm and endless silliness.We are still working on his mouthing when he becomes overexcited. Progress is steady, and every small step forward feels like a real achievement. Flash loves his freedom. We take daily trips to the big yard where he runs around like the happiest whirlwind you have ever seen. He would really benefit from visits to private dog fields where he can safely zoom and explore to his heart’s content.Meeting new people can still be a little tricky for him, but we have a careful plan in place to help him do so safely and comfortably. For those of us who know Flash well, he shows a side many would not expect. Beneath his anxiousness and the need for a muzzle at times is the sweetest, softest boy who adores snuggles and head tickles.He loves to stay close, gently resting his head in your lap until he drifts off to sleep. At the end of each day, we share a quiet moment together, curled up side by side until his eyes close and he is peacefully dreaming.Flash truly is a special boy who deserves a very special home. He needs someone who can offer love, patience, and understanding. In return, he will give unwavering loyalty and companionship for life.

Flash is an absolute character, this boy is full of life and once he knows you he adores you but his introductions to new people always need to be slow as this sensitive boy can be easily overwhelmed.

Flash works well within a routine, he has an abundance of energy and a super inquisitive nature which can sometimes lead to him becoming quite lively, he will often revert to ‘mouthing’ and jumping up when he is uncomfortable or unsure.

Flash has a super inquisitive nature and loves to observe what’s going on at a distance. This boy can also be an absolute cuddle monster and adores a snuggle with his favourite people, and will fall asleep next to us when we have the opportunity to sit quietly with him. One of flash’s unique traits is his love of a pamper session! I have never known a dog who enjoys a bath and hairdryer the way he does and he becomes quite docile under the hair dryer whilst having a gentle brush.

Flash’s ideal home would be a quiet an experienced dog owning home (adults only) with humans that have the patience and time to help grow his confidence and who also understand the need to grow calmness in him. Flash has had a few dog friends at the rescue but his high spirted nature can be a bit much for some dogs and he does not seem to like male dogs, for this reason a home where he is the only pooch would suit him best.

Flash walks well on lead but can become very excited when he sees other dogs, walks in quiet areas would be perfect for him, he loves to be outdoors and often just enjoys sitting in the yard with me watching birds, but he will equally enjoy time spent just hanging out with his humans at home. Flash really is a wonderfully, lovable, giant puppy, looking for kind and understanding humans to provide him with the security and love he deserves.

Flash has had a brilliant few weeks. He celebrated his second Birthday and was treated with his first snow day, which he loved frolicking about in. Unfortunately, he did have to spend his birthday in kennels, but I made sure it was still a very special day for him. We hopped in the car, and I took him for a little walk before heading to the pet shop to pick out some birthday treats. He chose a dinosaur toy and a knuckle bone, which he thoroughly enjoyed. I also bought him some special birthday biscuits to keep him going through the week.

After that, I truly spoiled him, and we decided to go to Burger King for a burger! Flash was quite confused at first, but once he had a taste, he couldn’t get enough.

Flash truly is a special boy and will need a special home to match. He can be very wary of strangers and doesn’t always show clear signs if he’s uncomfortable. For this reason, he is muzzled when out on walks, which I make sure to do whenever we go out.

Flash will need an adult-only home with no visiting children. He cannot live with cats or any small animals

The stunning Flash had a little pamper today, which he absolutely loved. He was as good as gold whilst in the bath and sat very contentedly on the grooming table looking out the window enjoying the warmth of the blowers. I have never known a dog to enjoy bath time as much as he does, he becomes quite sleepy and cuddley, it’s the cutest thing.

After his wash and blow dry he got to open some Christmas treats out in the yard sent in from the lovely folks out there that support Many Tears, Flash particular enjoyed the food presents.

Flash really is the most wonderful boy once you get to know him, and we see improvement every day in his sometimes lively and skittish nature. This fantastic boy will make the most awesome and loyal of friend with the person or couple that are prepared to put the time and love into him he so deserves.

Today, Flash and I went on a little adventure to the local pet shop, finishing off with a much-needed pupcup! He was an absolute angel in the car—he hopped right in, buckled up, and sat calmly for the whole journey. I watched him in the mirror as he gazed out at the world passing by. When we arrived, we popped his muzzle on as a precaution since he can sometimes be wary of strangers. Flash wandered around the shop, taking in all the sights and smells, until he discovered the pick-and-mix treats—which of course we had to get. After choosing his goodies, he rummaged through the plushies and picked out a squeaky monkey to take home.

As we headed toward the till, another dog suddenly rushed around a corner and startled Flash, causing him to react. I managed to whisk him away with a distraction, but he became very on edge and tucked himself into me for comfort. A lovely lady then came around the opposite corner and, despite having seen his reaction, kindly stopped to say hello. She happened to be a previous Many Tears adopter, and her own pup is doing wonderfully. She chose to ignore Flash at first to give him space, and once he settled, he allowed her to gently stroke his head. After wishing us well, we carried on with our day.

We sat outside Costa in the sunshine, and Flash absolutely loved his pupcup, so much so that he managed to get it all over his nose.

I’m incredibly proud of Flash today. It was his first outing since arriving in kennels, and I could really see how scared he can be underneath it all, and how much he relies on his human for reassurance. Flash needs someone devoted, patient, and willing to put time into training and working through his reactivity toward other dogs. He truly is a special boy.

Another bleak day at the rescue with the rain pouring down non-stop, but it meant I got to spend some more quality time with the dogsI’ve never been one for poodles but Flash really has stolen my heartToday I really did see his loving and caring side as we cuddled away from the downpour. He rolled around, brought me his toys and chews before receiving belly rubs and head scratches until he fell asleep

If you’ve been to the rescue I’m usually walking Flash around the reception area in order for him to build his relationship with humans and other dogs but it was nice to just have some one on one time with him today, once you’ve created a bond with this boy he is yours for life.Flash’s’ training is still continuing and has managed to learn ‘touch’ where he boops his nose to the designated hand and is in the midst of learning ‘place’.

In a home will still need some training in order for him to thrive.

Flash is still a little mouthy when he gets super excited but is doing much better to when he first arrived with us.

It's been almost 4 months since Flash arrived here and I feel he has been very much overlooked. Deep down he’s a big cuddle bug and thrives on attention.

I tried spending most of my time with him today as kennel life is very stressful for him. We visited the big yard multiple times, went for a stroll and even met 2 dogs on a walk and greeted them with such kindness. Might I say he walks beautifully alongside you!

We met some new people in the reception area, which can sometimes make Flash a little uneasy but he did fantastically and even accepted some treats. For this he is muzzle trained as he can be mouthy when he’s a little too excited!

The last task of the day was to be groomed. I never thought I would see this boy sit still for more than a few seconds, but he amazed me and hopped up onto the grooming table ready to be pampered! And he now looks so good! Flash really doesn’t mind being groomed which will be helpful for the future.

I absolutely adore this beautiful boy. Flash is best described as an oversized puppy at heart. He has a playful, goofy nature but sometimes forgets his size, which can make him a little overexcited and mouthy, especially if he’s unsure or feeling frustrated.

Flash can be a sensitive soul who is often scared of his own shadow and needs plenty of reassurance and gentle confidence-building when faced with new experiences (like the umbrella he discovered today!). Because of this, kennel life is very tough for him, and I truly believe he will thrive so much more in a loving home.

Once Flash trusts you, he shows his affectionate side. He adores snuggling in for a cuddle and loves being pampered.

Flash’s ideal home would be an adult-only household where he can be the only dog. He isn’t aggressive with other dogs but can become boisterous and overwhelming. A quiet location with a secure, good-sized garden would be perfect, ideally with adopters who enjoy long walks and hikes.

He walks beautifully on the lead, enjoys all weathers, and has the stamina to go for hours. While he may sometimes react to new situations or get excitable around other dogs, his confidence and manners are improving every day. Flash will need adopters who are home most of the time or able to include him in their daily lives, as he finds being left alone difficult and may struggle with separation anxiety.

Flash is a truly striking Standard Poodle who has come to us from a home as his owners no longer had time for him and couldn't offer him the exercise he requires. Although he has lived in a home before, Flash is a worried boy who finds new experiences and people quite daunting. Because of this, Flash is looking for an experienced, adult only home with adopters who have plenty of time, patience, and love to give. He needs adopters who won’t give up on him and who will allow him to settle in at his own pace.Initially, Flash can be wary of new people and unsure of their intentions, but once you’ve gained his trust, he begins to show his affectionate and loyal nature. He forms strong bonds with those he feels safe around and shows a deep need for companionship. He does not cope well with being left alone and has shown signs of separation anxiety, getting very worried and anxious when left on his own. He will need an adopter who is able to be with him all of the time and who will work on this with him, slowly building up any time he is left. We are told he was crate trained in his previous home, which may help with this process.Flash is particularly sensitive to loud or sudden noises, which can cause him to panic. He feels safest in quiet, familiar surroundings and finds walks outside of the rescue grounds very difficult at the moment. When taken beyond his comfort zone, he becomes overwhelmed and spins in circles in an attempt to return to safety. While he can walk on a lead, his adopters will need to help him grow in confidence very gradually and may need to begin with simply enjoying time in the garden or short walks in peaceful areas.Flash would be best suited to being homed as an only dog as he can be grumbly when meeting other dogs. This is something his adopters will need to work on with him with patience and positive reinforcement. Because of this, he must only be allowed off lead in secure areas with no other dogs present. He also cannot live with cats or small animals and we are told he isn't a fan of pigeons or seagulls.Flash will be looking for a calm and quiet home, where he can settle in at his own pace and where his adopters will help him settle into a consistent routine so he can feel safe and secure. We are told that he is house trained but he may need some reminders of this in his new homeAlthough he is currently worried about going for walks, he is an active breed who will need to be kept both mentally and physically stimulated. We have been told that he has previously done some agility and scent work, so once his confidence grows this is something he may like to explore again in the future. His previous owners also said he enjoyed being groomed and bathed, though we haven’t yet introduced this at the rescue.This beautiful boy has so much potential in the right hands. If you have the time, experience, and understanding to give Flash the security and stability he needs, he will reward you with trust, loyalty, and love. Please don’t overlook him, he’s waiting for that one special person to believe in him.Flash has an entropion in both eyes, which has been repaired by our vets but does have some dermatitis around his eyes.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Reet
description:
I would like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’d enjoy one or two quiet walks a day and plenty of cuddle time.

I am living in a foster home and doing very well with toilet training

I have been spayed and ready for my new home

Reet has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived she was a little worried and quite overwhelmed, but soon started to settle in. Although wary at first, it was clear there was a sweet and curious little Beagle waiting to emerge.

During her time at the rescue, Reet gradually began to grow in confidence and show more of her true personality. She proved to be a bright, inquisitive girl with a larger-than-life personality and a real enthusiasm for life. She enjoyed meeting other dogs, exploring new areas, and learning about the world around her. With encouragement (and the promise of a tasty treat!), she even tackled challenges she was initially unsure of. Her confidence on the lead also improved greatly, and she soon began to enjoy her walks and adventures around the rescue.

Reet has now moved into a foster home, where she continues to make lovely progress. She has settled in well and is showing herself to be a busy, active little dog who loves to investigate everything around her. One thing that has remained consistent throughout her journey is her love of food, and she is more than happy to accept treats from anyone willing to share them! Toilet training is going very well, and she has been enjoying regular walks outside too.

The main area where Reet still needs support is learning to trust human touch. While she is becoming increasingly confident in many aspects of daily life, being handled still worries her and she will often move away from hands. She has only recently arrived in foster and is being allowed to settle at her own pace, with plenty of patience and understanding, but the next step is to introduce her to more bonding activities and getting her more used to being handled.

Reet will be looking for a calm and understanding home with patient adopters who are prepared to continue building her confidence and helping her discover how wonderful her new life can be. She will need another kind and confident resident dog in her new home to reassure her and take her under their wing.

Helping a dog like Reet is what makes fostering (and adopting!) so rewarding. So if you like beagles, and have the patience, understanding, and commitment to help a sensitive dog learn how gentle human hands can be, then please apply for this wonderful sweet girl!

Reet has been given the Gift of Life by Patti Tucker

Reet had a busy weekend! We went to our local beagle meet in an enclosed field. There were 15 beagles and their humans too, and Reet made me so proud!

She came up and said hello to everyone, took treats and even had some cuddles from humans. She explored the field, but kept an eye on me and came over to check in every now and then. Such a brave girl.

Then back home and look who's applying for a bbq supervisor position! My husband is delighted that Reet doesn't seem to have that fear of men that is so typical of many ex-breeders. I'm sure it gets her extra treats from him, clever girl!

She is getting more confident at home too and now lets me stroke her. She still has a way to go, but it's very early days, and I'm sure she will become a happy cheeky beagle very soon.

Reet is settling well into her foster home. She is a busy, inquisitive little dog who absolutely adores her food and will take treats from anyone! Toilet training is going very well, and she has been joining us for walks outside too!

In fact, the only stumbling block for Reet is human touch. It still scares her, and she will move away from our hands as quickly as possible. She has only been with us for a week though and I let her settle in with no pressure.

Next week we have some bonding activities planned, extra walks if the weather allows, some touch games - I will keep you posted on Reet's progress!

Helping a dog like Reet is what makes fostering - and adopting! - so rewarding. So if you like beagles, and have the patience, understanding, and commitment to help a sensitive dog learn how gentle human hands can be, then please apply for this wonderful sweet girl!

Reet has been my project dog for the past couple of weeks, during this time my main objective was to encourage this happy curious girl to blossom. She's still got a little way to go, but I'm so proud for how far she's come.

When I first started working with Reet her sweet, curious nature was already there, that's just who she is. Since then she's been meeting new dogs and people and building up the courage to explore new areas of her life at the rescue.

As you can see in the videos she's got a larger than life personality that seems to brighten my day. She's now walking perfectly on the lead with confidence in her stride. She's expressing herself openly around people and dogs in the yard. I also got her to build the courage to climb an obstacle she was wary of, with the help of food of course, she is a beagle after all.

Reet has continued her harness training today. She took some encouragement around a large group of people, but bravely continued despite her fears.She absolutely loves the freedom, and space of the big yard with her friends. And she certainty won't say no to a treat.

Reet has made wonderful progress this week. She is doing really well with her harness training and even ventured down to the big play yard for the very first time today, which she absolutely loved. She gets along great with the other dogs and happily shares her food, beds and toys. Her tail never seems to stop wagging!

Reet is a happy girl who has come to us from a breeder and would love to find an active home! She was a little wary at first, but she is really coming out of her shell as she continues to settle in and is showing herself to be a very curious girl. That being said, she is initially nervous when we approach so you do have to go slow with her and allow her time to warm up to you.Reet will be looking for a calm and quiet home, where there is another kind and confident resident dog in the home that she can look to as she continues to build her confidence. As she has never lived in a home before, she will need patient and understanding adopters while she learns about house training and walking on a lead.Reet isn't yet used to being handled and can be a little worried about this, but she is getting more used to this every day and with time and TLC, we think she will truly blossom.Once she has mastered her lead training, Reek would love plenty of adventures and will make a wonderful companion after she has had time to settle in and adjust to home life.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.
