name: Toast
description:
I would like a quiet, adult-only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me feel safe. I live with a cat in my foster home.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in (6ft+)

I'm harness training currently but need to build my confidence around lead walking.

I will need to learn about house training and lead walking

Toast is a delicate little girl who came to us from a breeder and has been slowly finding her confidence in the world. When she first arrived, she was very nervous and overwhelmed by everything around her, often running to hide in her bed.

Since arriving in foster, it has become clear that Toast is an extremely nervous girl and remains very unsure of new situations. She does not enjoy handling and can panic when she feels pressured, so she will need careful, gentle management and time to feel safe.

Toast is currently living with a cat in her foster home and can live with a dog-savvy cat who respects her space.

She will need a quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand nervous ex-breeding dogs and can support her without expectation or pressure. A calm, predictable environment will be essential for her ongoing confidence building.

She has come on in some areas, but handling still needs to be approached very carefully. While she has previously shown some progress with being picked up and having a harness fitted, this must always be done with patience and awareness of her limits, as she can become overwhelmed.

Toast is currently working on her harness training, and does allow her foster mummy to put one on her now, but she is not comfortable on a lead just yet and will need continued support and encouragement to build confidence. She is still learning about house training, and seems to be clean in the home when her foster mummy is around but can have a few accidents when she is left alone. With time and patience, she will begin to pick this up and understand the rules of house training.

She need a kind, confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and provide reassurance as she adjusts to home life.

Toast arrived with a low-grade luxating patella on her left side, which may improve with gentle exercise. She has also been treated for mucky ears, right-sided otitis, hot spots, and dermatitis, all of which are improving with care. Her general health will need to continue to be monitored in her new home, with any future treatment provided as needed.

Behind her shy beginnings is a sensitive little dog who is still learning to trust the world around her. With patience, understanding, and the right experienced home, Toast will continue to take small steps forward at her own pace.

Toast has arrived in Foster and continues to be a very frightened little girl and can panic easily if anyone tries to pick her up, so she really doesn’t cope well with handling at this stage. Once she is settled in her bed, she is a little more comfortable and will allow gentle strokes and will accept quiet reassurance and talking.

She is extremely nervous overall and will need a quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand how to support a dog like her at her own pace, without pressure. She is also currently living with a cat in her foster home.

I can't believe toast has been here so long that she was due another haircut with me!She has come on tremendously for handling and as long as you're gentle she is content with being picked up, as well as having a harness put on. She is on the journey of learning to walk on a lead and it shouldn't be long before she achieves it.

I've worked with Toast for a while now and I have been able to see how much she's grown over time, especially when it comes to being groomed. The bath is still not her favourite place to be, but she tolerates it, and I think she quite enjoys the warm air of the dryer. She ends up with such a cute haircut because she is so well behaved when it comes to the styling.

This small dog has such a big and beautiful personality and I believe in the right home with another confidence dog she will flourish tremendously.

Toast was such a nervous girl when she came in and she has come on really far since then. I had the pleasure of giving her a much needed groom and although she was nervous she took to it like a champ. She will need grooming upkeep in her forever home to look after her evergrowing locks and I think with a few more pampers she will feel so much more comfortable with it.

She's currently having harness training so she can be more comfortable with the sensation and has gotten much better at being picked up and handled although she can still be a bit jumpy at times but once she's in your arms she's quite calm. She still has a ways to go but I'm so proud of her progress so far and can't wait for when she flourishes with her forever family.

Toast is a delicate little girl who has come to us from a breeder to start her journey towards a brighter future. Right now, the world feels like a very big and scary place to her, and her first instinct is to run and hide in her bed. However, once in your arms, she is easy to handle and accepts gentle reassurance with quiet trust.

Toast will need a calm and understanding home with patient adopters who can give her all the time she needs to find her feet. A kind and confident resident dog will be essential to help guide her, show her that the world can be safe, and give her the courage to explore new experiences.

As she has never lived inside a home before, everything will be new to her, from walking on a lead to learning about house training and family life. Gentle encouragement and plenty of love will help her begin to understand how wonderful it feels to be part of a home.

Toast arrived with a low-grade (Grade 1) luxating patella on the left side, which may improve with gentle exercise. She is also being treated for mucky ears and right-sided otitis as well as dermatitis.

Toast may be shy now, but behind those worried eyes is a sweet girl who just needs the right people to help her shine. With kindness, patience, and a little guidance, she will blossom into a loving and loyal companion.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Jerry
description:
I need a calm and quiet adult only home with adopters with experience of collies.

I would like to be the only dog in my home! I cannot live with cats.

I would love a garden to run and play in!

I would like an active home and love to go for walks.

I have lived in a home before so know the basics.

I am neutered and ready to find my forever home!

Jerry is a lovely boy who originally came to us from a breeder a few years ago and went on to find a wonderful forever home. Sadly, through no fault of his own, he has now returned to our care due to his adopters ill health.

Jerry is a Collie lover’s dream! He is affectionate, clever and eager to be part of the family. He is house trained, walks nicely on the lead and we are told that he has good recall too. He is a very smart boy who already knows a range of commands including sit, wait, off, drop and leave, and would thrive in an active home where he can be kept both mentally and physically stimulated.

Jerry would like to be the only dog in his forever home, however he does enjoy the company of other dogs and would benefit from the opportunity to socialise with other dogs. He would be best suited to an adult only home and would benefit from adopters with collie and rescue dog experience. He would love a rural home, as he can be sensitive to certain sounds and so homes in busier areas wouldn't be suitable for him. He would really benefit from adopters who are around for much of the day and have plenty of time to spend with him.

Jerry does show some signs of separation anxiety, so his new family will need to continue building up the time he is left gradually. We are told that he is happy being left for short periods but will need patience and understanding while he settles. He isn’t keen on car travel, and he can be sensitive to sounds and may react to sounds such as fizzy drink cans or bottles opening, sneezing, coughing, nose blowing, printers, hoovers, microwaves, lorry reversing beeps, motorbike revving, army range noise and fireworks. He will bark towards you but with reassurance he settles quickly. He has never bitten and is just worried but his new owners will need to continue noise desensitization training in the home. It may never be something he entirely overcomes.

Jerry has settled back into the rescue well and is a wonderful boy who is already enjoying snuggles and really loves to play! He is a truly special boy who has so much love to give and is ready to find a calm, understanding home to call his own once again.

He has had a wound on his right paw that has caused a limp, which has since healed and he is no longer showing signs of limping.

Jer-bear, Jerry! This handsome boy is going to melt someone’s heart.

Jerry has come in from a home with a variety of noise related triggers but do not let this put you off meeting him, for he is one quick brave border collie. He was once visibly stressed at the sight of a can of pop and more stressed when carbonated drink cans/bottles were opened. He has improved and is not as stressed when he sees a can of pop and due to him moving around the rescue we have given him some time to settle as he wasn’t feeling himself in his previous kennel.

Since moving he has been showing us bit by bit his playful sassy side. He loves going for his morning walk to take a break from the overwhelming kennels, Jerry has even been socialising with other dogs. We have found that he gets on well with other dogs however if put into an overwhelming situation he is likely to redirect onto his kennel buddy so is safer for him and any other dogs if he is not homed with one. We have found that once he is comfortable with his people, he is quick to desensitise to certain triggers.

For example: one of his handlers is diabetic and uses cans of lucozade to treat hypos on walks and in kennels. He has become alert but has not reacted and is less anxious at the sight of cans or bottles now

When I first met Jerry I had heard about his triggers and was very curious, he is a very interesting young dog who is bound to be the light of someone’s life!

I have been working with Jerry for a little while now and have found that his can/bottle related trigger is a bit deeper than we were led to believe, the visual prompt causes him some stress, but found that he is quick to look for comfort. He really enjoys spending time with his doggy friends and looks to humans for comfort, and enjoys going out for walks, he seems to feel more relaxed on walks and have found once triggered by a noise he over analyses the indoor environment not so much the outdoor space.

During Jerry's stay here I plan to slowly introduce new noises and help him to understand that new noises are not necessarily a bad thing.

Jerry would like to be the only dog in his forever home. He would be best suited to an adult only home and would benefit from adopters with collie and rescue dog experience. He would love a rural home, as he can be sensitive to certain sounds and so homes in busier areas wouldn't be suitable for him. He would really benefit from adopters who are around for much of the day and have plenty of time to spend with him.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Indie
description:
I would like an understanding home, and I can live with older teens, 16+

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I have never been on walks, but may be too scared and will need some time.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I will be spayed before going to my forever home!

17-06-26 Indie is a gentle and worried little lady who has come to us from a breeder. At the moment, she is not comfortable approaching people in her kennel and will often shy away from her handlers. She is still learning that people can be kind and trustworthy, and she finds handling difficult right now, sometimes showing that she is touch-sensitive.

Indie will need a patient and understanding home with adopters who are experienced with, or knowledgeable about, ex-breeding dogs and the challenges they can face when adjusting to life in a home environment. She can live with older teens, aged 16+ who are sensible and will allow her the patience she needs. She will need time to settle at her own pace and should never be rushed.

She will need at least one kind and confident resident dog in her new home. A friendly canine companion will help her learn about home life, provide reassurance, and support her as she continues to build her confidence.

As Indie has never lived in a home before, she will need help learning all the basics, including house training and walking on a lead. To begin with, gentle garden explorations may be the best way for her to experience the world while she gains confidence.

Indie is a timid girl who will need plenty of love, patience, and understanding from her adopters as she learns about trust, companionship, and all the comforts of family life. In the right home, given the time and support she needs, we believe she has every chance of blossoming into a happy and devoted companion.

Indie has been given the Gift of Life by Kimberley Page

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Maggie
description:
I'd like a quiet, adult-only home to help me relax and feel safe.

I need just one doggy friend, my size or larger. I haven't met cats so I'm unsure!

I'd like a secure garden to play in that has 6ft fencing.

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy one or two walks a day!

I have lived in a home so I know the basics.

I have been spayed and I’m ready for my new home! Read below for more!

26-05-26 Maggie is a sweet Cockapoo who has returned to us from a home following an incident involving a smaller dog in the household. We do not know exactly what happened, but during the night while out in the garden, Maggie became frightened and was involved in a fight after the other dog barked.

Maggie is a very shy and sensitive girl who is wary of strangers, although she does allow handling. She is trying very hard to be brave and is beginning to show small signs of progress, even getting close to taking treats from our hands, though she is not quite confident enough just yet.

Maggie is not yet confident enough to be an only dog, so we feel she would settle best in a home with one calm and confident resident dog of a similar size or larger, who can help her feel safe and reassured as she adjusts.

As Maggie has previously lived in a home, she will be familiar with the sights and sounds of a home environment. However, she may still need some support with house training as she settles into a new routine.

Maggie can already walk on a lead, so she would enjoy one or two walks around the local area with the resident dog when she is settled in.

Maggie is particularly worried by men and did initially bark at the male resident in her previous home. With time, patience, and gentle handling, she slowly began to come around. Unfortunately, there was also an incident where Maggie became tangled in her harness and, in a panic, bit the hand of the male owner who was trying to help free her. Because of this, Maggie will need a calm adult-only home with patient and understanding adopters who have experience or understanding of the challenges that can come with rehoming ex-breeding dogs.

Since Maggie has been with us, she has shown that when she feels stressed or overwhelmed, she may try to find a way to escape by climbing. Because of this, she will need a very secure garden with boundaries of at least 6ft to help keep her safe and prevent any potential escape attempts.

Once Maggie knows and trusts someone, she is a very sweet and happy girl. In the right calm and understanding home, with patient and dog-savvy owners who are willing to let her settle at her own pace, we are confident she will continue to make wonderful progress and slowly come out of her shell.

Maggie is currently being treated for an ear infection in the left ear.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Cuba
description:
I will need a loving and understanding adult-only home!

I'd prefer to be the only dog. I can't live with cats.

I am still unsure about the world, so a secure garden would be ideal.

I walk well on lead but I can be spooked. Let's take it slow!

Please be patient with me, I'm nervous and learning everything from scratch.

I have been neutered and am ready for my forever home. Read below for more!

Cuba is a very sensitive and shy boy who is still finding the world quite overwhelming, but who is making lovely progress as his confidence slowly grows. Cuba arrived with very little life experience and can be easily worried by new situations, so he needs a calm, gentle approach and plenty of time to build trust.

As he gets to know people, Cuba is becoming increasingly sociable. He may need a couple of meetings to feel comfortable, but once he trusts you, he will happily approach with his tail wagging and enjoy quiet, gentle fuss. He particularly loves relaxing in his bed with trusted company nearby and finds calm strokes very reassuring, often drifting off to sleep while being gently fussed. As his confidence grows, his playful side is beginning to shine. He adores playing with toys, cuddling, giving kisses, and will happily bounce around like a lunatic when he’s feeling safe and excited.

Cuba has never lived in a home before, so everything will be new to him. He will need experienced, adult-only adopters who understand nervous dogs and are happy to let him settle at his own pace. Patient guidance will be needed as he learns all about home life, including house training and walking on a harness and lead. He has recently started harness training and is beginning to enjoy it, but this will still need to be continued slowly and positively, starting with gentle practice in the garden. Once out and about, Cuba enjoys taking in the world at his own pace and loves sniffing everything in sight on his walks.

Cuba would prefer to be the only dog in the home, so his adopters will need to be around enough to keep him company and provide reassurance as he settles.

He will need a home without cats or small furries as he does have a prey drive.

He is quite fond of using his voice and enjoys a good grumble rather than barking, which is very much part of his character. Beneath his worried exterior, Cuba is a sweet, gentle boy who has already formed strong bonds with familiar staff and is learning that not all people are bad.

Cuba will need a calm and understanding home where his boundaries are respected and he can have his own safe space. With kindness, patience, and the right support, he has the potential to continue blossoming into a truly rewarding companion.

Cuba has had a bilateral entropion repair.

Cubawill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

Cuba has had quite a busy few weeks at the rescue and has had to put his big boy pants on!! I’ve started taking him into our on site cabin which is similar to being in a home environment. It’s got a sofa, bed, TV etc … so I thought it would be a good idea to start taking him in there to help desensitise him to new things.

Obviously he’s never been in a home before, so everything was completely brand new for him. The first time he was very nervous and really didn’t know what to do at all. He was holding himself low to the floor and hunched, but he was walking around doing little sniffs of things. I was sat on the sofa and he did come over to me a couple of times for reassurance which did help a little, but after about only 5 minutes I took him back to his kennel as I didn’t want to overdo it.

The second time he was still nervous again, but after a little while he did start to drop his guard and became quite curious. He kept coming over to me for a fuss and a treat, so that was a really good sign being brave enough to eat in there. He was fascinated by the TV and didn’t quite know what to think of it. He was a bit scared of it I think, but equally as intrigued. I’m going to keep taking him into the cabin once a week or so just to ease him into it slowly, and then hopefully that will help his transition to home life when he does get adopted.

Cuba has been with me for over 3 months now and I literally love him more and more every day. I’m lucky to have a very close relationship with him and he loves me and trusts me completely, so I see a side to him that not many other people get to see.

He absolutely oozes charisma and character, and he makes me laugh and smile every time we’re together. He’s fiercely loyal, super affectionate, and SO comical. He gives the best of everything. He loves running around the yard with me and playing with toys, but loves a big sit down snuggle equally as much. So much so, he now likes to sprawl across my whole lap and fall into a deep sleep on me (snores and all)! His kennel is filled with plush toys that he loves to carry around in his mouth and sleep with, and he’s SO gentle with them. Isn’t he just the sweetest boy!?

He does still find a lot of things quite challenging, but I do feel as though we’re still making steady progress, and you can’t rush certain things. He’s still very easily spooked by loud noises, cars passing, people trying to approach him etc, so on walks especially I do have to make sure I’m paying complete attention to him. Most of the time he does recover quickly with a bit of reassurance from me, but then sometimes it will ruin his whole walk and he’ll immediately want to turn around and go back to the safety of his kennel. That’s okay though - I always allow it and take him back to make sure he knows he always has a safe place as and when he needs it.

We’ve briefly mentioned in a previous update that Cuba doesn’t like other dogs that much, but I hope this won’t put you off him as he’s not reactive to other dogs out on walks or anything like that - he simply just doesn’t like to share his space with them. I don’t think he was socialised with others apart from his old kennel friend before he came to us, so I think it’s understandable.

If you have the time and the patience to put the work in with Cuba, I can promise you 10 times over that adopting him will be the best decision of your life. Once he settles, he will truly be your best friend and the love of your life. That’s how I feel about him and I’m just his kennel mum!! He is utterly special and he deserves someone equally as special, who will love him as much as he will love them. He has such a big heart and he really does deserve the world and more.

Cuba has come on leaps and bounds over the past few weeks, and I feel so lucky to have been appointed as his “Kennel Auntie,” spending two days a week with this incredibly handsome boy.

Although Cuba can still be wary of strangers, the bond he forms once he trusts you is truly something special. Knowing him from when he first arrived, you honestly wouldn’t recognise him now. He used to hide away at the back of his kennel, wishing we would go away, now he demands to be the centre of attention and absolutely smothers you in kisses. While he may take a little time to warm up to new people, once he feels safe, his affectionate nature shines through.

Cuba has taken some big steps (quite literally!) and is absolutely smashing his harness training. His progress has been incredible, and his confidence grows more each day. He happily trots along on walks, tail wagging, taking in all the exciting new smells he’s never experienced before. Watching him discover so many “firsts” is truly magical.

We’ve found that Cuba isn’t too fussed about other dogs (aside from his kennel friend Chippy), so he would be best suited to a home where he can be the only dog. This will allow him to soak up all the love and attention he thrives on.

I cherish every moment I get to spend with Cuba. He is the sweetest, soppiest boy. He is so full of love, yet not enough people get to see this side of him. He adores playing with toys, cuddling, bouncing around like a lunatic, giving kisses, and sniffing everything in sight on his walks. But most of all, he loves his people.

There isn’t a moment with Cuba where you won’t feel loved. He makes every second count and whoever is lucky enough to adopt him should do the same. He truly is a special boy, and you wouldn’t want to miss out on him.

Cuba has been on my section for a few weeks now and I am just head over heels in love with him. I knew he was a nervous and sensitive boy so I have taken things slow with him and left him be to suss me out and get used to my routine, but it really didn’t take long for him to become quite attached to me. Within a few days he started to trust me, and now I would say he actually trusts me completely. So much so, I’ve been able to start harness training with him, put medication in his eyes after his surgery, and he’s given me his belly a couple of times for fusses!!!

I still haven’t taken him outside of the secure yard on his harness yet, but I do think he’s ready so I’m going to try it really soon. His biggest fear was having any tension on the lead - he would get really spooked by it and panic - but I think we’re just about over that now and he’s gotten used to it.With Cuba, once he gets to know you and forms a bond with you he is an extremely affectionate, happy, and playful boy, but he is still standoffish with people he doesn’t know and he doesn’t trust them to touch him or handle him. This is something his future adopters will need to take into consideration and prepare for. He’ll just need to be given some time, patience and gentleness to begin with. I am certain he’ll come around, but it will need to be in his own time as he can’t be pushed.

Cuba is hilarious and so goofy, and he will make such a great addition to someone’s home in time, but it does need to be the right home to ensure he’s set up for only success. I adore him very much and I just know his future parents will too, so please keep him in mind if you think you could be a good match for him!

Cuba is making lovely progress and is becoming increasingly sociable as his confidence grows. He is still finding walking on a harness quite scary and will need a patient home willing to help him continue this training at his own pace.

Cuba may need a couple of meetings to feel comfortable, but once he knows you, he will happily approach with his tail wagging and enjoy a gentle fuss. He particularly loves relaxing in his bed with trusted company nearby, quietly enjoying being stroked as he drifts off to sleep.

Cuba prefers to have his own space at mealtimes, as he can be a little protective over his food while eating, and this should be respected in his new home.

At heart, he is a very sweet and gentle boy who is learning to trust and has already formed strong bonds with familiar staff. He is simply a scared boy who needs a fresh start in a calm, understanding and respectful home where he can continue to blossom.

Cuba is a very nervous and shy boy who is finding the world quite overwhelming at the moment. He needs a slow, gentle approach and plenty of time to build trust, but once he feels safe with someone, he will allow calm, gentle fuss while settled in his bed, which he finds very reassuring. Underneath his worried exterior, Cuba is simply a big, scared baby who needs kindness, patience, and understanding to help him feel secure.

Cuba will need an adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand nervous dogs and are happy to let him settle at his own pace. He has never lived in a home before, so everything will be completely new to him. His adopters will need to be patient and supportive as he learns about home life, including house training and how to walk on a harness and lead. Gentle practice in the garden will be an important starting point before he is ready to venture further afield.

Cuba will also need at least one kind, confident resident dog in the home. He gains comfort and confidence from other dogs and will benefit greatly from having a calm canine role model to help guide him and show him that the world is a safe place. He is not suitable to be an only dog.

Cuba gets fed separately from his kennel friends as he can be a little protective of his food from other dogs while eating. That said, he is fine taking treats when around other dogs, but would need to be fed separately at meal times or when having long lasting treats and chews in his new home.

Cuba is currently learning to walk on a harness, but this is something he finds very challenging and will require a great deal of time, patience, and dedication. He is also quite fond of using his voice and enjoys a good grumble rather than barking, which is very much part of his character.

Cuba will need a calm, quiet home where he can have his own safe space and plenty of downtime alongside gentle interaction. With love, reassurance, and understanding adopters, he will slowly begin to realise that not all people are bad and that he is finally safe. In the right home, with the right support, Cuba has the potential to blossom into a deeply rewarding companion.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?
