name: Cloe
description:
I need an adult only home that understands ex-breeders.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I need an extra secure garden and home that can accommodate my needs.

I have never been on walks, but may be too scared and will need some time.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have had 7 teeth extracted and an inguinal hernia repaired. Read below for more!

Cloe is a very worried little lady who has come to us from a breeding environment and is still learning that people can be kind. It is clear she has had very little positive experience with humans in the past, and as a result she finds trust incredibly difficult. She can be flighty when worried and may choose to protect herself if she feels pressured or uncomfortable.

Cloe is a sweet but complex girl who has already shown us that, once she truly trusts someone, she has a wonderful and affectionate side. With her favourite member of staff, the very first person who cared for her when she arrived, she is a completely different dog. She happily approaches for attention, enjoys gentle fusses, is comfortable being picked up, and greets him with excitement and affection. Watching the bond they have built is incredibly special and shows just how much potential Cloe has when given the time she needs to feel safe.

Since arriving with us, Cloe has continued to make lovely progress. There is now a real sparkle in her eyes that seems to grow brighter every day. She is much perkier, always happy to see familiar faces, and is even beginning to show a mischievous little side that makes everyone smile. She has become an absolute joy to care for and has settled into her daily routine beautifully.

Although trust does not come easily to Cloe, she is becoming increasingly comfortable with the people she knows. She is now much more accepting of being picked up and handled and takes these interactions in her stride. She recently enjoyed a pamper session in our grooming room, where she happily allowed her beautiful coat to be brushed and a few small matts to be clipped away, taking the whole experience in her stride. Moments like these are a wonderful reminder of just how far she has already come.

Cloe has also recently started her harness training. Although she is still a little too timid to venture out on walks just yet, she is making a fantastic start and we are confident that, with a little more time and encouragement, she will soon be ready to begin exploring the world beyond the rescue.

Cloe will need a calm and quiet adult-only home with no visiting children and adopters who have experience of very worried ex-breeding dogs. Her new family will need to understand that building a relationship with her is likely to take time and that progress will come through patience, consistency, and allowing her to make her own choices.

The best way to win Cloe over is to let her approach on her own terms. She needs people who can resist the temptation to rush her and instead allow her to work out for herself that they are safe and trustworthy. Once she reaches that point, we believe she has the potential to become a truly loving and devoted companion.

Cloe absolutely loves the company of other dogs and will need a kind, calm, and confident resident dog in her new home. Having a canine friend gives her comfort and reassurance and will help guide her as she learns about life beyond the rescue. We have also discovered that Cloe is not comfortable around cats and can become quite fixated by them, so she will need a cat-free home.

As she has never lived in a home before, Cloe's adopters will need to be patient as she learns all about home life, including house training and other everyday routines. She has now started to become familiar with wearing a harness, and while she is not yet ready to walk on lead, her adopters should continue this training gently and positively, allowing her confidence to grow at her own pace.

Although progress with Cloe may be slow at times, she continues to surprise us with just how much she is blossoming. She is becoming more affectionate with every passing week, and it has been a privilege to watch her begin to enjoy life. In a loving home where she has all the time in the world to build trust, alongside a kind resident dog, we believe she will flourish into an incredibly devoted companion. She has already waited far too long for the family she deserves, and we can't wait to see her finally find it.

Cloe has had 7 teeth extracted at the time of her spay, as well as an inguinal hernia repaired. She also had a lump removed from her right upper eyelid.

Cloe has been given the Gift of Life by Carol Unsworth

Progress with Cloe is still very slow, but it is there and it is happening, so that’s all that matters. Since my last update she has opened herself up to me ever so slightly, and now gets very excited to see me everything morning and will run up to me and sniff my fingers with a waggy tail. I know that may not sound like a lot to most, but for a dog like Cloe it’s a big step forward.

I mentioned in my last update that I had to take her into the vets a few times and it made her more frightened of me, so since then I’ve completely left her be as I felt as though we needed to reset. I’ve spent a lot of time with her, just sitting down and being present, but no touching (until today). She wasn’t happy about me picking her up and was trying to bite the blanket I was holding, but she put up way less of a fight than she did a few weeks ago … so I’m taking that as a win!! I took her outside of her kennel just to try and take some nice photos of her and she coped really well. When we were finished and I put her back in her kennel, I got down on the floor and wiggled my fingers at her to see if she was still my friend, and thankfully she did come over with a waggy tail, so that made me happy.

Over the next few weeks I’m going to continue some gentle handling like today, every so often so that she gets more used to it, but it also won’t overwhelm her. I’ll listen to her and what she’s telling me .. stop if it’s too much, and do a little more if she’s ready.I really love Cloe and I’m understanding her more and more each day, and I think she feels the same way about me. I’m starting to feel hopeful!!

Cloe is a sweet but complex little girl who has clearly been through a great deal in her life. She has major trust issues with humans and so far only trusts one person out of all the staff that work here. At the rescue we see 2 sides of her. One side is a happy, excitable girl who enjoys a little fuss and is fine to be picked up (by her favourite person), and the other side is a scared girl who would rather be left alone (by everyone else). Her favourite person here is the first person that worked with her when she first arrived, so I think she sees him as her ‘rescuer’ which is very sweet. She definitely trusts him and it’s quite shocking to see the difference in her when she’s around him - in a good way! With everyone else, she’s still very standoffish and doesn’t tolerate being touched or handled at all. She will nip to protect herself, but this doesn’t mean she’s a nasty dog at all .. she’s simply just that scared.I’ve been working with Cloe for about a month now and even though she’s a lot more comfortable around me now than she used to be, she definitely still doesn’t trust me. I think it’s because I had to take her into the vets a couple of times when she first came over to my section, and it’s definitely stunted our relationship. Normally with dogs like Cloe, I won’t handle them for a good while until I establish some form of bond with them, but I couldn’t do this with Cloe as she had to see the vet. Since then, I’ve been leaving her to her own devises and trying to win her over from afar. Although she still hardly approaches me, she’s more relaxed in my presence now .. so at least we are making progress.I think in Cloe’s case, the best way to work with her is to just leave her come to you first. If she can work out by herself that you’re kind and not a threat, you’re heading in the right direction!! And then when she trusts you, she’ll drop her guard with you and become a little sweetheart like she is with her favourite staff member here. In a home where she has all the time in the world to bond with her new parents, I think she will absolutely blossom!!! But it may take a little while to get there, so her future adopters will need to be very understanding and patient with her. It will be SO worth it though, I promise!

Cloe is a very worried little lady who has come to us from a breeding environment and is not yet comfortable with being handled. It’s clear she has had very little experience of kindness, so she can be quite flighty and may panic if approached too quickly and can choose to make the wrong decision if pushed out of her comfort zone.

Cloe settles quickly once she feels safe and has already begun making lovely progress. With her favourite member of staff she is already showing her funny and affectionate side and is happy to approach him, kiss his fingers and will even do a little excited dance. With patient, understanding adopters who can appreciate why she may feel scared or defensive and support her as she builds trust, we believe she will settle in and start to come out of her shell.

She will need a calm and quiet adult-only home with no visiting children and with adopters who have experience of scared ex-breeding dogs and who are willing to give her the patience, kindness, and time she needs to truly thrive.

Cloe is already starting to approach you slowly on her own terms and so will need plenty of time, gentle encouragement, and extra TLC to continue building her confidence. She absolutely loves the company of other dogs and will need a kind, calm, and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her as she adjusts to home life.

As she has never lived in a home before, Cloe's adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she settles in and learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She doesn't yet walk on a lead and harness and will need to practice around the home and garden once she is more comfortable with being handled.

Cloe has had 7 teeth extracted at the time of her spay as well as an inguinal hernia repaired. She had a lump on her right upper eye lid that has been removed.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Carry
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed & have a Grade 3 patella luxation in my right hind leg.

Carry is a sweet and gentle girl who came to us from a breeder looking for a fresh start. When she first arrived, she was very scared and unsure of people, but she has already made some wonderful progress during her time with us and is becoming more confident every day.

Carry is now a happy and lively little lady with a constantly wagging tail. She has discovered toys and is learning how to play with them, something she is thoroughly enjoying, and she has also revealed herself to be quite the foodie, which has been a great help in building her confidence.

Although Carry is still nervous about direct handling and may move away if approached too quickly, she is incredibly curious and always likes to be close by, watching what is going on. She is happy to approach people for a sniff and will gently take treats from your hand, showing just how far she has come already. We feel it won't be long before she starts to feel comfortable accepting gentle fusses from those she trusts.

Carry will be looking for a calm and quiet adult-only home where she can continue to build her confidence at her own pace. She relies heavily on other dogs for reassurance and will need at least one kind and confident resident dog in her new home. A canine companion will help guide her through new experiences and provide comfort as she settles into family life.

As Carry has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be understanding while she learns the basics, such as house training and walking on a lead. We have not yet started harness training with her, but as her confidence continues to grow, we feel she is becoming ready to begin exploring this next step. Her adopters should be prepared to continue this training gently and positively once she is home.

Carry is the kind of dog who can brighten even the darkest of days. She is an absolute joy to spend time with and, in a loving home where she is given patience, understanding, and plenty of reassurance, we believe she will truly blossom into a happy and trusting companion.

Our vets have noted that Carry has a Grade 3 patella luxation in her right hind leg. This is not causing her any issues at present and may improve with gentle exercise, but it will need to be monitored by her adopters and their veterinary team. Should treatment be required in the future, this condition would not be covered by insurance.

Carry has been given the Gift of Life by Wendy Bowen

I’m lucky enough to get to work with Carry twice a week whilst her kennel mum is off for the weekend, and every week I get to spend with her the more in confidence she grows!Carry is such a character, and everyday she shows you how much she is blossoming and how much she appreciates feeling safe & loved. She absolutely loves cuddling up to her kennel friends, and tearing it up in the yard and the kennel. Her one true love is toys, and as long as you have toys around you needn’t worry if she is happy or satisfied!!We recently took Carry out on a harness for the first time, and as a beginner we think she smashed it. She didn’t freak out, and simply followed the lead of her kennel mum & another dog by her side. We think with another few more goes she will be enjoying her small adventures in no time. Carry broke up the walk with a few stops for some squeezey cheese which she simply couldn’t say no to!Carry is such an adoring girl, and watching her flourish infront of our eyes is so beautiful to watch. A loving family, a resident dog, and lots of understanding is all she needs, and she’ll be ready to take on the world, day by day & little by little.

Carry is coming out of her shell more and more every day and is now a very happy and lively girl who always has a waggy tail. She’s discovered toys and is learning how to play with them which she’s really enjoying, and she’s also turning into a massive foodie which is always very helpful with nervous dogs. She is still hand shy and will run away if I go towards her, but she’s extremely curious and always likes to be close to me watching what I’m doing. She’s happy to approach me to give me lots of sniffs and to take treats from my hand, so we’re definitely moving in the right direction and I really don’t think it’s going to be long until she starts to accept gentle fusses.

Carry is the type of dog that can put a smile on your face even on the darkest of days, and she really is a joy to work with and be around. In a home environment where she has lots of time with her new parents, I think she’ll absolutely blossom. She probably will be nervous at first of course, so as long as her adopters are patient with her and work with her through her worries and fears, I think it will work out so well.

I haven’t started harness training with Carry yet as she hasn’t been on my section for that long, but I do feel as though she may be ready to start exploring it soon. So, over the next couple of weeks I’ll get one fitted to her and see how she does. I’ll keep you all updated!

Carry is a sweet, gentle girl who has come to us from a breeder and is currently very scared, timid, and unsure of humans. She will need plenty of time, patience, and TLC in her new home to help her learn to trust and build a loving bond with her family. Once she gets to know you, she will approach you for a fuss but she isn't comfortable being picked up at the moment as this is very new for her.

Carry will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She relies on other dogs for comfort and will need at least one kind and confident resident dog in her new home. A canine companion will provide her with reassurance and help guide her as she learns about home life, giving her someone to lean on as her confidence grows.

As Carry has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be understanding while she learns the basics, such as house training and walking on a lead. The big wide world may feel overwhelming for her at first, so starting with calm, gentle experiences, like exploring the garden will help her gradually gain confidence.

With love, patience, and understanding, Carry has all the potential to blossom into a happy and trusting companion.

Our vets have noted that Carry has a Grade 3 patella luxation in her right hind leg, this isn't causing her any issues at the moment and may improve with gentle exercise but it will need to be monitored by her adopters and their vets and should she need any treatment in the future it wouldn't be coverable by insurance.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. name: Venus
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I will need kind resident dogs to take me under their wing. I am fine with cats!

I will need a secure garden where I can learn to go outside without fear.

I am not ready to learn about walking on a lead and harness yet.

I am in a foster home now and am house trained!

I am spayed and have been treated for sore ears.

Venus is a timid girl who has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived, she was so worried that she would run away and hide when she saw us, but she has started to make slow but steady progress. She spent quite a few months in kennels, slowly settling in and starting to learn all about her new life, but she has now moved to a wonderful foster home and has been getting to experience life in a home for the very first time.

Initially she was very worried about humans, but as she has settled in she has started to become more comfortable around humans and is even fine with strangers visiting the home. She has started to feel brave enough to come forward to collect a treat from in front you. She is still scared of hands and is very worried about handling and finds this quite stressful at the moment, but this is something her fosterer will work on getting her used to.

Venus will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home and would need adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will allow her to settle in at her own pace without any pressure or expectations. She relies heavily on other dogs for comfort and reassurance and will be looking for a multi dog home with confident resident dogs to be her friend and companion and take her under their wing. She gets on well with all dogs, but tends to gravitate towards younger male dogs and bigger dogs. She cannot cope without the company of another dog and as she isn't walking on a lead yet, she will need to always have a dog home with her, while the other dog/s are out on walks.

Venus had never lived in a home before moving to foster, but she has taken to home life very well and is now house trained and sleeps quietly all night on her fosterers bed. She doesn't walk on a lead or harness just yet, but she has visited a secure field, which she thoroughly enjoyed and this will hopefully help her progress. When she's ready, she will need to start by wearing a harness around the home and garden, before progressing to walking on a lead around the garden.

Venus' journey may not be a quick one, but it will be so rewarding when you see her reach new milestones like wagging her tail for the first time and in the right home, she will truly blossom.

Venus has been treated for sore ears and she has a travel document.

Venus has settled well in foster with me. She is 100% toilet trained and sleeps on my bed alongside her foster sisters all night. She shares her food and toys politely and can be left for a few hours in her crate. She has met my cats with no interest too, as well as coping with my respectful teenage grandchildren visiting my home.

Venus has tolerated a visit to the vet for her booster vaccination, has had a lovely bath and also visited the secure field which she thoroughly enjoyed which I think will be the way forward with her. Venus is still wary of human hands but is becoming more confident in my movements around the house and watches everything with interest now not fear as she did in the beginning.

She thrives on her routine and the company of other dogs in the household. She has met visiting dogs here and gets along with them all but gravitates towards younger males and bigger dogs. Venus would need a kind, experienced, patient adopter and playful, kind dogs to support her even further in her onward journey.

Hope this helps Venus find her forever home as she's been waiting a long long time bless her.

Venus has settled in really well to foster, almost immediately she followed my dogs and picked up the routine.

She sleeps in a crate in the kitchen all night and is very happy to potter around in the garden and around the home. She has been clean in the house so far.

I have picked her up a few times to take her coat off and it’s clear she finds handling very stressful. We will work on that slowly over the coming weeks. Venus is not ready for lead walking yet

Venus is a very sweet little dog, inoffensive and shares really nicely. She likes to flip the toys and explore the garden.

Our beautiful little Venus had a well needed groom this week by our amazing groomer who is excellent with very scared ex breeding dogs. I would have liked to have gotten her more used to being brushed etc first, but her coat was staring to get a little matted with all the rain we’ve been having so we couldn’t wait any longer. She was stressed during the groom, but she did manage her back, belly, legs and feet being shaved relatively ok. It’s her head that she’s the most sensitive with so this did take some work. She was too scared of having the clippers anywhere near, so we just had to use scissors as best we could. I’m not sure what’s happened in her past to make her so fearful of her face being touched, but it’s something I’ll try and work on with her as gently as I can.

Aside from grooming, Venus is still struggling to make progress and it’s breaking my heart. I’m doing everything I normally do with my scared dogs to try and earn her trust, but she’s just not ready to take any steps forward. She’ll eat food in front of me and take treats when I throw them on the floor near her, but she’s still not brave enough to even take them from my hand. This is usually the first step in starting to form some sort of trust, so I’m going to keep trying … but what Venus really needs, is an experienced home with people who will understand her and be able to work with her.In general, Venus is quite a happy girl and she’s very easy to look after at the rescue. She loves her food, toys, a cosy bed, other dogs, and she shares everything with no problems. She’s been kennelled with so many dogs who have come and gone since her arrival, and that just makes me so sad. I REALLY hope she gets her happy ever after soon. She needs it and deserves it so much.

Venus has recently been moved to my section to ensure that she has consistency and routine, and the same couple of people working with her continuously so she can finally start to establish a bond. I did meet Venus when she first arrived and I knew that she was a nervous girl that doesn’t like to be handled, and unfortunately that is still the reality for her. She definitely finds kennel life quite overwhelming and I think it’s holding her back massively. In a calm and quiet home environment with someone working 1 on 1 with her every day, I do believe she would progress a lot faster. But, until the day comes when she’s chosen, I’ll do everything I can to ease her worries and fears.

Venus does need a lot of work when it comes to behind handled as at the moment, she hates anything being done to her (such as grooming etc). I’m going to take things slow with her, but I do think she’s going to need to be pushed out of her comfort zone just a little, or she won’t learn that our hands are kind. I won’t do anything that’s going to set her back or stress her out, it will just be gentle guidance in the right direction.

I’ll keep you all updated on our progress!

I adore Venus; she is a nervous girl, but I can see that she wants to learn to trust humans. She just isn't quite there yet. She will now come over and have a sniff of whatever I may have in my hand, but then she does back up again.

We do not know what she has been through prior to arriving here, so it could take her some time to get used to being comfortable with us. I try every day that I'm at the rescue, I sit in her kennel or the yard with her for a bit, just so she can get used to me being there and for her to start to understand that not all humans will harm her. I think she is starting to understand this, but it's going to take some time. So, having said all that, she will need a home with people who are going to be patient and give their time to helping her, but also will give her her space when she needs it, so she doesn't get too overwhelmed.

Venus needs at least one other confident resident dog to help her settle in, and to start gaining some confidence, she should also learn house training skills by copying what they do. I am hoping that by being in a home, it will help her confidence. Kennel life can be stressful and overwhelming for many dogs, and so we don't always get to see their true personalities coming through.

So please, if you think you could give Venus a loving home and help to show her that not everything or everyone is scary, then please put in an application or come to the rescue on one of our showarounds to see her in person. She is a beautiful girl, just got off to a bad start in life.

Venus has been at Many Tears for a little while now, and within that time, she has somewhat settled into kennel life, but is still finding the environment and ever-changing staff members a bit too overwhelming.

Venus is still quite touch-sensitive and hasn't had much human experience before finding herself here, so calm and kind adopters with ex-breeder experience would be beneficial. I think with time and patience, and a few more creature comforts, Venus will become a fan of home life and everything it has to offer.

I haven't been working with Venus long as she's only just come over to my section. Straight away you can see that she is a scared girly and given that we don't know anything about her past other than that she's come from a breeder, trusting humans fully might take a bit longer for her, which is understandable.

I am making sure I do not push her or stress her out too much. I try and sit in the kennel and in the yard with her everyday, even if she's not ready to come over and say hello, it's hopefully just getting her used to me being there.

I offer her treats, she won't take them out of my hand just yet but I can place them just in front of me and she will come up and take them, progress, and in my eyes all progress is progress no matter how small.

I feel like she would progress more in a home as there won't be any of the kennel life stresses for her, it can be overwhelming here for some dogs and although we try very hard to provide them with everything they need and give them the best day we can every day, it's not going to be like home life.

Hopefully someone, somewhere soon can see past those scared little eyes and understand that she just wants to be loved. She's trying so hard to be brave, all I want for her is to find that forever, loving home where she can finally flourish and show her family the loving, playful side that she so deserves to feel safe showing.

Venus is a very scared girl who has come to us from a breeder. She runs and hides when she sees us approaching so will need an adult only home where there is at least one other dog who is very kind and who will allow her to cuddle up to them for comfort and support if she wants to. Venus can grumble a little when we pick her up but this is purely out of fear as she has no idea that hands can be kind so we will gradually get her used to this.Venus will need a secure garden as she is a flight risk at the moment but with adopters who have plenty of time, love and patience she will come round and they will be rewarded by seeing her transform into the loving girl we are sure is hiding under all her bichon curls. She will need regular trips to a kind and experienced groomer to make sure she is comfortable.Venus really loves her kennel friends so at least one confident dog in her new home will help raise her confidence with people and she will watch them and start to copy what they do. This will also help her learn new skills like house training and lead walking. These may take her time to learn as the garden may be as scary as the inside of a home at first and lead walking will need to start in the garden to make sure she is comfortable wearing a harness and lead before venturing out in the world.

With love, gentle words of encouragement and praise Venus will gradually start to realise there is nothing to fear and you will see her start to blossom.

Venus is currently being treated for sore ears. She has a travel document.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Periwinkle
description:
I need an adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I'd like a secure garden to play and excercise in.

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

I have Grade 2 luxating patellas, which may improve with exercise.

Periwinkle is a gorgeous lady who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a coy but kind little lady who is still finding her feet and building her confidence. Since arriving with us, Periwinkle has made lovely progress. She now allows us to handle her with ease and is beginning to enjoy gentle fuss and attention, often rewarding us with sweet little kisses on our hands. She is growing braver every day and is becoming more confident with the people she knows. Periwinkle has also started her harness training and is making fantastic progress. Beneath her worries is a truly lovely little girl who is slowly learning that people can be trusted.

Periwinkle gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

As Periwinkle has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. However, she has already shown us how willing she is to learn, and her progress with handling and harness training gives us every confidence that she will continue to flourish with patient and understanding adopters.

She will need an adult-only home that can understand she may take a little time to settle in and build trust. Although she has grown in confidence during her stay with us, it is important that her adopters allow her to adjust at her own pace, as she may initially find the transition into home life a little overwhelming. A calm and predictable environment will help her feel safe and allow her confidence to continue growing.

Periwinkle has arrived with a Grade 2 patella luxation. At present, the joint and surrounding muscles appear stable, and she is able to walk and run comfortably without any difficulties. This is something that her adopters and their vet will need to monitor but may improve with gentle exercise as she builds muscle.

Periwinkle loves playing with her kennel friends and enjoys their company, often looking to them for reassurance. Although she can still be a little unsure at times, she is blossoming into a wonderful little dog and has been an absolute angel during her time with us. We know that with time, patience, and the support of a canine companion, Periwinkle will continue to grow in confidence and show even more of her lovely personality.

Periwinkle has made lovely progress since arriving in our care. She now allows us to handle her and is beginning to enjoy gentle fuss and attention. In fact, she'll often reward you with sweet little kisses on your hand!

She is growing braver every day and is becoming more confident with the people she knows. Periwinkle has also started her harness training and is making great progress. She really is an absolute angel, and it's been wonderful to watch her confidence slowly blossom.

Periwinkle is a gorgeous Bichon Cross who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a coy but kind little lady who is still finding her feet and building her confidence. At the moment, Periwinkle is not quite brave enough to approach us on her own, but once she has had a little time in your arms, she begins to relax and enjoy a gentle fuss. Beneath her worries is a sweet-natured girl who is slowly learning that people can be trusted.

As Periwinkle has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Her adopters will need to be patient and understanding as she adjusts to a completely new way of life.

She will need an adult-only home that can understand that she needs time to settle in. A calm and predictable environment will help her feel safe and allow her confidence to grow at her own pace.

Periwinkle loves playing with her kennel friends and enjoys their company, often looking to them for reassurance. Although she lacks confidence right now, we hope that with time, patience, and the support of a canine companion, she will continue to gain confidence and show more of her lovely personality as she settles into home life.

name: Lemon Pie
description:
I can live with older dog-savvy children who understand I need time to settle in.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in as I get comfortable in my new home.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed and my right eye has been removed.

01-06-26 Lemon Pie is an absolute heartbreaker who came to us from a breeder and initially found it difficult to settle into her new environment. We believe this was partly due to discomfort in her right eye, which has since been removed. She is recovering well and already seems much more comfortable. She has corneal scarring on her left eye that will need to be monitored for any future issues that may arise, but she is able to see around it for now.

Lemon Pie can be a little sensitive to touch and may shy away from hands, suggesting that she may not always have been treated with the kindness she deserves. However, once she is gently picked up and held, she settles nicely and simply needs plenty of love, patience, and reassurance to help her learn to trust.

Having never lived in a home before, Lemon Pie will need understanding adopters who can guide her through all the new experiences and routines of family life. She may initially be too nervous for lead walks, so spending time exploring a secure garden at her own pace would be a great way to help her build confidence as she adjusts to her new surroundings.

Lemon Pie will need at least one kind, confident resident dog in her new home to help show her the way and provide reassurance as she settles in. She could live with older, dog-savvy teenagers who will respect her space and offer her the patience and understanding she needs.

She is an incredibly deserving little girl who simply needs time, kindness, and patience to blossom into the happy companion she was always meant to be.

Lemon Pie has been given the Gift of Life by Sarah Hicklin

name: Primrose
description:
I am looking for an incredibly special, adult only home.

I need at least one other dog in my new home who is happy to share their bed.

I need a very secure garden in my new home.

I am not ready to learn about walking on a lead and harness yet.

I am in a foster home and am currently using puppy pads.

I am spayed and ready to find my forever home.

Primrose is a very scared little girl who has come to us from a breeder to start her new life. When she first arrived, she was completely shut down and was too fearful to even leave her bed, choosing to cuddle close to her kennel friends. She had clearly never experienced much in life before and the world around her felt overwhelming and frightening. She soon moved to a wonderful foster home, where she has spent the last several months settling in and adjusting to home life.

Primrose is a very special little dog who will need an equally special home. When she first arrived in foster, she was utterly petrified of people. For several weeks she would bury her head into her chest and hide under a cupboard just to avoid looking at her foster carer. Although she is still very fearful of humans, she has come a long way since then. She doesn’t interact with people or other dogs, but she does find enormous comfort in calm, affectionate little dogs who choose to cuddle into her.

In foster, Primrose has slowly begun to blossom in her own quiet way. She now chooses to sit in one of her three “safe places,” finding comfort in having spaces that feel secure and predictable. She has started to trust her foster family and looks to her fosterer for treats and is now even happy to be placed on the sofa next to her and on occasion has even rested her head on her lap, which is a huge step for Primrose.

At night, when the home is quiet and everyone is in bed, Primrose starts to come out of her shell and has even been seen on camera emptying the toy box and playing by herself, which is a lovely glimpse of the happy dog she may become once she feels truly safe.

Primrose is still incredibly frightened of anything beyond her safe spaces, but is now venturing outdoors into the garden. Her foster mum does have to put her outside, but when she does she is happy to explore it and runs around with excitement. She’s even played chase a couple of times with her foster brothers and sisters. Whilst incredibly nervous she is clearly coming out of her shell a little more now, and her foster mum says it’s wonderful to see this most gentle dog, finally learn it’s okay to be happy and have fun.

She is now happy to wear a harness, although she is not yet ready for walks. She is currently using puppy pads for her toileting.

Primrose will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult-only home with adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will understand that she needs to be allowed to settle in and progress at her own pace, with no pressure or expectations.

She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog who will take her under their wing, cuddle up with her and offer her the reassurance she needs as she settles in to her new home. She seems to prefer smaller dogs and dogs around her size and is less comfortable around larger dogs.

Her adopters must be prepared to dedicate the time, patience, and kindness needed to help her continue building confidence. A quiet environment with no visiting young children is essential. Her fosterer will support the adopter through this process.

With patience, understanding, and love, Primrose has the potential to blossom fully and show the happy, affectionate dog she should have always had the chance to be. She just needs the right family to help her take the next steps.

Primrose has been given the Gift of Life by an Anonymous Supporter

Primrose is a very special little dog who will need a very special home.

She is an incredibly frightened ex-breeding dog whose past has clearly left her deeply traumatised. When she first arrived in foster five months ago, she was utterly petrified of people. For several weeks she would bury her head into her chest and hide under a cupboard just to avoid looking at her foster carer. Although she is still very fearful of humans, she has come a long way since then.

Primrose is probably the gentlest dog her foster carer has ever looked after, but also the most emotionally damaged. She is sweet, patient, and completely unassuming. She never causes any trouble, joins in with nothing, and asks for very little. She doesn’t interact with people or other dogs, but she does find enormous comfort in calm, affectionate little dogs who choose to cuddle into her. She seems to especially like small dogs and appears less comfortable around larger ones.

In foster, Primrose’s little canine housemates have gently helped her in their own way. They share her bed, steal her treats, pinch her food, take her spot on the sofa, and she accepts it all with quiet patience. If placed on the sofa or bed, she will happily snuggle down beside them. She does not actively seek dogs out for play or socialising, but she clearly takes comfort from being close to kind, cuddly companions.

Primrose spends most of her time in her safe places and will usually only move from one safe space to another if encouraged. She does not yet venture out confidently on her own. However, at night, when the house is quiet and her foster carer has gone to bed, Primrose becomes a different little soul. Caught on camera, she carefully empties every toy out of the toy box, and plays by herself. It is a lovely glimpse of the happy dog she may become once she feels truly safe.

Over the past month, Primrose has started to show small but meaningful signs of progress. She now looks to her foster carer for treats, especially when sitting together on the sofa, and she has rested her head on her a couple of times. These moments may seem tiny, but for Primrose they are huge steps forward.

Primrose is currently using puppy pads, as she is still too frightened to go outdoors of her own accord. When placed outside, she will usually tuck herself into a corner of the garden. More recently, if carried around the garden, she has started to show some interest in her surroundings. She is now happy to wear a harness, although she is not yet ready for walks.

Primrose will need an exceptionally kind, patient, and understanding adopter with experience of ex-breeding dogs or very fearful dogs. She must be allowed to go at her own pace, with no pressure and no expectations of quick progress. She needs a calm home with at least one small, affectionate dog who loves to cuddle, as canine companionship is essential for her confidence and comfort.

She also seems wary of men, particularly men with beards, and appears to feel safer with women.

Primrose is both the sweetest and the most broken little dog her foster carer has ever known. But after five months, she is slowly starting to come alive. She needs someone who can see the beauty in the smallest steps and who will cherish every tiny piece of trust she gives. For the right person, helping Primrose heal will be an extraordinary privilege.

Given the chance, Primrose will choose to sit on her own in one of her three “safe places” in her foster home. Whilst it is common for ex-breeding dogs that have never lived in a home before to not want to be near humans, Primrose unusually chooses not to be with the other dogs living in the house either.Whilst she has progressed significantly since going into care, we have decided that there is still a little way to go before we make her available for adoption.Primrose flees from one of her “safe places” to another keeping her body close to the ground. Her foster mum wants to see her walking upright as well as expose her to areas beyond her comfort zones, including outdoors which she currently fears, so she is better prepared for adoption.Whilst her progress is slow, she has still come along way from the early days when she would bury her head in her chest or hide behind or under furniture. She’s even played with a ball for about a minute, which her fosterer said was possibly the most moving experience she’s seen with a dog in years, suggesting there is a happy dog “inside” Primrose wanting to come out.She also uses a puppy pad if placed close to her bed. She eats well, is very gentle, and allows her quieter foster brothers and sisters to cuddle up to her. She also likes her tummy rubbed, is happy to be carried for short periods, and if put next to her foster mum, will lay down and sleep.Before Primrose will be made available for adoption we would like to see Primrose walking upright as well as walking outdoors which she still fears.Anyone applying to adopt Primrose will need experience with very frightened ex-breeding dogs that have never lived in a home. They will need to have at least one confident gentle dog,  ideally no larger than her, and a quiet home with no visiting young children. Importantly they will need to be prepared to dedicate the time it will take to help develop Primrose’s confidence, Her fosterer is happy to help guide the adopter going forwards.

Primrose is a very scared little girl and is unsure of her new surroundings. She hasn’t yet found the courage to leave her bed but she finds comfort in cuddling close to her kennel friends because that’s the only comfort she’s ever known.

Primrose needs a calm, adult only home with at least one gentle dog to guide her and give her the reassurance she needs and who will allow her to cuddle up to them for support and comfort if she wants to. She has never lived in a home, so everything will feel overwhelming at first. She will have no idea about house training and the garden may be as scary as being in the home at first so she will need adopters who have plenty of time and patience to help her.

Lead walking will be a new experience for her and should start in the garden before venturing into the outside world.

With endless patience, kindness and time, her new family will see Primrose slowly open up and blossom into the happy girl she was always meant to be.

name: Fox
description:
I could live with dog-savvy teenagers who respect my space.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I haven't tried walks yet, a secure garden will be fine for now.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

I have been spayed & am ready for my forever home!

Fox is a nervous lady who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. Since arriving, Fox has really started to blossom and is showing us more of her lovely personality every day. She is a wonderfully smiley girl whose confidence continues to grow, and she will now happily approach people for a gentle chin scratch before settling nearby and relaxing in their company.

Fox still takes a great deal of comfort from her canine companions and relies heavily on her kennel friends for reassurance. During her time at the rescue, she has shared her kennel with several different dogs and has been an absolute angel with every one of them, regardless of their size or gender. She is a gentle and social girl who clearly finds confidence from having another dog by her side.

Fox will need a kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she continues to settle in and find her paws. A canine companion will be an important part of helping Fox feel safe and continue building her confidence.

As Fox has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. She will need patient adopters who are happy to take things at her pace and allow her confidence to build gradually.

In the right home, Fox could share her space with dog-savvy teenagers aged 16 and over who can appreciate and understand that she will need a gentle, quiet introduction to the home and her own space to settle in.

Fox is a sensitive and gentle girl who is trying her best to be brave. She has already come such a long way, and with continued patience, support, and the companionship of another dog, she will continue to blossom into a happy and confident companion.

Fox has been given the Gift of Life by Judie Fisher

Fox is really blossoming during her time at the rescue. She’s a wonderfully smiley girl whose confidence grows a little more every day. She will now happily approach people for a gentle chin scratch and is content to lie down and relax nearby.Fox still takes a great deal of comfort from her canine companions and relies heavily on her kennel friends for reassurance. During her time at the rescue, she has shared her kennel with several different dogs and has been an absolute angel with every one of them, regardless of their size or gender.

Fox is a nervous lady who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is currently too worried to ask for fuss, but her confidence is slowly beginning to grow. At the moment, she will only approach when she has the reassurance of hiding behind one of her kennel friends, showing just how much she relies on their support.

Fox gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies heavily on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

Fox is a sensitive and gentle girl who is trying her best to be brave. With time, patience, and the support of another dog, she will begin to feel safe and slowly blossom into a much more confident companion.

name: Nelly
description:
I will need an adult only home with breed-experience.

I’d like to be the only dog in the home and no cats please!

I will need an extra secure garden, at least 6ft+ where I will be safe!

I walk well on the lead but I am strong, so I will need continued training!

I have lived in a home, but may need a few reminders as I settle in.

I've started my vaccinations and will be spayed before adoption.

01-07-26 Nelly, also known as Hippo amongst the kennel staff, is a beautiful girl with a huge personality and an even bigger heart. Nelly came into our care after experiencing a difficult past, having spent much of her life confined to a very small room and sadly being passed between several homes. Despite everything she has been through, her love for people has never faded. She absolutely adores human company and greets everyone she meets like they are her new best friend.

Nelly is a typical fun-loving Bulldog girl who is full of character. She loves attention, affection and being involved in everything her people are doing. She enjoys a good play session, especially when there is a ball involved! Tennis balls, footballs, honestly, anything she can throw around will make her happy. She is even learning that dropping the ball means the fun can start all over again. Once playtime is finished, she is just as happy settling down for a well-earned rest.

Sadly, the instability Nelly has experienced has left her feeling worried when she is left alone. She can become distressed when separated and will need a home where she is not left for long periods. Her adopters will need to build up time apart gradually, using patience and consistency to help her understand that her people will always return.

Nelly is currently learning about other dogs and is not yet comfortable around them. She is doing brilliantly with her muzzle training, which allows her to enjoy her walks safely while she learns that other dogs are not something to worry about. She can enjoy walking alongside calm, quiet dogs, but she may react if she feels overwhelmed, especially with larger dogs or if another dog barks at her first. Her new family will need to continue her muzzle training on walks and support her with positive, patient guidance.

Nelly can already walk on a lead, although she is a strong and enthusiastic girl who can pull when excited. She will need adopters who are confident handling her and who are happy to continue her lead training. She loves getting out for walks, having zoomies and exploring, but she is equally happy being a cuddle-loving couch potato afterwards.

Nelly has come from a home before, so she may already understand some household routines, but she may need gentle reminders as she settles into a new environment. She will need an adult-only home with breed-experienced adopters who understand her bouncy nature and can provide the boundaries, consistency and love she needs. She will need to be the only dog in the home, with no visiting dogs and no cats. She will also need an extra-secure garden with fencing of at least 6ft+.

Nelly is wonderful at the vets and loves the attention she receives there. She enjoys spending time with the staff, soaking up the fuss and showing everyone what a friendly and affectionate girl she is.

Nelly is a resilient, loving dog who has been through more than she should have, yet she continues to greet the world with excitement and affection. She is ready for a family who will spoil her, make her feel safe and show her just how wonderful life can be. For lovers of Bulldog breeds, Nelly’s silly personality and loving nature are sure to bring endless joy.

*Nelly has been measured by our local Dog Licensing Officer and confirmed that she is not considered a banned breed.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Chewwie
description:
I’d like a quiet home filled with love and kindness!

I would like a home with another dog medium size or larger. I am fine with cats.

I will need a secure garden with minimum 6ft fencing.

I'm new to lead walking and need some practice!

I have lived in a home so I know the basics.

I have been neutered and have had 2 teeth removed!

Chewwie originally came to us from a breeder to find his forever home. He was lucky enough to be adopted, but sadly, despite being very loved, it became clear that the home wasn't quite the right match for him and he has now returned to our care to begin his search once again. Chewwie was very worried of the adult sons in the home and also wasn't a great match with the smaller resident dog.

Chewwie is a sensitive boy who had experienced very little before coming into rescue and can find unfamiliar situations overwhelming. In his previous home, he formed a very close bond with his female adopter and absolutely adored her, but remained worried around the adult sons in the household. For this reason, he is looking for a calm and quiet home with patient adopters who have experience of nervous ex-breeding dogs and who will allow him all the time he needs to settle and build trust.

Since returning to our care, however, Chewwie has been an absolute pleasure. From the day he came back, he has been nothing but gentle, loving and affectionate with everyone here. Despite being returned because he was said to be nervous, we have seen a completely different side to him in kennels. He happily bounces over to greet us, is always looking for a fuss, and simply loves being around people. His affectionate nature shines through every day, and he has reminded us just what a special boy he is.

Although he had never lived in a home before his adoption, Chewwie adapted beautifully to family life. He was house trained and thoroughly enjoyed all the comforts of home, especially curling up in his cosy beds. He did find being left alone quite worrying, so his new adopters will need to be around for most of the time initially and gradually build up any time spent alone as his confidence grows.

Chewwie will need another kind and confident resident dog in his new home to help reassure him and show him the ropes, but this dog will need to be medium-sized or larger. He previously shared his home with a cat and happily ignored them, so he could potentially live with a dog-savvy cat.

Since being adopted, Chewwie has learnt to walk nicely on a lead and now really enjoys his walks, although he can still be worried in unfamiliar places and will benefit from ongoing encouragement as his confidence continues to grow.

Once Chewwie knows you, he is an incredibly loving and affectionate companion with a wonderfully cheeky personality. He loves playing with other dogs and is especially fond of his kennel friend. He's also a total water baby and absolutely loves splashing around in the paddling pool, making the most of every opportunity for fun. Like many mischievous youngsters, he has also been known to pinch things he shouldn't! His adopters will need to keep a close eye on shoes and other tempting items, as he developed quite a fondness for chewing shoes in his previous home. He especially enjoys hard toys, while softer toys and enrichment items should be supervised as he can quickly chew through them.

Chewwie is a truly special boy who has so much love to give. His return to rescue has shown us just how affectionate, gentle and resilient he is, and we know that in the right home he will continue to flourish. In return, his lucky adopters will gain a devoted, fun-loving and wonderfully cheeky companion who will fill their days with smiles.

When Chewwie first arrived, he had a lick granuloma on his front leg, which healed well. Following his return to the rescue, he began limping on the same leg and holding it up. X-rays at a local veterinary practice showed no abnormalities or injuries, so it is thought to be a soft tissue injury which should continue to improve. His adopters and their own vet should continue to monitor this as a precaution.

Chewwie has been given the Gift of Life by Paula Elliot

Chewwie is an absolute angel. From the day he arrived on my section, he's been nothing but gentle, loving, affectionate, and just a truly special soul.

Despite being returned because he was said to be nervous, I honestly haven't seen that side of him. With me, he's all about cuddles and attention. He'll happily bounce over to say hello and is always looking for affection. He's also got a cheeky side that keeps me smiling. If you leave your folder, phone, or radio lying around for even a second, don't be surprised if Chewwie decides it's his now!

He's a total water baby and absolutely loves splashing around in the pool with his kennel friend. Watching him let loose, have fun, and just be a dog is one of my favourite things. He also loves heading down to the big yard where he can have a good sniff, stretch his legs, and enjoy himself.

Chewwie really is such an amazing dog, and I feel so lucky to know him. Looking after him has been an absolute privilege.

Chewwie is a sensitive and nervous boy who had experienced very little before coming into rescue and still finds new situations quite overwhelming. In his previous home, he formed a very close bond with his female adopter and absolutely adored her, but he remained worried around men and struggled to relax around the adult sons in the household. He will therefore be looking for a calm and quiet home with patient adopters who have experience of scared ex-breeding dogs and who will allow him all the time he needs to settle and build trust.

Although he had never lived in a home before his adoption, Chewwie adapted beautifully to home life. He was house trained and thoroughly enjoyed all the comforts that a home has to offer, especially his cosy beds! He did find being left alone quite worrying, so his new adopters will need to be around for most of the time initially and slowly build up the amount of time he is left as his confidence grows.

Chewwie will need another kind and confident resident dog in his new home to help reassure him and show him the ropes, but this dog will need to be medium size or larger. He previously shared his home with a cat and happily ignored them, so he could potentially live with a dog savvy cat.

Since being adopted, Chewwie has also learnt how to walk on a lead and now enjoys his walks, although he can still be worried in unfamiliar places and will need ongoing support and encouragement as he continues to gain confidence.

Once Chewwie gets to know you, he is an incredibly loving and affectionate boy who adores a fuss and cuddle. He has a wonderfully cheeky personality and loves to play with other dogs. He is also a bit of a mischievous boy and has been known to pinch things he shouldn't! His adopters will need to keep a close eye on shoes and other tempting items, as we are told he developed quite a fondness for chewing shoes in his previous home. He especially loves hard toys, but softer toys and enrichment items will need to be supervised as he can quickly chew through them.

Chewwie is a truly special boy who has so much love to give. In the right home he will start to come out of his shell and truly flourish. In return, his lucky adopters will gain a devoted, affectionate and wonderfully cheeky friend who will make them smile every day.

When Chewwie first arrived he had a lick granuloma on his front leg, which healed well. When he returned to the rescue he was limping on his front leg and holding it up, we took him to a local vets for an x-ray and no abnormalities or injuries were found, so it's possible that it is a soft tissue injury which should start to feel better soon, but this is something his adopters and their vet will need to monitor.

name: Nana
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I haven't tried walks just yet and will find this a bit overwhelming at the moment.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have some hormonal alopecia, but this should grow back. Read below for more!

10-05-26 Nana has come to us from a breeder and is now searching for her forever home. She is a sweet girl who is understandably feeling very scared and overwhelmed to find herself here. It’s clear she hasn’t had much handling or socialisation before, so human hands are quite worrying for her at the moment and she is very nervous of being handled. We will continue gently helping her learn that human hands can be kind.

Nana will be looking for a calm and quiet adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would really benefit from adopters who have experience with nervous ex-breeding dogs and who understand the patience, time and reassurance she will need as she learns to trust.

She absolutely loves other dogs and relies on them for comfort and reassurance, so Nana will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing and help her feel safe and secure.

As Nana has never lived in a home before, she will need patient adopters who will be kind and understanding as she learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She will also need to learn all about walking on a lead and harness, first practicing in the garden as she builds confidence.

With time, kindness and plenty of TLC, Nana will begin to leave the past behind her and let her true personality shine through.

Nana has some hormonal alopecia, but this should start to grow back once she has been spayed. She has a small inguinal hernia that has been repaired and has had several teeth removed at the time of her spay.

Nana has been given the Gift of Life by Susan Stacey

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. name: Dutch
description:
I would like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need to be the only dog in my new home. No cats please!

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy an active lifestyle!

Please be patient with me, I haven’t lived in a home before.

I am already vaccinated and neutered and ready for my new home!

Dutch is an incredible boy who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a fresh start. He is one of the most loving and affectionate dogs you could ever hope to meet, with a heart as big as his personality. Dutch simply wants to be close to his people, whether that's following you around, leaning into you for fuss, or curling up beside you for cuddles. Being with the people he loves is all he really wants.

Dutch would love to find a home with adopters who are around for most of the day and can include him in everyday life. He thrives on human companionship and will make a wonderfully devoted shadow to his new family. He will need adopters who have experience with similar breeds and understand typical breed traits, including prey drive and the need for appropriate mental and physical enrichment.

Dutch would be best suited to life as an only dog. Although he can socialise well with other dogs of a similar size, he can become overexcited and mouthy, which may lead to frustration. Because of this, he is being trained to wear a muzzle during social interactions to help him continue building positive experiences. He cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Dutch has never lived in a home before, he will need support with house training and learning the general routines of family life. He is such a clever boy and always tries his very best to do the right thing, so we are confident he will enjoy learning with patient, positive guidance.

Dutch already walks well on a lead, although he can occasionally be reactive when passing other kennels. This is understandable in a busy rescue environment and may be something his adopters will need to continue working on. He is an energetic, happy-go-lucky boy who is more than ready to start exploring the world with his new family. An active home where he can enjoy plenty of walks, enrichment, training and quality time with his people would suit him perfectly.

Dutch could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate that he will need a gentle introduction to his new surroundings and his own space to settle in.

Dutch is bright, affectionate and full of love. He has so much to offer and is simply looking for someone to love as much as he loves them. For the right family, he will be an incredibly loyal, fun-loving and devoted companion.

Dutch has a passport and travel document.

Dutch has been given the Gift of Life by Louise Southward

Dutch, or as I like to call him, Dutchy, is honestly one of the most loving and affectionate boys you could ever hope to meet.

He's the kind of dog who just wants to be close to you. Whether it's following you around, leaning into you for fuss, or curling up for cuddles, being with his people is all he really wants. Watching him have to go back into his kennel after spending time together is heartbreaking because kennel life is just no place for a boy like him.

What makes it even sadder is that he hasn't received a single application. If I'm honest, my biggest worry is that he'll become one of our long-stay dogs, and my heart breaks at the thought of that because he simply doesn't deserve it.

He's such a clever boy and always tries so hard to do the right thing. He recently went on his first long walk with one of our wonderful regular volunteers and was an absolute angel. He took everything in his stride and made everyone so proud.

He's also been having play dates with our lovely girl, Swanny, to help him burn off some of that doggy energy while learning good doggy manners.

At the end of the day, Dutchy is just a baby. He's got the biggest heart, so much love to give, and he deserves a home where he'll be someone's whole world instead of waiting behind kennel doors. I know his family is out there somewhere; I just hope they find him before he spends another day wondering why no one has chosen him.

Dutch is an incredible boy who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a fresh start. He is a lovely, affectionate boy who enjoys a fuss and will happily take treats from us.

He would love to find a loving home with adopters who are around most of the time and would have plenty of time to spend with him. He would need adopters who have experience with similar breeds and experience with his typical breed traits like prey drive etc.

Dutch would be best suited to being homed as an only dog as although he does socialise well with other dogs of his size, he can become over excited and mouthy which can lead to frustration. For this reason he is being trained to wear a muzzle when socialising. He cannot be homed with cats or small furries.

As Dutch has never lived in a home before, he will need to learn all about house training and the other basics of home life. With patience and guidance, we think he will adapt well and enjoy all the comforts a home has to offer.

Dutch can already walk well on a lead, though he can be a little reactive when passing other kennels, this is understandable in a busy rescue environment, but may be something his adopters will need to work with him on. He is an energetic and incredibly lovely bo who ie is happy-go-lucky in nature and is more than ready to start adventuring with his new adopters. He would love an active home where he can enjoy plenty of walks, enrichment, and quality time with his people.

Dutch could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate and understand that he will need a gentle and quiet introduction to the home, along with his own space to settle in.

He is a bright, affectionate, and fun-loving boy who is sure to make a wonderful and loyal companion for a lucky family.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. name: Whimsey
description:
I can live with older, dog savvy teens who are sensible and will allow me to settle.

I would like to be the only dog in the home and no cats please!

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I walk well on a lead, but will need guidance in the outside world.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

Whimsey came to us from a breeder as a very worried little girl who found the world quite overwhelming. When she first arrived, she was unsettled and skittish, needing plenty of time and reassurance to help her feel safe. Since then, she has made wonderful progress and has really started to show what a special girl she is.

Whimsey has formed a lovely bond with her carers and now greets those she knows and trusts with enthusiastic cuddles and kisses. It has been a joy to watch her confidence grow, and she is becoming more comfortable with the people around her every day.

While Whimsey has come on tremendously, she can still be nervous and unsure around new people. She will need a calm, patient, and understanding home that is willing to give her the time and space she needs to build trust and settle at her own pace. Once she feels safe, her affectionate and loving personality truly shines through. She can live with older dog savvy teens aged, 16+ that will allow her time to settle.

Whimsey does walk nicely on a lead, but as she can still be sensitive to new experiences, her adopters will need to continue introducing her to the world gently and at a pace she is comfortable with, helping her build confidence without feeling overwhelmed.

Although Whimsey enjoys socialising with other dogs and loves spending time with her canine friends in the exercise yards, we feel she would be happiest as the only dog in her home. This will allow her to fully enjoy the attention and reassurance of her family while continuing to develop her confidence. She would still enjoy opportunities to meet and spend time with other dogs out and about. She will also need a home without cats.

Whimsey has such a sweet heart and has already shown how much progress she can make when given kindness, patience, and understanding. With the right home behind her, we believe she will make a truly wonderful companion and a much-loved member of the family.

Whimsey arrived with an abscess on the right side of her jaw, which has been treated by our veterinary team. Our vets have also noted that she has a fractured canine tooth with no root or nerve exposure. At this stage, no treatment is required, but it should be monitored by her adopters and their vet. She has also received treatment for a urinary tract infection, which has been managed under veterinary guidance.

Oh, my heart. Whimsey has completely stolen it.

She is honestly one of the most loving, affectionate, and gentle girls I've ever met. The moment I walk into her kennel or sit with her in the yard, she melts into me. All she wants is kisses, cuddles, and to be as close to you as possible. She has so much love to give!

She's doing so well with her dog socialising too and absolutely adores her best friend, Royal.

Whimsey is going to make the most incredible addition to a very lucky family. She deserves so much more than just being someone's dog. She deserves to be a cherished member of the family, included in all of life's little moments, loved unconditionally, and never made to wonder if she's enough again.

I truly can't wait for the day she finds the home she's been waiting for. Whoever gets to call this beautiful girl theirs is going to be incredibly lucky.

Whimsey has come on so well since she first arrived. She’s bonded with me so much and always welcomes me with a big kiss and cuddle.

She can be nervous and unsure around new people, but with time, patience, and understanding, she’s going to make the most wonderful addition to someone’s home.

Although she needs to be the only dog in the home, she can happily socialise with other dogs and loves going down to the big yard with her friends.

Whimsy has come to us from a breeder and is a very worried girl. When she first arrived, she was very unsettled at the moment and could be quite skittish, finding so many of the sights and sounds of the world quite overwhelming. Since arriving she has started to settle in and come out of her shell, but will still need time and TLC as she settles in to her new life. Once she gets to know you, she will quietly approach you and allow a gentle fuss.

Whimsy needs time to learn and to trust, and needs a calm and quiet home home that understands ex-breeding dogs and will allow her the space she needs to learn about trust at her own pace. She will need a calm, patient, and understanding family who are willing to let her take things slowly and gently as she begins to discover the comforts of home life.

She will need a kind and confident resident dog in her home that she can look to and rely on as she learns about home life and settles in. Having a canine companion to guide her will really help build her confidence and show her that the world can be a safe and happy place.

Whimsy does walk well on a lead, but as she can be a bit skittish, her adopters will need to take the outside world at her own pace, allowing her the time she needs to gain confidence in her surroundings without feeling pressured.

Whimsy seems like she has such a sweet heart, and with time, patience, and kindness, she will begin to show her loving and gentle nature. She just needs someone special to help her learn about trust and companionship and to show her that she is safe and loved.

Whimsy has come to us with an abscess on the right side of her jaw, which is being treated by the vets. Our vets have noted that she has one fractured canine, but no root or nerve exposure and so it is advised to leave it at this time, but will need monitoring by her adopters and their vet. She is currently being treated for a UTI and will be rechecked once the course of medication has finished.

name: Kate
description:
I can live with older dog-savvy children, aged 16+ and allow me time to settle in.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have some hormonal alopecia, but this should grow back with time.

17-06-26 Kate has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. She is a shy but very sweet little girl who is feeling rather overwhelmed to find herself here at the moment. Despite her worries, Kate will greet you with a gentle wag of her little tail and is easy to handle, showing what a lovely nature she has.

Kate is looking for a calm and quiet home with patient adopters who will allow her to settle in at her own pace and give her the time she needs to blossom. She has never lived in a home before, so everything will be very new to her and she will need kind and understanding owners who can help her learn all about home life and house training. She will also need to learn all about walking on a lead and harness.

As Kate gains confidence from the company of other dogs, she will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to be her friend and show her the way. With the support of her canine companion and loving adopters, we are sure she will flourish. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens aged 16+ that will allow her time to settle in at her own pace.

She is such a sweet girl and we think with time, love and TLC she will truly blossom into the most wonderful friend.

She has arrived with some hormonal alopecia, which should start to grow back once she has been spayed.

Kate has been given the Gift of Life by Jane Clifford

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears.
