name: Gary
description:
I need an adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home with 4ft boundaries.

I haven't tried walks just yet and will find this a bit overwhelming at the moment.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I am missing my left eye, and I have been castrated & have had 1 tooth removed.

08-05-26 Gary is a very sweet and sensitive boy who has come to us from a breeder. He currently finds human interaction quite overwhelming and can be touch-sensitive, so he needs time and patience to build trust. He is very much on the worried side and can react by snapping if he becomes frightened when being picked up, so it’s important that his adopters take a gentle, hands-off approach and allow him to come to them in his own time.

Although he is still nervous, Gary is beginning to take treats from people he knows well and has spent time with, which is a lovely sign of progress and shows his potential to build trusting relationships.

Gary takes great comfort from his kennel friends and has always lived alongside other dogs, so he will need at least one kind and confident resident dog in his new home to help guide him, reassure him, and show him the ropes as he settles in.

As Gary has never lived in a home before, his adopters will need to be understanding while he learns about house training and walking on a lead. He is not yet used to lead walking, so a secure garden will be essential, giving him a safe space to explore and build confidence at his own pace.

Due to his sensitive nature, Gary is looking for a calm adult-only home with ex-breeding experience, where he can settle in at his own pace without pressure. He will need plenty of time, love, and reassurance, but with patience and consistency, he will continue to make progress and grow in confidence.

Gary has come to us missing his left eye, but this doesn’t slow him down at all, if anything, it adds to his unique and endearing charm.

Gary is a little superstar who is trying his best in a world he finds quite overwhelming. With the right support, he will continue to blossom and form a deep, loyal bond with his future family.

At the time of castration, he has had 1 tooth removed.

Gary has been given the Gift of Life by Anne Breen

Garry is just the funniest little dude, and works hard ever day to become braver with us humans. Thanks to the kindness and perseverance of staff at the rescue Garry slowly grows more confident with us.

Garry has been deeply affected by his rough start in life and arrived terrified of everyone, hating being touched or just being near us. But day by day Garry gets braver and will now allow a little scratch under his chin or take a treat (he loves his food). It’s hard not to fall for Garry there is just something about him and within a patient loving home I believe the true Garry will shine through.

When you seem him playing with his friends in the yard he’s just adorable and happy. Garrys ideal home would be experienced with nervous rescues, have a resident dog and no children, or visiting children.

Gary is a very sweet boy once he gets to know you, although this can take a few meetings. He is very shy and timid in his kennel but will put on a brave front when you're outside it.

Gary is currently undergoing muzzle training as he can be tricky when handled. He is doing really well and is now able to tolerate gentle touches under his chin during his training sessions. Thankfully, Gary is a big foodie, which makes muzzle training much more engaging and rewarding for him.

I’ve been at the rescue for a little while now, and I’ve started to make some two-legged friends. I love the food they give me, especially the squeezy paste in a tube, and I’ll go right up to them now for that tasty treat. Sometimes, when they’re not looking, I’ll creep up behind them and check to see if there’s any in their pockets (but they don’t know I do this, so don’t tell them!).

I prefer it when the two-legged ones move slowly and quietly. It’s very unsettling for me if they rush about, and I’ll find a corner to curl up in and make myself feel safe when they do. On the whole though, they’re okay, and I love it when they let me go out into the big yards. I may be quite tiny, but that doesn’t stop me roaming around for all the good sniffs, and I don’t mind if they sit and chat with me while I go about my business.

Yesterday, one of the two-legged ones sat in the kennel with me. I curled up in my bed and watched them, and I even let them give me a little scratch. I think I would quite like some of these two-legged friends of my own, as long as they’re quiet and gentle (none of the small, bouncy ones!). A cosy bed of my own does sound rather nice, and of course I’ll need a four-legged friend as well.

Perhaps I’ll find some lovely two-legged friends soon… let me know if you hear of any.

Gary is a very sweet and sensitive boy who has come to us from a breeder. He currently finds human interaction quite overwhelming and can be touch-sensitive, so he needs time and patience to build trust. He is very much on the worried side and can react by snapping if he becomes frightened when being picked up, so it’s important that his adopters take a gentle, hands-off approach and allow him to come to them in his own time.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Crumble
description:
I need an adult-only, experienced home where I can feel safe and loved.

I want to be the only dog in the house. No cats please!

I’m still shy, so I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I love my walks so one or two a day would be great!

I’ve never lived in a home, so I need to learn housetraining and lead walking.

I have Grade 2 luxating patella, which may improve with exercise.

Crumble is a worried lady who came into our care from a breeder, searching for a fresh start. When she first arrived, she was very worried and unsure of the world around her, but with time and patience she has begun to settle beautifully. Crumble is now much less fearful of us humans and has really started to come out of her shell, choosing to engage more with staff members and showing curiosity about her surroundings. She seems to be gaining confidence each day, which is wonderful to see.

While she can still be sensitive and appreciates having her space respected, it’s clear that Crumble simply needed time to feel safe. With gentle reassurance and plenty of TLC, her true personality is beginning to shine through. She is a gentle soul who responds best to calm, kind interactions and slow introductions.

She would be happiest and thrive best as the only dog in the home, where se can enjoy all the attention, settle more easily into a routine, and build a strong bond with her new family.

As she has never lived in a home environment before, she will need patient support with house training and learning everyday routines. She will need a home without cats as she is overly interested.

We are looking for an experienced, adult-only home where Crumble can continue to build her confidence at her own pace. A quiet household with understanding adopters who are happy to let her take things slowly will allow her to truly flourish. With the right people, time, and lots of love, Crumble will make a wonderfully rewarding companion.

Crumble has a previous bite wound inside her ear, which is currently being treated and our vets have noted that she has a Grade 2 luxating patella. This doesn't cause her any issues and may improve with gentle exercise as she builds muscle.

There’s not a bad word you could say about this girly pop. She is goofy, affectionate, playful, friendly and gaining confidence by the day! She has been trying her best to be kennel clean, no encouragement needed.

She is a staff favourite and that girls little teefys are part of her personality! Nibbling at you to show you just how much she loves to play with you, entertains her pal Criss cross and her kennel mums in the yard.

She belongs in a home where her people can show her the best doggy life imaginable while also understanding that Crumble has also not known much of the world and requires a bit of patience and care when handling her.

Taking every opportunity to play and zoom around with her best friend, Criss Cross.Her confidence is growing and enjoying her walks and greeting new people at her own free will as long as she is guaranteed some fuss or treats.

She enjoys sprawling out on the floor with her chew toy just enjoying the vibes.When she finds her home she will be very eager in sharing her affectionate nature with her family

Crumble is becoming a very happy chow now and has recently discovered that there's no limit on zoomies. She does get along with her chow friend, Criss Cross, but isn't keen on other dogs, so is looking for a home as an only. She is wary of people initially, but once you've gained her trust she becomes your absolute best mate. She loves her walks and gets very excited when she sees you bringing her harness towards her. She's a big foodie too and loves treats.

Crumble is coming on so beautifully and it truly makes my heart melt. Today we had some cuddle and play time in her kennel, and it was such a special moment. For a girl who once wasn’t very happy with touch, she’s now rolling over for belly rubs, snuggling in her bed, and even playing “paw paw” with me.

She’s also learning about wearing a harness and is improving every day. If you have a Crumble shaped hole in your life, she really is something special.

Crumble is a worried lady who came into our care from a breeder, searching for a fresh start. When she first arrived, she was very worried and unsure of the world around her, but with time and patience she has begun to settle beautifully. Crumble is now much less fearful of us humans and has really started to come out of her shell, choosing to engage more with staff members and showing curiosity about her surroundings.

Crumble will need a confident and friendly resident dog who can help guide her and show her the ropes. As she has never lived in a home environment before, she will need patient support with house training, lead walking, and learning everyday routines. But will need to take things slow to start with some garden sniffs adventures on the lead may be too scary to start.

Crumble has a previous bite wound inside her ear, which is currently being treated.

Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Clippa
description:
I’d like an active home home filled with love and kindness!

I can be an only dog or live with others. No cats or small furries please!

I would love a secure garden with 6ft+ fences that I can run and play in!

I walk on a lead but need some training as I'm strong and can pull.

I have some separation anxiety which my adopters will need to continue to work on.

I have been spayed! Please read below for more information.

Clippa is a wonderful girl who came to us from a breeder in search of a fresh start. From the moment she arrived, she showed us what a loving and affectionate little girl she is. Whilst in our care at the rescue, Clippa quickly became a favourite with staff and volunteers thanks to her happy nature and love of human attention. She adored going out on walks with volunteers, loved exploring the Welsh countryside and was always happiest when she was close to people getting cuddles and fuss. She also got on brilliantly with her kennel friends and enjoyed the company of other dogs.

She has now moved to a foster home where she has continued to blossom and has settled beautifully into home life. On arrival, she immediately made it her mission to explore every single inch of the garden, showing just how adventurous and inquisitive she is! Because of this, she will need a very secure garden with minimum 6ft fencing as she is an active little spaniel who loves to investigate everything around her.

Clippa is an incredibly affectionate and people orientated dog who absolutely adores human company. Visitors are instantly greeted by Clippa demanding cuddles, belly rubs and attention, and she will happily snuggle into you whenever given the opportunity. Although she enjoys playing with polite dogs and gets on wonderfully with the resident dogs in foster, people will always be her favourite.

She has taken to home life brilliantly and despite having never lived in a home before, she has been clean around the house, with no accidents indoors. She settles beautifully overnight and loves curling up somewhere warm and cosy with her foster family. In fact, she gets so comfortable at bedtime that convincing her to go outside for her final toilet trip can sometimes be a challenge!

Clippa absolutely LOVES her walks and thrives on getting out and exploring the world around her. She would love an active home that understands the busy, intelligent and inquisitive nature of spaniels and can provide her with plenty of mental and physical stimulation. She already walks well on lead most of the time, although she can sometimes be strong and excitable when out and about. At the moment, her recall is non-existent in secure fields as she is far too busy exploring to pay attention, so her adopters will need to be committed to continuing her training with this.

She could happily be homed as an only dog as long as her adopters were around most of the time and had plenty of time to spend with her, but she could also be homed with another kind and playful resident dog. She could live with gentle dog savvy teenagers but cannot live with cats or small furries.

Since arriving in foster, Clippa has shown signs of separation anxiety and struggles being away from her people even for short periods. Her fosterers are already working hard on building her confidence and independence, and her future adopters will need to continue this, gradually building up the time she is left.

Clippa arrived in our care with bilateral conjunctivitis which responded very well to treatment and continues to be monitored. She also had one tooth removed at the time of her spay.

Clippa is such a happy, loving and affectionate little girl who has embraced her new life with enthusiasm. Whether she is out exploring, making new friends, or snuggled up for cuddles, she throws herself wholeheartedly into everything she does. We think she will make an absolutely wonderful companion for a very lucky family.

Clippa has been given the Gift of Life by Carol Unsworth

Clippa has now moved into foster and is settling in brilliantly! On arrival, she made it her mission to explore every inch of the garden, so she will definitely need a very secure garden with a minimum of 6ft fencing as she is an adventurous and inquisitive girl.

She has settled beautifully into home life and gets on really well with the resident dogs, although her true preference is definitely humans! Every visitor is instantly greeted by Clippa demanding cuddles, belly rubs and attention. She is such a happy, affectionate little girl who absolutely adores people.

Clippa LOVES her walks and exploring the world around her. She would thrive in an active home that understands the busy, inquisitive nature of spaniels and can keep both her mind and body stimulated. She enjoys playing with polite dogs too, but people will always be her favourite.

At the moment, Clippa has zero recall in secure fields and is far too busy exploring to pay any attention to you! She will need lots of training and practice with this before potentially being allowed off lead and her adopters must be prepared to put the work in.

She has met older children calmly and could be homed with kind and gentle, dog savvy teenagers. She has taken everything in her stride. Around the home she is very clean, has never had a single accident indoors, and settles beautifully overnight sleeping upstairs in the bedroom. In fact, she gets so cosy at bedtime that getting her outside for her final toilet trip can be a challenge and her Foster Dad often has to carry her out because she doesn’t want to leave her warm snuggle spot!

Meal times can sometimes be entertaining as Clippa tends to take a bite, wander off to investigate something else, then come back for another bite as there is always something more exciting to check out! Her absolute favourite things in life are walkies, cheese, and snuggling up with Foster Daddy.

Since arriving in foster, Clippa has decided she loves people so much that she finds being away from them difficult, even for 30 seconds. Her fosterers are already working on her separation anxiety with her and gradually increasing the time she is left, but any future adopters will need to continue working with her and making this a priority with patience, consistency and understanding.

Clippa is an absolute angel girl. She is lovely and affectionate and will snuggle right in for cuddles. Clippa also loves going on long walks and meeting new people.

This week Clippa has been enjoying the sunshine at the rescue, and has been out on some super walks with our lovely volunteers and having plenty of sniff time in our play yards.

Clippa would love a home where she will be able to join in on all the family adventures and have plenty of cuddles.

Clippa is an absolutely lovely Springer Spaniel who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a very affectionate girl who loves human company and will happily snuggle right into you for a cuddle.

Clippa gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and enjoys their company, so would be happy to share her home with another kind resident dog. She is also confident enough that we would consider homing her as an only dog in the right circumstances, as long as her adopters were around most of the time. She couldn't be homed with cats or small furries. We would consider homing her with dog savvy teenagers.

As Clippa has never lived in a home before, she will need support with house training and learning the basics of home life. With her friendly and affectionate nature, she is likely to enjoy this learning process with her new family.

Clippa can already walk on a lead and enjoys getting out and about, though she can be a little strong at times and can pull, so she will benefit from ongoing guidance and gentle training to help her with her lead manners. She would love to find an active home where she can go on lots of walks and adventures!

Clippa arrived with bilateral conjunctivitis which is already looking much better following veterinary care and will continue to be monitored. She has had 1 tooth removed at the time of her spay.

Clippa is a loving, affectionate girl who clearly enjoys being close to people and other dogs and we think that she will make a wonderful companion!

name: Max
description:
I need an active, breed experienced home that loves playtime!

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I walk on the lead but I can pull when excited.

I am house trained but I need to learn some manners.

I will need to be neutered when I am of age! It's important!

Max is a superstar Lurcher who has been returned to us from his adoptive family. They made the heartbreaking decision to bring him back as he was becoming dominant over the other dog in the home.

He is a very excitable, happy boy - essentially an overgrown puppy in a big body. Max is full of fun and enthusiasm and will need an active home that can meet his exercise and stimulation needs. He already knows some basic commands such as “sit”, “paw”, “lay down”, and “wait”, and he is eager to interact with his people. He is very food oriented, which makes training enjoyable and rewarding, and he already shows lovely food manners, waiting patiently when asked.

Max absolutely loves other dogs and gets incredibly excited when he sees a potential new friend. However, he is still young, full of energy, and not always aware of his size and strength. This can sometimes lead to him becoming overexcited, a little mouthy, and unintentionally overwhelming other dogs. He has been working hard on his social skills and has been socialising while wearing a muzzle for safe and positive interactions. This is purely as a precaution, and his future adopters will need to continue this training and take a responsible approach when introducing him to other dogs.

As he was too much for a more timid dog in his previous home, we feel that Max would be best suited to being homed as an only dog, provided he has plenty of appropriate socialisation with dogs of a similar size and play style. He cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Max has previously lived in a home, he is familiar with household sights and sounds and is house trained. However, he is still working on his lead manners and can pull when he gets excited. Ongoing training, structure, and consistency will be important, along with plenty of mental stimulation to keep his busy mind engaged. He would thrive in a home that can offer activities such as training games or even dog sports.

Max could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers aged 16 and over, who understand that he can be a bit of a bull in a china shop. Any young people in the home will need to be confident and not worried by an enthusiastic dog who may jump up while he continues to work on his manners.

Max does struggle with separation anxiety and can become destructive if left alone for long periods, so he will need a home where someone is around most of the time and who is committed to helping him gradually build confidence with being left.

Max's new home will need a large, secure garden with 6ft boundaries, where he can let off some steam.

Max will make an amazing addition to a home with experience of similar breeds. He truly is a lovely boy, bouncy, affectionate, and full of life, with so much potential. He is a big-hearted lad who gives his whole heart, and with the right guidance, he will grow into an incredible companion.

Myself and Max have formed a very close bond over the last few months we’ve been working together. We take daily walks, go to the big play yard and have plenty of cuddles and fusses.I’m not sure what it is about Max that keeps getting him overlooked, it could be his size or that he is like a big playful puppy or something else, whatever it is, anyone would be lucky to have this handsome chap in their lives, he will always put a smile on your face and he has such a good sense of humour, watching him having zoomies in his kennel yard while he’s jumping off the obstacles in there then stoping then going back round again is nothing short of entertaining, or when he’s fishing treats out of the paddling pool while simultaneously blowing bubbles with his nose.Max truly deserves the world, he is such a sweet and caring boy with a big personality and so much love to give, as well as his big size there is also a big heart and all he wants is to be able to fully give it to someone and finally find his forever home.

Max is a big, happy baby with bags of energy and so much love to give. He is an excitable, affectionate boy who adores attention and loves having people around him. He is always happy to greet you and be part of whatever is going on.

As Max is still young and growing, he is slowly learning his manners and how to greet people politely without jumping. . He will need adopters who can match his energy, continue his training, and give him the stimulation, guidance, and love he needs to thrive.

Max is a gentle, overlooked soul who is learning new things every day at the rescue, but he is missing the comforts of home. He cannot wait to find a family who understands him and can help him blossom into the wonderful companion he is meant to be.With the right home, patience, and plenty of love, Max will make a loyal and joyful best friend.

Since coming back into our care, Max has been working hard on learning how to socialise with other dogs. He absolutely adores them and gets incredibly excited when he sees a potential new friend! Because he’s still young, full of beans, and a bit unaware of his own size, he can sometimes be a little too enthusiastic. This can lead to him becoming overexcited, a bit mouthy, and unintentionally overwhelming other dogs.

To help him learn safe and positive interactions, Max has been socialising while wearing a muzzle. This is purely for his safety and that of other dogs, and his future adopters will need to continue this training and take a responsible approach when introducing him to new canine companions. Aside from that, Max is everything you’d want in a young pup he's bouncy, happy, affectionate, and full of life.

He’s very food oriented, which makes training a dream! He already knows commands like “sit” and has learned great food manners where be waits patiently until he’s told he can eat. Max thrives on mental stimulation and ongoing training, and with the right guidance, he has all the potential to grow into an incredible dog. Max does struggle with separation anxiety and can become destructive if left alone for long periods. He really craves human company so he’ll need a home where someone is around most of the time and is committed to helping him build confidence being alone gradually. He’s such a loving, handsome boy who gives his whole heart, and it would be wonderful to see him back in a home environment where he can truly thrive. As a young pup, he’s missed out on some important early experiences, so a patient, committed family will make all the difference in helping him become the best version of himself.

He is a very excitable, happy boy - essentially an overgrown puppy in a big body. Max is full of fun and enthusiasm and will need an active home that can meet his exercise and stimulation needs. He already knows some basic commands such as “sit”, “paw”, “lay down”, and “wait”, and he is eager to interact with his people.

Max loves other dogs but is often unaware of his size and strength, having grown into such a big lad very quickly. As he is still just a puppy, he is still learning about manners and can be mouthy and plays in a very typical Lurcher way. In his previous home, there was an incident when he was around 3 months old, where he was a little too rough and resulted in a small dog needing veterinary treatment. He has started muzzle training for off-lead play as a precaution, and he will need a home that understands Lurcher play styles and is confident managing this appropriately. We have been socialising him with dogs of all sizes on walks and he is doing very well.

As Max has previously lived in a home, he is familiar with household sights and sounds and is house trained. However, he is still working on his lead manners and can pull when he gets excited. Ongoing training and consistency will be important.

Max will make an amazing addition to a home with experience of similar breeds. He truly is a lovely boy. He is a big-hearted, affectionate lad who is still convinced he can curl up and fit on your lap.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Cooper
description:
I would like a calm and quiet, adult only home.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would love a good sized garden to explore!

I am a beginner to walking on a lead, but would like an active home.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I am neutered and ready to find my forever home!

26-06-26 Meet Cooper, a lovely boy who has travelled all the way from Romania to begin his new life and search for his forever home. He is a sweet boy who is quite overwhelmed to find himself here, but is starting to settle in and adjust to his new surroundings. Understandably, he is feeling quite scared at the moment, especially when he first meets new people. With those he knows, he is starting to come out of his shell and its lovely to see his confidence growing. Despite his worries, he is easy to handle and in the right home we think he will truly thrive.

Cooper absolutely adores other dogs and takes great comfort from having canine friends around him, so he will need another kind, confident and playful resident dog in his new home to be his companion and show him the ropes. He is also beginning to discover the joy of toys and is enjoying learning how to play, which is so sweet.

As Cooper has never lived in a home before, his adopters will need to be patient, kind and understanding while he settles in and learns all about home life, including new skills like house training. He is very much a beginner when it comes to walking on a lead and harness, but he is already starting to enjoy his walks and would love an active home where he can continue exploring the world at his own pace.

Cooper would love to find adopters who are experienced with dogs and, whilst not essential, experience with Romanian rescue dogs would be a bonus. He would be happiest in a calm, quiet, adult only home where he can settle in at his own pace and continue building his confidence. With patience, understanding and plenty of love, Cooper will start to discover just how wonderful his life can now be. In return, this gentle boy will reward his family with the privilege of watching him blossom into the happy and loved companion he was always meant to be.

He has tested negative prior to arrival for heartworm, lyme disease, ehrlichia, anaplasma, giardia, leishmania and brucellosis.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

Many Tears Animal Rescue, Registered Charity No. 1192227Privacy PolicyCookie PolicyTerms & Conditions

name: Wanda
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed & have had 13 teeth removed!

18-06-26 Wanda has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. She is feeling a little worried and nervous at the moment and is understandably finding all the changes in her life rather overwhelming. However, if you spend some time with her she does begin to seek out a gentle fuss, although she is still unsure about being handled. With plenty of love, patience and kindness, we think this sweet little girl will soon begin to come out of her shell.

Wanda is looking for a calm and quiet home with patient adopters who will allow her to settle in at her own pace and help her build her confidence. She gains comfort from the company of other dogs and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to be her friend and take her under their wing.

Having never lived in a home before, everything will be new to Wanda and her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she learns all about home life. She will need help with new skills such as house training and will also need to learn how to walk on a lead and harness, with plenty of encouragement and positive experiences to help her gain confidence. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens aged 16+ who will allow her time to settle in at her own pace.

Wanda is a lovely little girl who simply needs a family willing to give her the time and reassurance she deserves. In return, we are sure she will reward them with a lifetime of love and companionship as she blossoms into the happy little dog she was always meant to be. She has had 13 teeth removed at the time of her spay.

Wanda has been given the Gift of Life by Lauren Hyslop

name: Louise
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home with experienced adopters.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I am not ready to learn about walking on a lead and harness yet.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I will be spayed before finding my forever home.

03-06-26 Louise has come to us from a breeder and is now looking for a very special forever home. She is an extremely worried little girl who has had very little positive human socialisation and currently finds people and handling very frightening. At the moment, Louise feels safest keeping her distance and, if pushed beyond her comfort zone, may make the wrong decision and nip purely out of fear. She is a very scared girl who needs understanding, patience and time.

Louise will need a calm and quiet adult-only home with no visiting children, and adopters who have experience of helping fearful ex-breeding dogs learn to trust. She relies heavily on the comfort and reassurance of other dogs and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home who can take her under their wing.

Although Louise takes great comfort from other dogs, she can show signs of resource guarding around food. For this reason, she will need to be fed separately from other dogs, and care will need to be taken when giving treats or other high-value food items. Her adopters will need experience of managing this behaviour and the confidence to do so appropriately.

Having never lived in a home before, Louise has lots to learn. Her adopters will need to be patient and understanding as she adjusts to home life and learns new skills such as house training. She is not yet ready to begin learning about lead walking or wearing a harness, as handling is still very worrying for her. These things will need to be introduced gradually and at her pace once she feels more comfortable and secure.

Louise is a sensitive soul who will need a dedicated home willing to invest time, patience and understanding into helping her build confidence. We will continue to work with her every day, helping her take small steps forward at a pace she is comfortable with. In the right home, with the support of canine friends and experienced adopters, we hope Louise will slowly learn that people can be kind and that life has so much to offer.

Louise has been given the Gift of Life by Ann Winter

name: Nora
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

Nora has come to us from a breeder and is now looking for a very special forever home. She is a scared girl who is understandably feeling overwhelmed to find herself here and is desperately in need of kindness, patience and TLC as she begins to settle and learn that the world can be a safe place.

When Nora first arrived with us, she was showing some neurological symptoms and ataxia, and her back legs would occasionally slide from underneath her, causing her to stumble. Following investigations with our vets, it was found that her proprioception was delayed in all four limbs. She was trialled on a course of medication alongside crate rest and has made wonderful progress. Her proprioception is now normal, she is able to get around and play happily, and she enjoys life much more comfortably. She does still have a slight amount of ataxia and can occasionally limp still. Our vet feels that she may have suffered an acute injury at some point in the past which has largely resolved itself, though she will likely never present like a normal dog.

Nora will be looking for adopters who will love and cherish her just as she is. Due to her mobility history, she will need a home without slippery flooring, or one where plenty of rugs and runners are in place to help her move around confidently and safely.

Since arriving with us, Nora has slowly begun to show glimpses of the gentle and affectionate dog she has the potential to become. Like many nervous dogs, she can be vocal when she feels unsure, and her deep, defensive howl is very much in keeping with her Beagle heritage. However, with patience and a calm approach, she soon settles and begins to reveal her softer side.

Although Nora is still learning to feel comfortable around people, she is making steady progress. She is beginning to choose to settle near her carers and, once she has had time to relax, becomes much more accepting of gentle interaction. In these quieter moments she has allowed us to stroke her and handle her gently, which is a hugely positive step for a dog who finds the world so overwhelming.

It is clear that Nora's worries stem not from a dislike of people, but from uncertainty. Having had such limited life experiences, she simply does not yet understand who we are or what to expect from us. As her confidence grows, she becomes increasingly willing to be approached and reassured, and each small step forward is a testament to her resilience.

Nora would be best suited to a calm and quiet home with adopters who have experience of nervous ex-breeding dogs and understand her worries. She will need time to settle at her own pace and will thrive with patient adopters who are happy to let her progress in her own time. We believe that what Nora needs most now is the stability, security and reassurance that only a loving home can provide.

She takes great comfort from the company of other dogs and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to show her the way and help her build confidence. While Nora has successfully shared her kennel with smaller dogs, we feel she would be happiest living alongside a dog of a similar size or larger.

Nora has never experienced life in a home before so her new family will need to be understanding as she learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She does not yet walk on a lead and harness, and while she remains with us we will be working on introducing her to a harness and helping her gain the confidence she needs to begin exploring the outside world safely. Initially, she will need to practise wearing her harness and exploring safely within the home and garden before she is ready to venture further afield.

Although Nora is still very worried and unsure of the world around her, beneath her fears is a sweet and deserving little girl who simply needs someone to believe in her. Watching a scared dog slowly learn to trust is one of the most rewarding experiences, and with love, patience and understanding, Nora will blossom. The day she greets you with a wagging tail and a sparkle in her eyes will make every moment of that journey worthwhile.

Nora has been given the Gift of Life by Judie Fisher

Nora is so gentle and if you ignore the deep defensive howl (she is a beagle after all!) eventually she quietens down and she shows her soft side.Nora is still trying to get used to enjoying human company. She is not there yet but is slowly beginning to show signs of trusting people and does settle near you with a little patience.

It appears to me that it's not that Nora dislikes people, it's more a matter of how she is unsure of who we are and how we do things.

Once she  settles and it does take a little time, although still afraid, is much more willing to be approached by people.

In this calmer state, I was able to smooth her coat all over and gently stroke her without her worrying too much which is a positive step for her.

Nora will need this gentle approach for some time but the reward will be endless love and loyalty which is priceless.

I believe all Nora needs right now is the stability and security that a loving home can offer.Nora does much prefer the company of dogs  at the moment so it is a must that there is a resident dog already in the home for her to be with.

Nora has kennelled with small dogs but we do feel she would be better placed with a dog of a similar size or larger.

Our next step whilst Nora is with us will be to train her to walk on a harness so she can start to discover and enjoy the outside world.

When Nora first arrived with us, she was showing some neurological symptoms and ataxia, and her back legs would occasionally slide from underneath her, causing her to stumble. Following investigations with our vets, it was found that her proprioception was delayed in all four limbs. She was trialed on a course of medication alongside crate rest and has made wonderful progress. Her proprioception is now normal, she is able to get around and play happily, and she enjoys life much more comfortably. She does still have a slight amount of ataxia and can occasionally limp still. Our vet feels that she may have suffered an acute injury at some point in the past which has largely resolved itself, though she will likely never present like a normal dog.

She would be best suited to a calm and quiet home with adopters who have experience of nervous ex-breeding dogs and understand her worries. Nora will need time to settle at her own pace and will thrive with patient adopters who are happy to let her progress in her own time. She takes great comfort from the company of other dogs and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to show her the way and help her build confidence.

Nora has never experienced life in a home before so her new family will need to be understanding as she learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She doesn't yet walk on a lead and harness and initially she will need to practise wearing her harness and exploring safely within the home and garden before she is ready to venture further afield.

name: Grape
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I'd like a secure garden to potter around in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

Please be patient with me, I need to learn house training and lead walking.

Grape has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a scared girl who is quite overwhelmed to find herself here at the moment and clearly isn't used to much human interaction, so finds this quite worrying at the moment. Despite her fears she will let you gently stroke her and is trying so hard to be brave.

She will be looking for a calm and quiet home where she can settle in at her own pace and would benefit from adopters with experience of ex-breeding dogs. She gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

As Grape has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. Everything will be new to her, so a calm and consistent routine will be important.

Grape has arrived a little overweight, but with a good diet and gentle exercise, she should soon start to shed the excess. She has some hormonal alopecia, but this should start to grow back now she has been spayed. She has had 2 teeth extracted as well as a hernia repair and our vets have noted she has a small cyst, which was removed at the time of her spay. She is also being treated for a ear infection.

Grape is a very worried little girl at the moment, but with time, patience, and the support of another dog, she has the potential to slowly gain confidence and begin to show more of her true character.

Grape has been given the Gift of Life Wendy Stewart

Grape is still a nervous girlie and is taking longer to trust humans, given we know nothing of her past this is understandable.

I am trying to show her that all I want to do is help her and show her love, while also respecting her space and going slowly with her, which is what her adopters may have to do depending on where’s she’s at.

She will allow a gentle fuss when she’s in her bed but will not willingly come up and ask for one yet. I think maybe in a calm home she will start to feel more comfortable, kennel life isn’t for everyone, and learn to trust humans.

She is a beautiful girl and I hope she starts to trust soon so we can see that amazing and sweet personality that I’m sure is hiding in her somewhere just bursting to come out.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Gala
description:
I need a quiet adult-only home with no visiting kids to keep me stress-free.

I need at least one resident dog to help me feel safe, preferably a female my size.

I’m frightened and will need a secure garden to learn about being outside.

I enjoy secure enclosed areas where I can run around, but the lead still worries me.

I’m learning to walk on the lead, it's scary for me but I am toilet trained!

I’m fully vaccinated and spayed, ready for my forever home!

Gala is an extremely sweet but incredibly sensitive girl who originally came to us from a breeder. Sadly, she has already been adopted twice and returned both times, as the homes simply weren’t the right fit for her. Because of this, Gala is now searching for a very special forever home with people who truly understand her needs and are committed to giving her the time, patience, and stability she has never had.

When she first arrived, she was an extremely scared, flighty, and shut-down girl who wanted nothing to do with us humans. Since then, Gala has made some lovely, if small, steps forward. She will now allow a gentle stroke while she is on the sofa, usually for around a minute before she chooses to move away. This is huge progress for her and shows just how brave she is becoming.

On one occasion, she even chose to jump up onto the sofa and sit beside someone. Although she ran off as soon as there was a slight movement, it was still a big milestone and a really positive sign of her growing confidence.

That said, Gala is still a very worried girl who carries a lot of fear, and she has a long way to go. She is still frightened of interaction and will run away when approached. However, once she has settled into one of her beds, she will stay still to be gently picked up or have her lead put on.

She remains very nervous of the harness and lead. While she settles a little once it is on, the process of putting it on is still very scary for her. This will need to be built up slowly and gently to avoid overwhelming her or setting her back. She does know how to walk on a lead, but finds it quite daunting, and the outside world is still very overwhelming. She will need time and patience, starting in a secure garden to help build her confidence before any bigger adventures.

Gala takes great comfort from other dogs and truly thrives in their company. She loves to snuggle up with them and will be happiest in a home with other female dogs — the more, the better if possible. She has already shown that she forms particularly strong bonds, often choosing a favourite companion and looking to them for guidance. A kind, confident, and playful female dog of a similar size would be ideal for her, as she finds reassurance and happiness with other dogs while humans are still a source of uncertainty.

She also has a lovely playful side beginning to emerge, and she enjoys carrying around and playing with cuddly toys, which seem to bring her comfort and contentment.

Gala cannot live with cats and will need a calm, child-free home, including no visiting children, where she can feel safe and secure.

Gala has never truly known security, so what she needs most now is patience, kindness, and someone who will never give up on her. There won’t be any overnight miracles, and she may never become a confident, outgoing dog, but with the right person by her side, we know she can continue to grow in confidence, heal from her past, and finally understand what it means to feel safe and loved.

Gala has visited the vets regarding a small mass of inguinal fat. This is not causing her any harm and is currently being left alone, but it may need monitoring in the future.

Gala has made a few small improvements over the past month. She will now allow us to give her a short stroke while she’s on the sofa, usually for about a minute before she moves away. This is massive progress for her and shows how brave she’s becoming.

On one occasion, she even jumped up onto the sofa and sat beside me. As soon as I moved slightly, she ran off, but it was still a big step forward and she did so well.

She is still very scared of the harness. Once it’s on, she settles a little, but the process of putting the lead on frightens her. It’s going to be a very slow process, as we don’t want to push her too far and risk setting her back.

She also loves carrying around and playing with a cuddly toy, which seems to make her feel more content and happy.

Gala is still happiest when surrounded by other female dogs — the more, the better if possible. She has chosen a favourite and looks to her for guidance. A confident, playful female companion would be ideal for her, as she finds comfort in other dogs while humans are still a source of fear.

Gala has been back in foster for a few weeks and she seems to be happy and loves being with her dog friends. She settles very quickly and will happily sleep on a comfy chair. But she is still a very worried girl, as although looking settled, If one of us gets up, she'll be up and running to hide out of our  way. We have found routine is the best thing for her, she is more confident with this.

She very quickly learns what to do and does get animated when certain times arrive, such as breakfast and dinner - as long as she doesn't need to be too near a human. She will definitely need a very stable home without changes and without expectation. At the moment she will interact with us from a distance ok but doesn't get close. If you approach slowly she will stay put and you can stroke her or pick her up but she's not comfortable with it and would much rather escape from you...

On the positive side, she has started to show signs of enjoying a tummy rub, she will lift her leg for more to show she's liking it - almost like her doggy instincts are getting the better of her, this is the only time she happily excepts touching that she seems to enjoy.

Putting her harness on she shakes with fear when she sees her harness and it needs putting on very very slowly. She then is happy to walk in her own surroundings on a lead, but is so scared of being outside in the big world on it and will try to escape.

However she does love being taken to a secure field where she can run around playing with her furry friends. She loves all our other dogs and interacts very well with them all. Plus she is house trained.

Her new home must be one who gives her patience and time and expects nothing in return, this could be for a long time. She needs a very stable quiet home without too many changes happening and a good routine She wouldn't react well to things like doggy day care or holidays. She needs some very gentle persuasion to gain trust on humans but without expectations so she is not pushed too far and regressed.

Gala has been back at the rescue with me for just about two months now. I also looked after her when she was at the rescue originally, around a year and a half ago. Seeing her arrive back was hard for me, but it was nice to see how much progress she’s made during her time away. When I knew her all that time ago, she was an extremely scared, flighty, and shut-down girl who wanted nothing to do with us humans. It’s very clear she has gained confidence since then and has (sort of) learnt that she can trust us humans, so it is lovely to see the difference in her. Having said that, she does still carry a lot of worry and fear, and she still has a long way to go. Steps have been made in the right direction, so hopefully she can continue learning and growing, and eventually try to put her past behind her.Gala’s biggest love is food, so this is the main thing I’ve been using to get her comfortable around me. She has learnt how to ‘sit’ and also how to give the best puppy dog eyes that I can never say no to! She waits patiently for her turn for a treat, and takes them nice and gently too.Gala does still have quite a big fear of human hands and won’t approach me for a fuss, but she is good when it comes to things such as being groomed. When I need to pick her up, she’ll usually jump into one of her beds and then stay still for me to scoop her. She knows how to walk on a harness, but the outside world is another one of her fears, so this is something that needs to be taken slowly with her. I doubt very much she’ll be up for big adventures any time soon and will need a lot of practice in the garden and very quiet areas for a good while, so this is something her future adopters will need to take into consideration.After a life full of uncertainty and confusion, her next home HAS to be the right one for her. She really needs adopters who will love her regardless of her worries and work with her through them. Most importantly of all, she can’t be given up on again. She deserves to know where she’s going to wake up in the morning and not have to worry about being passed from pillar to post. She deserves love, trust, and commitment … and not a smidge less.

Sadly, Gala has returned to the kennels after her recent adoption. Although her new family cared deeply for her, it turned out not to be the right match. This decision was made with everyone's best interests at heart, and she’s now back with us while we look for her perfect forever home. Gala is a wonderful dog who just needs the right environment to truly thrive, and we’re hopeful that her special someone is out there waiting for her.

Gala is still in foster and gradually making progress. She is still extremely hand-shy but has started to come up and let her nose touch your hand before running off! She will then come back wanting to do it again and wagging her tail (huge progress!).Gala is looking for a forever home to offer her patience and lots of time to help her get over her fears. She is fully house trained and very happy to follow our other dogs around copying what they do. She is an escape artist on a harness as she is still nervous walking on a lead, but we have found taking her to a secure dog run field is amazing for her and she absolutely loves her freedom running around. Gala is ideally looking for some playmates in her new home as she does love to run and play with other dogs.

Gala is doing brilliantly in her foster home. She loves the company of other dogs and loves to play with them but is still very hand-shy with us humans and very wary.Gala would love a home with one or more furry friends who love playing, but what she also needs is a home which would help her understand to not be frightened anymore and people who will understand it's going to take time and patience until you get any affection or love back from her.Gala has big trust issues and needs a selfless home to get her over this hurdle she is facing.

Gala has sadly returned to the kennels through no fault of her own after a six month adoption. Her adopter made the difficult decision to bring her back due to a change in their personal circumstances, and she was much loved during her time with them. She’s now back with us and receiving lots of care and attention while we help her find another loving home. Gala is a lovely dog who truly deserves her second chance.

Gala came to us from a breeder a few months ago. We are told she is a Poodle cross Cavalier cross Bichon and she is the cutest little girl but is very overwhelmed and nervous so does need an experienced home who have had scared ex-breeding dogs before.Sadly Gala was returned after a short adoption due to a change in her adopters circumstances but was lucky enough to go to a foster home. She will need a calm and quiet home and is very scared of children so will need an adult only home with no visiting children. She will need a secure garden as she is so worried she can be a flight risk.Gala will need a home with at least one other kind and confident resident dog to be her friend and help her to settle in. She is house trained, but may need some reminders in her new home and walking on a lead is a little scary for her, so she will need more practice with this. She LOVES food and that's the way to her heart, she will take a treat from our hand, but other than that she is very hand shy and will run away if we go to stroke or approach her.This little lady just needs to find her perfect home for she can learn that people can offer her kindness and love. She is a sweet girl who with time, love and kindness will soon start to blossom.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Toucan
description:
I would like an active, adult-only home with plenty of adventures and love!

I need to be the only dog in the home, and cannot live with cats!

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden with 6ft+ fences to play in.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I am in a foster home and am house trained.

I am neutered and ready to find my forever home!

Toucan is a very handsome and active boy who originally came to us from a breeder. He was adopted for a short period of time, but unfortunately found himself back in rescue. Toucan was adopted into a family home and though he loved the whole family, there was one isolated incident where he was snoozing in his bed and was accidentally startled by the older child in the home. Waking suddenly and feeling frightened, Toucan reacted in panic and gave a nip before immediately moving away. We believe this response was purely due to being startled while asleep, but for this reason, Toucan will be looking for an adult-only home where there are no visiting children. His adopters will need to provide him with a quiet, safe space where he can rest undisturbed and feel completely relaxed.

Toucan spent some time in a foster home, but sadly it became clear that his preference was to be an only dog and he didn't enjoy sharing his home with another dog. He does get along well with other dogs when out and about and is fine passing them on walks etc, he would just prefer not to share his space with them. We think he is going to love being the only dog in the home where he can get all the love and attention for himself.

Toucan is a bright and friendly boy who enjoys meeting new people and, given time to build a bond, forms lovely relationships with his humans. He is affectionate and engaging, and with the right guidance, will make a wonderful addition to a committed home.

He is an active lad who loves his walks and exploring the world around him. At present, he can be strong on the lead as he is eager to get out and sniff everything he sees, so his adopters will need to continue working on his lead training and help him learn to walk more calmly. He is currently being trained to walk on a halti here. His absolute favourite thing in the world is playing with a ball and he just lives to play fetch! He would choose his ball over a treat any day and has proven himself to be the best ball catcher the rescue has seen!

Toucan is a clever boy who is picking things up quickly. He will benefit from a consistent routine and ongoing training to help him feel secure and understand expectations. Providing him with structure, alongside quiet resting spaces where he won’t be disturbed, will be key in helping him manage any anxieties. Although he has lived in a home, he may need some reminders about home life and skills like house training as he settles back into home life.

Toucan is a fun, intelligent, and affectionate boy who, with the right experienced adopters, routine, and understanding, will grow into a fantastic and rewarding companion.

Our vets have noted that his back legs are slightly deflected outward, but this isn’t causing him any issues.

Toucan has been given the Gift of Life by Alison Hale

Toucan spent some time in a foster home, but sadly it became clear that his preference was to be an only dog and he didn't enjoy sharing his home with another dog, so he has come back to kennels to continue the search for his perfect home.

Toucan is an absolute angel, but he does find kennel life quite stressful and can become vocal when unsettled. As a result of kennel stress, he can lose weight, so I’m keeping a close eye on him and making sure he’s getting all the nutrition and care he needs.

Toucan is a very active boy! His favourite thing in the world? A ball! He lives to play fetch. I can offer him a treat, but if there’s a ball around he’s much more interested in that. He is the best ball catcher at the rescue, the focus he has on that tennis ball is honestly something I admire. Besides his love for all things round, he is a genuinely sweet boy, who enjoys a fuss and of course a walk. He can be strong on a lead so for this reason I’m currently halti training him and although he’s not a huge fan yet, he is doing really well with it and is currently tolerating it.

Toucan is very trainable and tries his best to figure out what you’re asking him. He’s very smart and it breaks my heart that this beautiful boy is spending his days in kennels instead of out there in the world, playing fetch to his hearts content.

If you’re looking for a smart, active and adventurous best friend then please consider Toucan, he really is an amazing dog and he’s very handsome too.

Toucan is a lovely boy who has settled in as though he’s always been part of the family. He’s very confident and affectionate, and has happily met family and friends - he loves everyone, including my 17-year-old nephew.

He has a big love of food (including everyone else’s!), so he is fed in his crate using a slow feeder bowl to help him pace himself. He needs to gain some weight, so we’ve been adding extra protein to his biscuits and he’s already starting to look healthier.

Toucan is house clean and travels brilliantly in the car, happily jumping in and out. He gets on amazingly well with the four dogs in the home as well as friends’ dogs.

He is comfortable with the crate and, although the door is now left open at night, he still chooses to sleep in there. Toucan can pull on the lead, which we’re currently working on. He isn’t bothered by loud noises and absolutely loves playing ball - he’s fantastic at fetch.

Toucan is a very handsome and active boy who originally came to us from a breeder. He was adopted for a short period of time, but has unfortunately found himself back in rescue. Toucan was adopted into a family home and though he loved the whole family, there was one isolated incident where he was snoozing in his bed and was accidentally startled by the older child in the home. Waking suddenly and feeling frightened, Toucan reacted in panic and gave a nip before immediately moving away. We believe this response was purely due to being startled while asleep, but for this reason, Toucan will be looking for an adult only home, where there are no visiting children. His adopters will need to provide him with a quiet, safe space where he can rest undisturbed and feel completely relaxed. Please don't let this put you off, as he is truly a wonderful boy and his previous adopters have said how wonderful he is and will be in the right home.

Toucan is is always on the go, eager to explore, and looking for his next task. He has a vibrant, happy-go-lucky personality and would thrive in an active home that can provide plenty of attention, as well as physical and mental stimulation. He is super smart and loves to use his brain and already knows "sit", "paw" and "down." He is a lovely boy who is very affectionate and really enjoys having cuddles and fuss once he gets to know you.

Toucan is confident enough that he could be homed as an only dog as long as his adopters had plenty of time to spend with him. He could also be homed with another kind resident dog and has shared his kennel well with dogs at the rescue. He can sometimes be reactive to other dogs when passing their kennels but is fine when out and about on lead. He still needs some training on the lead as he likes to be in charge, pulling us along on his travels.

Toucan has learned to walk on a harness, but he can pull and so will need adopters who can manage this and who are willing to work on this with him. He is obsessed with tennis balls and will play all day if you had the time, so he really hopes his new family will enjoy throwing balls for him!

Toucan can be a little greedy, so at the moment he is fed separately from any other dogs. He is a little and so he is now on a raw food diet to help him gain weight.

He had never lived in a home before his adoption and so home life is very new to him and he will need kindness and patience as he settles in. He was clean in his previous home but may have some accidents as he settles into a new home. With kindness, consistency, and plenty of opportunities to burn off his energy, he will make a wonderful, loyal companion for the right family.

Our vets have noted that his back legs are slightly deflected outward, but this isn't causing him any issues.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

name: Emrys
description:
I am looking for an adult only home with experienced adopters.

I could live with other dogs or be the only dog. I cannot be homed with cats.

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

Emrys is currently in foster care and looking for an experienced, understanding forever home, a second chance for a brilliant but misunderstood Collie. Emrys came to us from a farm. He was young, vulnerable, and completely overwhelmed when he first arrived, as so many working Collies are when their world turns upside down.

With no history, we could only judge him by what we saw. And what we saw was a bright, sensitive dog who found the chaos of a rescue kennel absolutely terrifying. Thankfully, a kind and knowledgeable Collie experienced fosterer stepped forward. In their calm and structured home, Emrys began to thrive. He stayed with them for six weeks or so enjoying long walks, plenty of enrichment, and the steady, respectful approach that working Collies truly need. There were no confrontations, no worries, just progress and peace. But Emrys wasn’t there to stay. He was only passing through on his way to what we hoped would be his forever home.

Sadly, that next chapter didn’t go to plan. After several weeks in a new home, Emrys reacted negatively to the owner’s visiting adult son-in-law. He displayed reactive behaviour and later redirected onto a behaviourist when restrained on a lead. Following this incident, the behaviourist recommended that euthanasia be considered.

But his original Fosterer couldn’t give up on him. His original foster welcomed him back, and to this day, he has never shown a single sign of aggression or resource guarding with them or with regular visitors and he gets on well amazingly with the resident dogs in the home. The other resident dogs keep him occupied and we believe this really benefits him. In their care, he continues to flourish. He’s happy, safe, and very much loved. They manage him carefully, understand his boundaries, and give him the active lifestyle and thoughtful leadership he needs.

Emrys is not a beginner’s dog. He is an intelligent, driven Collie who needs clear structure, exercise, and a handler who respects the way his brain works. Many farm-bred Collies don’t transition easily to domestic life, they need understanding, not punishment. They can become overwhelmed, fearful, or reactive when their needs are ignored, or simply not recognized.

However plenty of challenges. Work needs to be play for him, enjoyable fun where he is learning, he will then love his lessons respecting and looking up to humans for his next task, whether that’s obedience, flyball, canicross, agility, search and recovery - just some thing to engage his brain.

Someone experienced with Collie behaviour.

A secure home with limited foot traffic and not a lot of goings-on. He will need to continue the use of a muzzle in public as a precaution, (though he has never shown any aggression while out walking with his fosters).

We are not trying to hide his history however we are also not writing him off for one reaction, in one moment, under pressure. Dogs deserve better than that. Emrys has proved himself safe and stable in a well-managed foster home. With the right person, we know he can have a good life.

If you’ve worked with Collies before, if you believe in second chances, and if you think you could be the steady, experienced home that Emrys needs please get in touch. He’s a brilliant boy, and he’s waiting.

To give Emrys the best chance of finding the perfect home, we are happy to waive the standard adoption fee and instead welcome a donation of any amount you feel comfortable with.

Emrys has been in foster with us for the past 10 months and has proven to be a confident, affectionate collie. He loves his walks just as much as he enjoys relaxing on the sofa afterwards.

He gets along well with both dogs and people, is clean in the house, and travels happily in the car. During his time with us, Emrys has shown no signs of reactivity and has greeted all visitors kindly.

He is a truly lovely dog and would make a fantastic, loyal companion.

Emrys has been in foster with us for seven months now, and he’s a real character. He gets on well with all dogs and, since being back with us, has shown no reactive or guarding behaviours. In the right home, he will truly thrive and make a wonderful companion.

Emrys can occasionally feel nervous around new people, and when this happens he takes himself off to his safe space, which is in the garden. From there, he’ll assess the situation at his own pace before coming back inside when he’s ready to meet someone new. It’s important that he’s given the time and space to do this calmly.

Once he knows you, Emrys is an absolute joy to be around. He loves his walks and secure field runs, is calm and settled in the evenings, and is incredibly affectionate.

With his wicked personality and loving nature, Emrys will make a fantastic companion for the right person.

Emrys has been back in foster for 11 weeks now and has settled in beautifully with no problems at all. He gets on very well with the three resident dogs as well as visiting dogs, and he is friendly with visitors and strangers outside the home. Emrys is totally house clean, travels well in the car, and is very treat-orientated, which makes him easy to train. After his walks, he loves nothing more than relaxing with his foster family.

Emrys has settled back in beautifully with his foster family and hasn’t shown any issues at all. He loves racing around the garden with the resident dogs and is starting to enjoy playing with toys and bringing a ball back.

He’s now completely house trained, walks very nicely on the lead, and is happy to travel in the car.

Emrys is such a lovable boy and is coming along wonderfully. He’ll make a fantastic companion for the right home.

Emrys is a striking, high-energy Collie who came to us from a farm where his owner could no longer give him the time he needed. After arriving he quickly found himself in foster, where he received plenty of exercise, mental stimulation, and affection, everything a clever Collie thrives on.He was adopted for a short time, but sadly returned to us after displaying behaviours in the home that hadn’t been seen while in foster. His adopters told us that Emrys exhibited some guarding tendencies and could react negatively when he felt backed into a corner or overwhelmed. Because of this, he’s looking for a calm, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand the needs of a sensitive, intelligent dog and can support him with firm but kind guidance.Emrys will need a secure garden with space to run, and a home that can provide him with plenty of structured physical and mental activity to keep him happy and settled. He’s a bright boy who’s eager to please and loves learning. With patience, consistency, and the right support, Emrys has every potential to thrive.Emrys is now back with his original foster where he has shown many lovely qualities, he is clean in the house, travels well in the car, walks nicely on the lead, and is affectionate with those he trusts. He’s also food-motivated and already learning basic commands. We believe that Emrys thrives on activity and as he has not displayed these behaviours in foster it may be because he is provided with so much stimulation and exercises. His adopters will need to keep this in mind, and ideally be very active and willing to provide him with different games and activities to keep his mind busy.Emrys could live with another confident resident dog or be the only dog in the right home. He cannot live with cats, and due to his sensitivity, he will need a home without children or visiting children.This boy has so much potential and is just waiting for the right people to help him blossom.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?
